title: Microsoft Fabric for Data Engineers: Senior-Level Track
description: Design a multi-tenant Fabric platform for a multi-country organization — workspace namespace strategy, OneLake data product design, cross-team ownership contracts, and the organizational patterns that prevent a 500-pipeline platform from collapsing under its own weight.

chapters.title: Platform Architecture at Scale
chapters.description: Design a multi-tenant Fabric platform for a multi-country organization — workspace namespace strategy, OneLake data product design, cross-team ownership contracts, and the organizational patterns that prevent a 500-pipeline platform from collapsing under its own weight.

blocks.content: You know how to build and operate a production Fabric platform. The mid-level track proved that: you tuned Spark performance, operated Delta tables with MERGE and maintenance, built resilient pipelines with ForEach and error handling, designed a Warehouse with materialized views and RLS, deployed streaming with Eventstreams and KQL, established testing and CI/CD, managed capacity across two capacities, and governed a 3-team environment with Purview and certification. NordGrid's platform served 15 people across 3 teams in one country. GridUnion Continental serves 80 engineers across 12 teams in 4 countries. The architectural problems at GridUnion's scale are qualitatively different from NordGrid's — not just bigger numbers, but different categories of problems that NordGrid's architecture cannot address without redesign.
What Breaks at 12-Team Scale

rows: Workspace discovery
Everyone knows all 13 workspaces by name
Nobody can find anything in 80+ workspaces without a catalog
The flat hybrid hierarchy does not encode enough information for 80 workspaces to be navigable
Ownership
One platform team owns everything; disputes are resolved in a hallway conversation
12 teams produce and consume data products; ownership disputes cross country and reporting-line boundaries
Informal ownership works for 3 teams. At 12 teams, ownership must be declared, registered, and enforceable
Schema changes
The engineer who changes the Silver schema walks to the analyst’s desk and explains the change
The German engineer who changes the Silver schema has no idea that the French regulatory pipeline depends on it
Schema evolution must be governed by contracts and automated impact analysis, not by hallway conversations
Capacity
Two capacities with informal staggering
Four capacities with formal allocation per country and per workload type — exceeding the budget requires cross-country governance
Informal capacity management scales to 2 capacities. At 4 capacities with 12 teams, allocation requires policy
Security
Purview labels and RLS with one compliance team reviewing everything
Four countries with four regulatory regimes — each country’s data has different classification requirements and different audit obligations
Single-country governance does not handle multi-jurisdictional compliance without country-level isolation and delegation

blocks.content: The GridUnion Continental Scenario

GridUnion Continental was formed when NordGrid Energy (Germany/Netherlands) acquired ÉnergieFrance (France) and merged with LuxGrid (Luxembourg). The acquisition brought together three independent data platforms with different architectures, different naming conventions, different security models, and different regulatory obligations. The post-acquisition mandate: consolidate onto a single Fabric tenant within 18 months while maintaining country-level data isolation for regulatory compliance. The platform team has 6 engineers to serve 12 teams across 4 countries. They cannot build and operate every pipeline — they must design a platform that enables the 74 other engineers to build and operate their own pipelines safely, efficiently, and consistently. This module teaches the architectural decisions that make that possible.
The Senior-Level Shift

The shift from mid-level to senior is not about knowing more tools or writing more complex code. It is about changing what you design. The mid-level engineer designs pipelines: the sequence of activities that transforms data from Bronze to Gold. The senior engineer designs the platform that makes those pipelines easy to build: the workspace namespace that makes items discoverable, the ownership contracts that make responsibilities clear, the data products that make consumption self-service, the governance automation that makes compliance effortless, and the capacity model that makes resource allocation fair. Every lesson in this module addresses a platform design decision — not a pipeline design decision. The pipeline is the product of the platform. The platform is what you design.

blocks.title: Platform engineering is organizational engineering
blocks.content: The hardest problems at GridUnion's scale are not technical. The Spark optimizer, the Delta transaction log, and the Eventstream engine work the same at 80 workspaces as at 13. The hard problems are organizational: which team owns the Silver meter table when it contains data from 4 countries? Who approves a schema change that affects pipelines owned by teams in different countries? How do you allocate capacity costs when a German pipeline's output is consumed by a French analytics team? Every architectural decision in this module is simultaneously a technical decision and an organizational decision. The architecture that ignores the organization will be circumvented. The architecture that reflects the organization will be adopted.

NordGrid's workspace hierarchy was hybrid: layer-based for shared production (ws_nordgrid_bronze, ws_nordgrid_silver, ws_nordgrid_gold) and team-based for team-specific work (ws_nordgrid_analytics, ws_nordgrid_ml). At 13 workspaces, this was navigable. At GridUnion's scale — 4 countries × 3 layers × 3 environments (dev/staging/prod) = 36 workspaces for the medallion alone, plus team workspaces, plus shared-services workspaces — the flat hybrid hierarchy collapses. An engineer searching for the French Silver Lakehouse finds 6 Silver workspaces (one per country plus staging plus dev). The namespace must encode country, layer, environment, and purpose so that the workspace name alone tells the engineer what it contains, who owns it, and what environment it belongs to.
The Namespace Design

GridUnion's namespace follows a four-segment pattern: ws_{country}_{layer}_{environment}. Production omits the environment suffix for brevity. Examples: ws_de_silver (German Silver, production), ws_fr_silver_staging (French Silver, staging), ws_nl_gold_dev (Dutch Gold, development). Shared workspaces that contain cross-country data use ws_gu_ prefix (GridUnion): ws_gu_platinum (the cross-country curated layer), ws_gu_platform (monitoring and governance tables), ws_gu_streaming (the unified Eventhouse and Eventstreams). Team-specific workspaces use ws_{country}_{team}: ws_de_analytics (German analytics team), ws_fr_ml (French ML team). The naming convention is enforced by Fabric domain policies — a workspace that does not match the pattern cannot be created.

blocks.title: GridUnion’s workspace namespace hierarchy
blocks.content:
GRIDUNION FABRIC TENANT
│
├─ COUNTRY WORKSPACES (per-country medallion)
│  ├─ ws_de_bronze, ws_de_silver, ws_de_gold         (Germany prod)
│  ├─ ws_de_silver_staging, ws_de_silver_dev         (Germany CI/CD)
│  ├─ ws_fr_bronze, ws_fr_silver, ws_fr_gold         (France prod)
│  ├─ ws_nl_bronze, ws_nl_silver, ws_nl_gold         (Netherlands prod)
│  └─ ws_lu_bronze, ws_lu_silver, ws_lu_gold         (Luxembourg prod)
│
├─ SHARED WORKSPACES (cross-country)
│  ├─ ws_gu_platinum         (Cross-country curated Gold)
│  ├─ ws_gu_platform         (Monitoring, governance, schema registry)
│  ├─ ws_gu_streaming        (Unified Eventhouse, 4 Eventstreams)
│  └─ ws_gu_warehouse        (Enterprise Gold Warehouse)
│
├─ TEAM WORKSPACES (per-team work areas)
│  ├─ ws_de_analytics, ws_fr_analytics, ws_nl_analytics, ws_lu_analytics
│  ├─ ws_de_ml, ws_fr_ml
│  └─ ws_gu_compliance       (Cross-country compliance team)
│
└─ SANDBOX (time-bounded experimentation)
   └─ ws_{initials}_sandbox_*  (auto-expire 90 days)

Total: ~80 workspaces across 4 countries, 3 environments, 12 teams
blocks.caption: The namespace encodes country (de/fr/nl/lu/gu), layer (bronze/silver/gold/platinum), and environment (prod implicit, staging/dev explicit) in the workspace name. Shared cross-country workspaces use the gu_ prefix. Team workspaces use country_team. Sandbox workspaces use engineer initials with auto-expiry.

visual-recommendation.description: A hierarchical tree diagram showing the complete workspace namespace with country-coded branches, color-coded by country (DE=blue, FR=red, NL=orange, LU=green, GU=purple), with workspace counts per branch and role annotations showing which teams have access to which workspace groups.

blocks.content: The Three Design Trade-Offs

rows: Country isolation vs shared access
Separate tenant per country (complete isolation, no cross-country analytics)
Single tenant, country-level workspaces (isolation via security, cross-country analytics via Platinum)
Single tenant, workspace isolation
Cross-country analytics is a business requirement; separate tenants make it impossible without data movement
Unified Silver vs per-country Silver
One Silver Lakehouse with all countries’ data (simpler pipeline, harder security)
Per-country Silver Lakehouses (more pipelines, cleaner security)
Per-country Silver
Country-level data isolation is a regulatory requirement; mixing countries in one Lakehouse requires complex RLS that auditors distrust
CI/CD environments per country vs shared
Each country has its own dev/staging/prod (108 workspaces total)
Shared CI/CD environments (German pipeline tested in shared staging)
Per-country CI/CD for Silver/Gold, shared for Platinum
Country teams need to test independently; Platinum is cross-country by nature and needs shared staging

blocks.title: Namespace design is the most consequential early decision
blocks.content: Renaming 80 workspaces after the platform is in production is operationally devastating — every Git connection, every deployment rule, every cross-database query, and every Power BI dataset references workspace names. GridUnion spent 3 weeks on namespace design before creating a single workspace. The three trade-offs above were debated in 4 architecture review sessions with representation from all 4 country teams. The investment was justified: 18 months later, the namespace has not been modified, and new engineers navigate the 80-workspace environment without a tour guide because the names are self-documenting.

OneLake is Fabric's unified storage layer — every Lakehouse, every Warehouse, every KQL database stores its data in OneLake. At NordGrid's scale (3.2TB, 13 workspaces), OneLake was invisible infrastructure that simply worked. At GridUnion's scale (15TB across 80+ workspaces, 4 countries, 2 Azure regions), OneLake becomes an architectural concern with performance characteristics, cost implications, and design decisions that affect every team. The most important OneLake feature at enterprise scale is the shortcut — a pointer from one workspace to data in another workspace that provides access without copying. Shortcuts replace data duplication with shared references, but they introduce latency trade-offs and security complexity that this lesson examines.
Shortcut Architecture

A shortcut is a symlink-like reference: it appears as a folder or table in the target Lakehouse but reads data from the source Lakehouse. When the French analytics team needs to join German Silver data with French Gold data, they do not copy the German Silver table — they create a shortcut in ws_fr_analytics that points to ws_de_silver's meter table. The shortcut appears as a local table, queries against it work identically to queries against local data, and the data is never duplicated. At GridUnion's scale, shortcuts eliminate approximately 8TB of data duplication that would otherwise be required for cross-country access — a significant OneLake storage savings and, more importantly, an elimination of the ETL pipelines that would maintain those copies.
Cross-Region Shortcuts

GridUnion operates in two Azure regions: EU West (France, Luxembourg) and EU North (Germany, Netherlands). When a French workspace creates a shortcut to a German Lakehouse, the read traverses the inter-region network. This adds latency: same-region reads average 15ms per 1MB, cross-region reads average 45ms per 1MB — a 3x penalty. For small dimension table lookups (the 12-row region reference table), the penalty is invisible. For large scans (joining 2TB of German Silver data from a French notebook), the penalty adds minutes. GridUnion's architectural rule: shortcuts to large tables must stay within the same region. Cross-region shortcuts are permitted only for reference tables under 100MB and for the Platinum layer (which consolidates pre-aggregated data specifically designed for cross-region consumption).
Shortcuts and Security

A shortcut inherits the source's OneLake data access roles. If the German Silver Lakehouse's data access role restricts the French analytics team to the meter_readings table (but not the billing_details table), the shortcut in ws_fr_analytics only provides access to meter_readings — even though the French analytics team has full access to their own workspace. This behavior is correct and essential for cross-country data isolation: the German data stays under German security control regardless of where the shortcut points. However, it creates a governance requirement: the source team must explicitly grant access to the consuming team's security group before the shortcut becomes usable. GridUnion's shortcut request process requires the source team's approval and the compliance team's review before any cross-country shortcut is created.

blocks.title: Shortcuts are not copies — the security implications are asymmetric
blocks.content: When you copy data, the copy inherits the destination's security model — the destination team controls access. When you create a shortcut, the shortcut inherits the source's security model — the source team controls access. This asymmetry confuses teams that expect the shortcut to behave like a local table. GridUnion's French analytics team filed a support ticket when their shortcut to German data returned 'access denied' — they had workspace Contributor access to their own workspace but not the German OneLake data access role. The platform team now includes a security explanation in the shortcut request approval email: 'Your shortcut will be governed by the source team's security rules, not your workspace's rules. If your access requirements change, you must request an update from the source team.'

At NordGrid's scale, every team member knew every table. At GridUnion's scale, the platform contains 200+ tables across 80+ workspaces. A German analytics engineer who needs French meter data cannot browse 80 workspaces looking for the right table. They need a catalog that tells them: what data exists, where it lives, who owns it, what schema it has, how fresh it is, and whether it is certified for production use. The data product model addresses this by treating each significant table (or set of tables) as a product with a declared interface — a schema contract, an SLA, a quality guarantee, an owner, and documentation — that consumers can discover and depend on without understanding the pipeline that produces it.
What Makes a Data Product

Not every table is a data product. A staging table that holds intermediate results during a pipeline run is not a product — it has no external consumers and no stability guarantee. A data product is a table (or set of tables) that external consumers depend on for business decisions. GridUnion's criteria for data product status: the table has at least one consumer outside the owning team, it has a declared schema that the owner commits to maintaining, it has a freshness SLA that the owner commits to meeting, it has data quality thresholds that are automatically verified, and it has documentation that a new consumer can read without contacting the owner. Tables that meet all five criteria are published as data products in GridUnion's catalog. Tables that do not are internal implementation details of the owning team's pipeline.
The Data Product Manifest

{
  "product_name": "German Daily Meter Readings (Silver)",
  "product_id": "dp_de_silver_meters",
  "owner_team": "DE Data Engineering",
  "owner_contact": "de-data-eng@gridunion.eu",
  "workspace": "ws_de_silver",
  "lakehouse": "lh_de_meter_readings",
  "table": "silver_meters",
  "schema_version": "3.2",
  "schema_registry_ref": "sr://de_silver_meters/v3.2",
  "freshness_sla": "Refreshed by 03:00 UTC daily",
  "quality_thresholds": {
    "null_rate_meter_id": 0,
    "null_rate_daily_kw": 0,
    "row_count_range": [900000, 1100000],
    "region_completeness": 8
  },
  "retention": "3 years rolling",
  "sensitivity_label": "Confidential",
  "certification": "Certified (last review: 2025-10-15)",
  "documentation_url": "https://gridunion.sharepoint.com/dataproducts/de_silver_meters",
  "consumers": [
    { "team": "DE Analytics", "usage": "Daily consumption dashboards" },
    { "team": "FR Analytics", "usage": "Cross-country comparison (via shortcut)" },
    { "team": "GU Compliance", "usage": "Quarterly regulatory submission" }
  ]
}

The manifest is a JSON file stored in the platform workspace's data_product_registry Delta table. It captures everything a consumer needs to evaluate and adopt the product: what data it contains, where it lives, who owns it, what quality to expect, and how to access the documentation. The consumers array tracks which teams depend on the product — critical for impact analysis when the owner plans a schema change or a freshness SLA adjustment. GridUnion's manifest schema is intentionally simple: the first version had 8 fields. Eighteen months later it has 14. The schema evolved based on what consumers actually needed, not on what the platform team imagined they would need.
Discovery and the OneLake Data Hub

The data product registry is a Delta table that the platform team maintains. But engineers do not query a Delta table to find data products — they browse the OneLake data hub in the Fabric portal. GridUnion populates the data hub's metadata by keeping the registry in sync with Fabric's item descriptions and endorsements. Every data product's Lakehouse item has a description that matches the manifest's documentation summary, a Certified endorsement badge, and tags that include the product_id and owner_team. The hub's search functionality allows an engineer to type 'meter readings France' and find dp_fr_silver_meters without knowing the workspace name, the table name, or the team that owns it. Discovery is the gateway to adoption — a data product that cannot be found will not be used.

blocks.title: The data product is the platform’s unit of trust
blocks.content: Without data products, trust is personal: ‘I trust this table because I know the engineer who built it.’ At 80 engineers, personal trust does not scale — the French analytics engineer does not know the German data engineer. Data products make trust institutional: ‘I trust this table because it is a certified data product with a declared schema, a quality SLA, and a compliance review.’ The manifest, the certification badge, and the quality thresholds replace the hallway conversation with a formal contract that any engineer can evaluate independently. GridUnion's shadow data problem (analytics teams building their own Silver tables) disappeared within 6 months of launching the data product catalog — not because the platform team mandated it, but because the data products were easier to use than building your own.

Every Fabric item has exactly one owner team. This principle sounds obvious until it is tested: who owns the unified Platinum table that combines meter data from all 4 countries? The German team produced the German data. The French team produced the French data. The platform team built the pipeline that merges them. The analytics team designed the schema. Four teams have legitimate claims. Without a clear ownership rule, the table has four partial owners and zero actual accountability — when the freshness SLA is breached, each team assumes another team is responsible. GridUnion's ownership model resolves this with a single rule: the team that writes the table owns the table. The German team owns ws_de_silver's tables. The platform team owns ws_gu_platinum's tables (because the platform team's pipeline writes the Platinum merge). Ownership follows the write path, not the data origin.
The Producer-Consumer Contract

Ownership creates accountability. The producer-consumer contract makes that accountability specific. The producer (the owning team) commits to: maintaining the declared schema without breaking changes unless the contract's breaking-change process is followed, meeting the freshness SLA published in the data product manifest, maintaining data quality above the declared thresholds, and notifying all registered consumers at least 48 hours before any planned disruption. The consumer commits to: querying the table through the published interface (no direct access to underlying files), not writing to the producer's Lakehouse or Warehouse, filing schema change requests through the contract process rather than asking the producer informally, and registering as a consumer in the data product manifest so the producer can assess impact. The contract is not a legal document — it is a team agreement documented in the manifest and enforced by CI/CD gates and monitoring.
The Ownership Registry

# GridUnion's ownership registry — a Delta table in ws_gu_platform
# Every production item has exactly one owner entry

ownership_schema = StructType([
    StructField("item_type", StringType()),       # lakehouse, warehouse, pipeline, notebook
    StructField("item_name", StringType()),        # e.g., lh_de_meter_readings
    StructField("workspace", StringType()),        # e.g., ws_de_silver
    StructField("owner_team", StringType()),       # e.g., DE Data Engineering
    StructField("owner_contact", StringType()),    # e.g., de-data-eng@gridunion.eu
    StructField("backup_contact", StringType()),   # e.g., platform-team@gridunion.eu
    StructField("data_product_id", StringType()),  # Null if not a data product
    StructField("last_review_date", DateType()),   # Quarterly ownership review
    StructField("country", StringType()),          # DE, FR, NL, LU, or GU (shared)
])

# The registry is populated during onboarding and updated during quarterly reviews
# Every item without an ownership entry is flagged in the weekly governance scan

The ownership registry is a Delta table that the weekly governance scan validates. The scan compares the registry entries against the actual Fabric items (queried via the Fabric REST API) and reports three categories of discrepancy: orphaned items (items that exist in Fabric but have no registry entry — typically created ad-hoc and forgotten), stale entries (registry entries whose items no longer exist — typically left behind after a table was decommissioned), and ownership gaps (items whose owner_contact email is no longer valid — typically left behind when an engineer leaves the team). GridUnion's scan runs every Monday and produces a report that the platform team reviews in their weekly governance meeting. Orphaned items are assigned to the workspace owner by default and escalated to the governance committee if the workspace owner disclaims responsibility.

blocks.title: Ownership without enforcement is suggestion
blocks.content: Declaring ownership in a registry is necessary but insufficient. If any engineer can write to any Lakehouse regardless of ownership, the registry is a suggestion that engineers ignore when it is inconvenient. GridUnion enforces ownership through workspace roles: the owning team has Contributor access (can write), consumer teams have Viewer access (can read only). A consumer team that needs to create a derived table from a producer's data does so in their own workspace, not in the producer's. This enforcement eliminates the accidental writes, unauthorized schema changes, and ad-hoc data modifications that plagued the pre-registry environment. The registry declares ownership. The workspace roles enforce it.

NordGrid used three layers: Bronze (raw), Silver (cleansed), Gold (business-aggregated). GridUnion adds a fourth: Platinum. The Platinum layer contains cross-country curated data products — tables that combine data from multiple countries into a unified schema for analytics, regulatory reporting, and executive dashboards. Platinum is not a universal requirement: organizations with one country or one business unit do not need it. GridUnion needs it because their highest-value analyses (consolidated generation capacity, cross-country consumption trends, unified regulatory submissions to the EU energy authority) require joining data from all four countries — and performing that join at query time across four country-level Gold Lakehouses is both slow and security-complex. Platinum materializes the join result.
Layer Contracts

rows: Bronze
Country DE teams (one per country)
Raw ingested data — CSV, JSON, Parquet as received from source systems
Schema matches source; no transformation applied
Silver pipelines only — no direct consumer access
Silver
Country DE teams
Cleansed, deduplicated, schema-enforced data — the system of record
Declared in the schema registry; breaking changes require contract process
Gold pipelines, analytics teams (read-only via shortcut or SQL endpoint)
Gold
Country domain teams (analytics, billing, regulatory)
Business-aggregated data — monthly billing, regional summaries, KPIs
Declared in the data product manifest; refresh SLA guaranteed
Warehouse, semantic models, Power BI reports, downstream pipelines
Platinum
Platform team
Cross-country curated joins — unified schemas for enterprise analytics
Declared in the data product manifest; derived from Gold contracts
Enterprise analytics, executive dashboards, EU regulatory submissions

visual-recommendation.description: A vertical four-layer diagram showing Bronze at the bottom, Silver, Gold, and Platinum at the top, with ownership labels on the left (country DE teams, domain teams, platform team), data flow arrows between layers, and contract boundaries at each transition point annotated with what each transition adds (quality, business context, cross-country breadth).

blocks.content: When Platinum Is Justified

Platinum adds a layer of pipelines, storage, and maintenance. The justification requires three conditions. First, cross-unit data is a frequent query pattern, not an occasional ad-hoc request. GridUnion's executive dashboard queries cross-country data 200+ times per day — this frequency justifies materialization. Second, the cross-unit join is expensive to perform at query time. Joining 4 country-level Gold tables via cross-database queries takes 15 seconds per query; the Platinum table returns the same result in 200 milliseconds. Third, the security model requires a clean boundary. The Platinum table has its own data access roles that grant analytics teams access to the unified data without granting them access to the country-level Gold tables that contain more granular (and more sensitive) detail. Organizations that do not meet all three conditions should use cross-database views (Module 4 pattern from mid-level) rather than adding a fourth layer.
Layer Ownership and the Write-Direction Rule

Data flows in one direction through the layers: Bronze → Silver → Gold → Platinum. Each layer writes only to the next layer — never backward, never sideways. A Silver pipeline reads from Bronze and writes to Silver. A Gold pipeline reads from Silver and writes to Gold. A Platinum pipeline reads from Gold (across countries) and writes to Platinum. This unidirectional flow makes the dependency graph a DAG (directed acyclic graph) rather than a tangle of circular references. When the German Silver table is delayed, the impact propagation follows the DAG: German Gold is delayed, then Platinum is delayed (because it depends on German Gold), then the executive dashboard shows stale data. The impact is traceable and predictable. If Gold could write back to Silver, the impact would loop and the dependency graph would become unanalyzable.

blocks.title: Layers are contracts, not folders
blocks.content: The four layers are not just a storage organization pattern — they are contract boundaries. Each layer transition is a transformation that adds value: Bronze → Silver adds quality (cleansing, deduplication, schema enforcement). Silver → Gold adds business context (aggregation, KPI computation, dimension enrichment). Gold → Platinum adds breadth (cross-country consolidation). Each transition is owned by a specific team, governed by a specific contract, and monitored by specific SLIs. The layers are meaningful because the contracts at each boundary are meaningful. A four-layer architecture without contracts at each boundary is just a folder hierarchy — it adds complexity without adding governance.

NordGrid's pipeline dependency graph was simple: Bronze → Silver → Gold, orchestrated by one master pipeline with parent-child invocations. At GridUnion's scale, the dependency graph has 500+ pipelines owned by 12 teams across 4 countries. The German Silver pipeline produces data that the German Gold pipeline, the French analytics shortcut, the Platinum consolidation pipeline, and the compliance audit pipeline all consume. When the German Silver pipeline is delayed by 30 minutes, four downstream pipelines are affected — owned by four different teams in three different countries. The mid-level pattern of pipeline dependencies within a single master pipeline does not scale to cross-team dependencies where the producer and consumer pipelines are owned by different teams with different schedules.
The Fan-Out Problem

A Silver table with 40 downstream consumers creates a fan-out: one producer's delay cascades to 40 consumers. At GridUnion's scale, the German Silver meter table has 23 downstream dependencies (8 German Gold pipelines, 3 French analytics shortcuts, 4 Dutch ML features, 2 Luxembourg reports, 4 Platinum consolidation queries, 2 compliance audits). If the German Silver pipeline fails and the platform team sends a manual notification, they must identify and contact 23 consumers across 4 countries. Without automated dependency tracking, this notification takes 2 hours. With the dependency graph, it takes 5 minutes — the system sends automated notifications to every registered consumer based on the data product manifest's consumer list.
Dependency-Aware Scheduling

GridUnion's cross-team dependencies use a publish-subscribe pattern rather than direct pipeline invocation. The producer pipeline (German Silver) completes and writes a completion event to a shared event table in ws_gu_platform. Consumer pipelines poll this table at their scheduled time — if the producer has completed, they proceed; if not, they wait with a configurable timeout. This decouples producer and consumer schedules: the German team can change their pipeline's start time without coordinating with 23 consumer teams. The completion event includes the producer's status (success, partial success, failure), the processing date, and the row count — so the consumer pipeline can make an informed decision about whether to proceed (producer succeeded), wait (producer still running), or alert (producer failed).
Circular Dependency Prevention

The dependency graph must be acyclic — no circular references. If the German Gold pipeline depends on the French Silver pipeline, and the French Silver pipeline depends on the German Gold pipeline, both pipelines deadlock. GridUnion prevents circular dependencies through the write-direction rule (lesson 6): data flows Bronze → Silver → Gold → Platinum, never backward. Since dependencies follow data flow, and data flow is unidirectional, circular dependencies within the medallion are impossible. Cross-layer dependencies (a Silver pipeline reading from another country's Silver table) are permitted but tracked in the dependency graph and reviewed quarterly to ensure they do not create indirect cycles. The platform team's dependency analyzer runs weekly and rejects any new dependency that would create a cycle.

blocks.title: Dependencies across team boundaries require contracts, not assumptions
blocks.content: When the German Silver pipeline is 30 minutes late, the German Gold pipeline waits — because the same master pipeline orchestrates both. But the French analytics team's pipeline does not wait — it is a separate pipeline owned by a separate team, triggered by a separate schedule. Without the publish-subscribe pattern, the French pipeline runs against stale German data and produces incorrect cross-country analytics. The contract between producer and consumer must include the dependency mechanism: how the consumer detects the producer's completion, how long the consumer waits, and what the consumer does if the producer fails. At NordGrid's scale, these contracts were implicit. At GridUnion's scale, they must be explicit, documented, and automated.

NordGrid's configuration was a single pipeline_config Delta table with 15 rows — one per configuration key per environment. At GridUnion's scale, configuration spans 4 countries × 3 environments × 12 teams, with country-specific regulatory settings, team-specific pipeline parameters, and environment-specific capacity allocations. The single-table pattern becomes a 500-row table with a 5-column composite key (country, team, environment, pipeline, config_key) that is difficult to navigate, error-prone to update, and impossible to review in a pull request. GridUnion replaces the flat table with a hierarchical configuration repository stored in Git alongside the pipeline code.
The Configuration Hierarchy

config/
├─ global/
│  ├─ defaults.json          # Platform-wide defaults (retry count, SLA thresholds)
│  └─ capacity_allocation.json # CU budgets per capacity, per team
├─ countries/
│  ├─ de/
│  │  ├─ pipelines.json        # German pipeline parameters (batch size, schedule)
│  │  ├─ regulatory.json       # BNetzA-specific settings (retention, audit)
│  │  └─ environments/
│  │     ├─ dev.json            # German dev overrides
│  │     ├─ staging.json        # German staging overrides
│  │     └─ prod.json           # German production overrides
│  ├─ fr/ ...                   # Same structure for France
│  ├─ nl/ ...                   # Same structure for Netherlands
│  └─ lu/ ...                   # Same structure for Luxembourg
└─ shared/
   ├─ platinum.json            # Cross-country Platinum settings
   ├─ streaming.json           # Eventstream configuration
   └─ governance.json          # Schema registry, ownership, certification

The hierarchy follows the workspace namespace: global settings apply everywhere, country settings override global defaults for country-specific behavior, and environment settings override country settings for dev/staging/prod differences. The merge order is deterministic: global → country → environment, with later values overriding earlier ones for the same key. This inheritance model means that changing a global default (like the retry count) automatically applies to all countries and all environments — unless a country or environment has an explicit override. GridUnion's deployment script reads the hierarchy, merges the layers, and produces the final configuration for each workspace.
Configuration as Code

Every configuration file lives in the Git repository alongside the pipeline code. Changes follow the same PR process: the engineer modifies the JSON file, the reviewer verifies the change, the CI pipeline validates the configuration syntax and checks for conflicts (two countries cannot claim the same CU budget if the total exceeds the capacity), and the deployment pipeline applies the change to the target workspaces. This GitOps model provides version history (who changed the German retry count and when), review (the change was approved before it took effect), and rollback (reverting the configuration is a Git revert). GridUnion's configuration repository has 140 files across the hierarchy and processes approximately 10 configuration changes per week — each one reviewed and deployed through the standard CI/CD pipeline.

blocks.title: Start with the global defaults, override sparingly
blocks.content: GridUnion's configuration hierarchy has 140 files, but 80% of the configuration values are inherited from the 2 global files. Only 20% are overrides in the country or environment layers. This ratio is intentional: a configuration that is mostly overrides means the countries have diverged so far that the global defaults are meaningless. GridUnion's platform team reviews the override ratio quarterly. If a country-level override is present in all four countries, it becomes a global default. If a global default is overridden by all four countries, the global default is wrong and needs updating. The hierarchy should simplify configuration, not complicate it.

NordGrid's monitoring dashboard tracked 5 pipelines with a morning check and a weekly trend review. GridUnion has 500+ pipelines across 4 countries — a morning check that examines each pipeline individually takes 4 hours, not 2 minutes. Platform observability at enterprise scale requires aggregation: instead of monitoring individual pipelines, the platform team monitors aggregated SLIs across the platform and drills into individual pipelines only when an aggregate SLI breaches its threshold. The observability system transforms 500 individual pipeline statuses into 5 aggregate health indicators that a single engineer can assess in 2 minutes.
The Aggregate SLI Dashboard

rows: Platform success rate
% of pipeline runs that succeeded across all teams and countries in the last 24 hours
Click to see which pipelines failed, grouped by country and team
Freshness compliance
% of data products that met their freshness SLA in the last 24 hours
Click to see which products are stale, with the owning team and current lag
Capacity headroom
Minimum CU headroom across all 4 capacities (peak utilization vs 70% threshold)
> 10% headroom
Click to see which capacity is constrained and which workloads are driving the peak

visual-recommendation.description: A mock dashboard layout showing the five aggregate SLI tiles as large status cards (green/yellow/red) across the top row, with a drill-down panel below showing the per-country breakdown for the selected SLI. Each card shows the current value, the SLO threshold, and a 7-day sparkline. The drill-down panel shows a bar chart of per-country compliance rates.

blocks.content: The Observability Architecture

GridUnion's observability system aggregates metrics from five sources into a unified dashboard in ws_gu_platform. The pipeline_metrics tables from all country workspaces are consolidated into a single gu_pipeline_metrics table via OneLake shortcuts (the platform workspace creates shortcuts to each country's metrics table). The data product contract test results are written directly to ws_gu_platform by the contract testing pipeline. The capacity metrics are extracted from the Fabric REST API by a daily notebook. The security posture metrics are computed by the weekly governance scan (lesson 5). The aggregation notebook runs at 04:00 UTC (after all overnight pipelines complete) and computes the five aggregate SLIs. The morning check dashboard reads from the aggregated tables, and the on-call engineer reviews 5 numbers instead of 500.
Anomaly Detection

Aggregate SLIs detect threshold breaches (freshness compliance dropped below 99%). Anomaly detection catches trends that have not yet breached a threshold but are heading toward one. GridUnion's anomaly detection notebook runs weekly and computes 4-week rolling averages for pipeline duration, row count, CU consumption, and failure rate — per pipeline. If any metric deviates from its 4-week average by more than 2 standard deviations, the notebook flags it for investigation. This statistical approach detects the slow decline that individual SLIs miss: a pipeline that takes 14 minutes, 14.3, 14.6, 14.9 — each run within the SLA but trending upward. The anomaly detector flags the trend after 2 weeks, giving the owning team time to optimize before the SLA is breached.

blocks.title: Observability is the platform team’s interface to the platform
blocks.content: The platform team does not build pipelines — the country teams do. The platform team does not write SQL queries — the analytics teams do. The platform team’s primary interaction with the platform is through the observability system: the aggregate SLIs, the anomaly detector, the governance scan, and the capacity metrics. If the observability system shows 5 green indicators, the platform is healthy and the team focuses on evolution (new features, migrations, improvements). If any indicator is yellow or red, the team investigates and coordinates the fix with the owning team. The quality of the observability system determines the quality of the platform team's decision-making — and at 500 pipelines across 4 countries, the platform team cannot make good decisions without good aggregated data.

The previous nine lessons covered the platform engineering shift from pipeline builder to platform architect, the multi-tenant workspace namespace with country-coded segments, OneLake shortcuts with cross-region trade-offs and security implications, the data product model with manifest-based discovery and certification, cross-team ownership contracts enforced through workspace roles, the four-layer Lakehouse model with Platinum for cross-country consolidation, cross-team pipeline dependencies with publish-subscribe scheduling and fan-out management, hierarchical configuration management with GitOps and inheritance, and platform observability with aggregate SLIs and anomaly detection. This lesson synthesizes everything into GridUnion Continental's complete platform architecture.
The Architecture Summary

blocks.content: What Comes Next

Module 2 goes inside the Catalyst optimizer and AQE at the implementation level — the rule batches, the cost model, the statistics propagation, and the runtime adaptation mechanics that determine how Fabric's managed Spark actually executes your queries. The platform architecture from this module determines what the pipelines do. The optimizer internals from Module 2 determine how efficiently Spark executes them. At GridUnion's scale, a 10% optimizer inefficiency on the German Silver pipeline costs 80 CU-minutes per day — 29,200 CU-minutes per year. Understanding the optimizer is not academic curiosity at this scale. It is financial responsibility.

blocks.title: The platform is the product that enables all other products
blocks.content: GridUnion's 12 teams produce 45 data products that serve billing, regulatory, analytics, ML, and executive decision-making across 4 countries. Every one of those products depends on the platform: the workspace namespace for discovery, the data product registry for trust, the ownership contracts for accountability, the four-layer model for data flow, the dependency graph for coordination, the configuration hierarchy for environment management, and the observability system for operational awareness. The platform team's 6 engineers do not build 45 data products — they build the infrastructure that enables 74 other engineers to build and operate those products safely, efficiently, and at scale. That is the platform engineering shift, and it is what the remaining nine modules of this senior track build upon.

chapters.title: Advanced Catalyst and AQE Internals
chapters.description: Understand the Catalyst optimizer and Adaptive Query Execution at the implementation level within Fabric's managed Spark — rule ordering, cost models, statistics propagation, runtime adaptation, and the diagnostic techniques for tracing unexpected plan decisions to their root cause.

blocks.content: The mid-level track taught you to read execution plans — the physical plan that Catalyst produces after optimizing your query. You verified pushdown, checked pruning, and identified shuffle boundaries. But you treated Catalyst as a black box: code goes in, plan comes out. At senior level, you open the box. Understanding how Catalyst transforms your code into a plan — which rules fire, in what order, and why — is essential for diagnosing the plan decisions that mid-level analysis cannot explain. GridUnion's 12-table regulatory query produced a plan that shuffled 8TB instead of 800GB. The explain() output showed the shuffle but not why Catalyst chose it. Tracing the decision required understanding the rule that failed to fire — and that requires understanding how rules are organized, sequenced, and applied.
The Rule Batch Architecture

Catalyst organizes optimization rules into batches. Each batch contains related rules (all filter-pushdown rules in one batch, all join-reordering rules in another). Batches execute sequentially in a fixed order: Analysis batches run first (resolve column references, validate types), then Optimization batches (push down predicates, eliminate unnecessary operations, reorder joins), then Physical Planning (choose join strategies, insert exchange operators). Within each batch, rules iterate to a fixed point — the batch applies its rules repeatedly until the plan stops changing. This iteration is why a single rule can trigger cascading optimizations: pushing a filter below a join changes the join's estimated input size, which may trigger a different join strategy on the next iteration.
The Four Phases Revisited

The mid-level track named the four phases: Parsed, Analyzed, Optimized, Physical. At senior level, you need to know what happens inside each. The Parsed plan is the direct translation of your DataFrame API calls into an unresolved logical tree — column references are strings, not resolved to specific tables. The Analyzed plan resolves those references: 'region' becomes 'silver_meters.region#45'. The Optimized plan is where the interesting work happens: the Optimization batches apply 60+ rules that push down filters, prune columns, fold constants, eliminate dead code, and reorder joins. The Physical plan converts logical operators to physical ones: LogicalFilter becomes a physical Filter, LogicalJoin becomes BroadcastHashJoin or SortMergeJoin. Each conversion involves a strategy selection based on the optimizer's cost model.
Tracing Rule Application

# Enable detailed optimizer logging to trace which rules fire
# WARNING: produces massive output — use only for debugging, never in production
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.optimizer.excludedRules", "")

# View the optimized plan with rule annotations
df = spark.read.table("silver_meters") \
    .filter(col("year") == 2025) \
    .filter(col("region") == "NORTH-1") \
    .groupBy("customer_type") \
    .agg(sum("daily_kw").alias("total_kw"))

# explain("cost") shows the plan with cost estimates at each operator
# This reveals what Catalyst estimated vs what the plan does
df.explain("cost")

# To trace specific rule behavior:
# 1. Run the query and capture the physical plan
# 2. Disable the suspected rule using spark.sql.optimizer.excludedRules
# 3. Re-run and compare — if the plan changes, that rule was responsible
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.optimizer.excludedRules",
    "org.apache.spark.sql.catalyst.optimizer.PushDownPredicates")
df.explain()  # Compare: pushdown should be missing now

The excludedRules configuration is the diagnostic scalpel for rule-level investigation. By disabling one rule at a time and comparing the plan output, you can identify exactly which rule is responsible for a specific plan decision — or which rule's failure to fire caused an unexpected plan. GridUnion's 8TB shuffle was traced to a missing PushDownPredicates application: the country filter was not pushed below the join because the filter referenced a column alias that the rule did not resolve through the alias chain. Disabling the rule produced an identical plan — confirming that the rule was already not firing, and the fix was to rewrite the filter to reference the original column name rather than the alias.

blocks.title: The optimizer is deterministic — unexpected plans have traceable causes
blocks.content: Catalyst is not random. Given the same input plan, the same statistics, and the same configuration, it produces the same output plan every time. When a plan is surprising, the cause is always one of three things: the statistics were wrong (the optimizer made a rational decision based on incorrect estimates), a rule did not fire (a condition prevented the expected optimization), or the cost model chose differently than you expected (the optimizer calculated that the 'worse-looking' plan was actually cheaper). All three are diagnosable. This module teaches the diagnostic techniques for each.

Every decision Catalyst makes — join strategy, join order, broadcast threshold, partition count — depends on row count and size estimates. These estimates come from table-level statistics (collected by ANALYZE TABLE or Fabric's automatic statistics) and are propagated through the plan tree: each operator estimates its output size based on its input size and the operation's selectivity. A filter with selectivity 0.1 on a 10-million-row input estimates 1 million output rows. A join between a 1-million-row table and a 100-row table estimates 1 million output rows (assuming 1:1 matching). When these estimates are accurate, Catalyst makes good decisions. When they compound errors through multiple operations, the final estimate can be orders of magnitude wrong — and the plan reflects that error.
How Estimates Degrade

Statistics error compounds multiplicatively through the plan tree. If a filter's selectivity is estimated at 0.1 but the actual selectivity is 0.01 (10x error), the filter's output estimate is 10x too high. If this output feeds a join, the join's output estimate inherits the 10x error and may add its own error (the join selectivity estimation assumes uniform key distribution, which is rarely true). After 3 operations with 3x error each, the final estimate is 27x off. After 5 operations (a typical complex query), the estimate can be 100x–1000x wrong. GridUnion's 12-table regulatory query accumulated estimation errors across 5 joins — the final stage estimated 800 million rows when the actual count was 2 million. This 400x overestimate caused Catalyst to allocate 200 shuffle partitions where 12 would have been optimal.
Diagnosing Estimation Errors

# Compare estimated vs actual row counts using the Spark UI SQL tab
# The SQL tab shows both 'number of output rows' (actual) for completed queries

# Programmatic approach: capture estimates from explain("cost")
# and compare to actuals from the executed query
df_filtered = spark.read.table("silver_meters") \
    .filter((col("year") == 2025) & (col("month") == 3) & (col("region") == "NORTH-1"))

# Get the estimate from the plan
df_filtered.explain("cost")
# Look for: Statistics(sizeInBytes=X, rowCount=Y)
# Example output: Statistics(sizeInBytes=2.4GB, rowCount=1200000)

# Get the actual count
actual = df_filtered.count()
print(f"Actual rows: {actual}")
# Output: Actual rows: 95000
# The estimate was 1,200,000 — 12.6x overestimate
# Cause: statistics for the region column assumed uniform distribution
# but NORTH-1 contains only 8% of rows, not the estimated 100%

The explain('cost') output includes Statistics annotations at each operator node showing the estimated sizeInBytes and rowCount. Comparing these estimates to the actual counts from the Spark UI's SQL tab (or from a count() call) reveals where the estimation error originates. GridUnion's diagnostic workflow for estimation errors: start at the leaf nodes (the table scans, where estimates come directly from table statistics and are usually accurate), then trace upward through each operator, comparing estimated output to actual output at each level. The first operator where the estimate diverges significantly from actuals is the error source — typically a filter with poor selectivity estimation or a join with non-uniform key distribution.
Column-Level Statistics

Table-level statistics provide row count and total size. Column-level statistics provide distinct value count, null count, min/max values, and optionally a histogram of value distribution. Column-level statistics dramatically improve filter selectivity estimation: without them, Catalyst assumes a filter WHERE region = 'NORTH-1' has selectivity 1/number_of_partitions (a rough heuristic). With column-level statistics showing 8 distinct regions with NORTH-1 containing 8% of rows, the selectivity estimate is 0.08 — much closer to reality. GridUnion maintains column-level statistics on all partition columns and all frequently-filtered columns (region, country, customer_type) using scheduled ANALYZE TABLE commands after each nightly refresh. The statistics collection adds 2 minutes to the pipeline but prevents the 400x estimation errors that caused the 8TB shuffle incident.

rows: Table-level (row count, size)
Total rows and bytes — the baseline for all estimates
Prevents the most extreme errors (empty table vs 1B rows)
< 1 second
Automatically maintained by Delta transaction log
Column-level (distinct count, min/max)
Cardinality and range for filter selectivity
Prevents errors on filtered columns (region, country, customer_type)
30–60 seconds per column
Collected nightly for all filter and join columns
Histogram (value distribution)
Frequency distribution for non-uniform columns
Prevents errors when data is heavily skewed (65% of meters in Germany)
2–5 minutes per column
Collected weekly for columns with known skew (country, region)

visual-recommendation.description: A flow diagram showing how statistics propagate through a 5-operator plan tree, with estimated row counts annotated at each operator and error magnitude growing at each stage. The diagram highlights the compounding effect: 3x error at each operator produces 243x error at the final stage.

blocks.title: Statistics are only as fresh as the last collection
blocks.content: Column-level statistics are snapshots — they reflect the data distribution at the time ANALYZE TABLE ran, not the current distribution. If GridUnion's German Silver table receives 200,000 new rows per day and statistics are collected weekly, the statistics are up to 1.4 million rows stale by the end of the week. For slowly-changing distributions (the proportion of rows per region is stable), weekly collection is sufficient. For rapidly-changing distributions (a new customer type that grows from 0% to 15% in a month), stale statistics produce increasingly wrong estimates until the next collection. GridUnion's monitoring notebook compares the ANALYZE TABLE timestamp to the table's last-modified timestamp and alerts when the gap exceeds the configured threshold.

The Cost-Based Optimizer (CBO) uses statistics to assign a numeric cost to each candidate physical plan and selects the plan with the lowest estimated cost. The cost model is the formula that converts statistics (row count, data size, cardinality) into a cost number. Understanding the cost model matters at senior level because the model's assumptions determine when it makes good decisions and when it makes bad ones — and knowing which scenarios break the model tells you when to intervene with manual hints rather than trusting the optimizer. GridUnion's platform team intervenes with broadcast hints on approximately 5% of production queries — each intervention was justified by a cost model analysis that showed why the automatic decision was suboptimal.
The Cost Formula for Joins

Catalyst assigns cost to join strategies using a simplified model. Broadcast hash join cost is proportional to the size of the broadcast table (the data sent to all executors) plus the probe cost (scanning the large table). Sort-merge join cost is proportional to the shuffle volume of both sides (both tables are redistributed) plus the sort cost (both sides are sorted within partitions) plus the merge cost (sorted sides are merged). Shuffle hash join cost is proportional to the shuffle volume of the smaller side plus the hash table build cost. The model uses data size (bytes) as the primary cost metric, with row count as a secondary factor for operations where per-row overhead matters (like hash table probing). The model does not account for network bandwidth, disk I/O speed, or executor memory — it assumes all bytes cost equally regardless of where they move.
When the Cost Model Fails

rows: Skewed join key
Rows are uniformly distributed across join key values
One key value has 65% of rows (Germany in the country column)
Sort-merge join looks cheap (uniform partition sizes) but one partition takes 10x longer
Country-level join where Germany dominates — AQE skew handling compensates
Post-filter size reduction
Statistics predict filter selectivity from column histograms
A correlated filter (WHERE country = 'DE' AND region LIKE 'DE-%') has much higher selectivity than independent estimates
Broadcast threshold not triggered because estimated post-filter size is 3x actual
German regional query where correlated filters reduced 2GB to 400MB — below broadcast threshold but estimated at 1.2GB
Stale statistics after major load
Statistics reflect the data at collection time
A quarterly bulk load added 500M rows — statistics still show pre-load counts
Join strategy chosen for 200M rows when the table now has 700M
Quarterly regulatory load followed by an analytics query before statistics refresh
Nested subquery estimation
Subquery output size estimated from inner statistics
The subquery contains a complex filter chain with 5 conditions — estimate is 50x too high
Sort-merge join chosen when broadcast would have been 100x faster
GridUnion's 12-table regulatory query — the root cause of the 8TB shuffle incident

blocks.content: Manual Hints as Cost Model Overrides

from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast

# The cost model chose sort-merge because it estimated df_small at 1.2GB
# Actual post-filter size is 400MB — well below the 10MB broadcast threshold
# Override with a broadcast hint
df_result = df_large.join(
    broadcast(df_small),  # Force broadcast regardless of cost estimate
    on="region"
)

# Verify the hint was applied
df_result.explain()
# Should show BroadcastHashJoin instead of SortMergeJoin

# IMPORTANT: broadcast hints override the cost model unconditionally
# If df_small is actually 10GB (not 400MB), the broadcast will OOM
# Use hints only when you have verified the actual size

Manual hints are the escape hatch when the cost model makes the wrong decision. GridUnion's policy on hints: every broadcast hint in production code must include a comment explaining why it exists (the specific cost model failure that necessitates it), the actual size of the broadcast table (measured, not estimated), and the conditions under which the hint should be removed (for example, 'remove this hint after statistics are collected on the region column'). Hints without this documentation are rejected in code review because undocumented hints become permanent fixtures that no one dares remove — even after the underlying issue (stale statistics) has been fixed. GridUnion reviews broadcast hints quarterly and has removed 3 of 12 hints after the original cost model failures were resolved by improved statistics collection.

blocks.title: The cost model is approximately right, not exactly right
blocks.content: The cost model's simplifications (uniform distribution, byte-based cost, no network modeling) mean it produces approximate costs, not precise predictions. For most queries, 'approximately right' produces the correct strategy — the cost difference between broadcast and sort-merge is usually large enough that approximation errors do not change the ranking. The model fails when the cost difference is small (two strategies are nearly equal cost) or when the approximation is systematically biased (skewed data always produces optimistic sort-merge estimates). Senior engineers do not distrust the cost model — they understand its approximation boundaries and intervene only when a specific query falls outside those boundaries.

Catalyst optimizes the plan before execution using statistics that may be stale or inaccurate. AQE optimizes the plan during execution using actual intermediate results that are always accurate. The mid-level track covered AQE's three features (partition coalescing, join conversion, skew handling) as black boxes. At senior level, you need to understand the mechanics inside each feature: how does AQE decide the target partition count for coalescing? What threshold triggers join conversion? How does skew splitting determine the sub-partition boundaries? These mechanics determine when AQE helps, when it does nothing, and when its intervention is suboptimal — knowledge that GridUnion's platform team uses to configure AQE for their specific workload characteristics.
Partition Coalescing Mechanics

After a shuffle, AQE examines the actual size of each output partition. If many partitions are smaller than the target size (controlled by spark.sql.adaptive.advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes, default 64MB), AQE merges adjacent partitions until they approach the target. The merging is greedy and order-preserving: partitions 0,1,2,3 might become [0+1, 2+3] if each pair is below the target, or [0+1+2, 3] if partition 3 alone is above the target. In Fabric's managed Spark, the advisory partition size interacts with the capacity model — smaller partitions mean more tasks with more scheduling overhead (consuming CUs for coordination rather than computation), while larger partitions mean fewer tasks but more memory per task. GridUnion sets advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes to 128MB (double the default) because their F128 capacity allocates large-memory executors that handle 128MB partitions efficiently.
Join Strategy Conversion

When Catalyst plans a sort-merge join (because it estimated both sides as large), AQE checks the actual post-shuffle size of each side after the shuffle completes. If one side is smaller than spark.sql.adaptive.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold (which in Fabric defaults to the same value as the static threshold, typically 10MB), AQE converts the sort-merge to a broadcast hash join by broadcasting the small side to all executors. The conversion eliminates the second shuffle and the sort — a significant performance improvement. However, the conversion only triggers after the first shuffle has already completed (both sides were shuffled for the sort-merge), so the benefit is the elimination of the sort and merge phases, not the elimination of both shuffles. GridUnion's diagnostic: if the Spark UI shows a CustomShuffleReader after a sort-merge join, AQE converted the join — but the first shuffle already consumed CUs. If the same query always triggers conversion, a static broadcast hint avoids the wasted first shuffle entirely.
Skew Splitting Mechanics

AQE detects skew by comparing each partition's size to the median partition size after a shuffle. If a partition exceeds spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionFactor (default 5) times the median size AND exceeds spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionThresholdInBytes (default 256MB), AQE splits that partition into sub-partitions of approximately advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes each and replicates the corresponding partition from the other side of the join. The splitting is local to the skewed partition — other partitions are unaffected. GridUnion's German data creates consistent skew (65% of meters), so the DE-country partition always triggers splitting. The splitting adds replication overhead (the non-skewed side's corresponding partition is copied to multiple sub-partition tasks), but the reduction in task duration from splitting the 65% partition typically outweighs the replication cost by 3–5x.
Fabric-Specific AQE Configuration

F128 executors have enough memory; larger partitions reduce task count and scheduling CU overhead

Fabric's managed pool can handle larger broadcasts; 30MB captures more conversion opportunities

visual-recommendation.description: A three-panel diagram showing AQE's three features in action: (left) partition coalescing merging 200 tiny partitions into 24 right-sized ones, (center) join conversion detecting a small post-shuffle table and switching from sort-merge to broadcast, (right) skew splitting dividing one oversized partition into 4 sub-partitions while replicating the other side.

blocks.title: Tune AQE for your dominant workload pattern, not for individual queries
blocks.content: GridUnion's AQE configuration is set at the workspace level, not per-notebook. The advisory partition size of 128MB and the skew factor of 3 are tuned for GridUnion's dominant pattern: country-level joins with German skew on F128 capacity. A different organization with different data distribution, different capacity SKU, and different join patterns would choose different values. The tuning process: run the 5 most expensive production notebooks with default AQE settings, record the Spark UI metrics (partition counts, task durations, shuffle volumes), then adjust one property at a time and remeasure. GridUnion's AQE tuning reduced the aggregate CU consumption of the top-5 notebooks by 18% — a larger improvement than any individual notebook optimization in Module 1.

Fabric's managed Spark is not identical to open-source Apache Spark. Microsoft applies proprietary optimizations, locks certain configuration properties, and adjusts default behaviors to suit the SaaS environment. These differences are mostly invisible — the plan output looks the same, the API is the same — but in specific scenarios, Fabric produces different plans than standalone Spark for the same query with the same data. Understanding these Fabric-specific behaviors is essential for senior engineers who reference open-source Spark documentation and find that the documentation does not match their Fabric experience. This lesson catalogs the known differences and their implications for plan analysis and performance tuning.
V-Order and Scan Optimization

V-Order is a Fabric-proprietary write optimization that sorts data within Parquet files for optimal compression and read performance. The mid-level track treated V-Order as a write-time setting. At senior level, V-Order interacts with the optimizer's scan behavior: V-Ordered files have better-organized row groups with tighter min/max statistics, which improves predicate pushdown effectiveness. A predicate pushdown that skips 40% of row groups on non-V-Ordered files may skip 65% on V-Ordered files because the sorted data creates row groups where min/max ranges do not overlap. This means the optimizer's cost estimate for scans on V-Ordered tables is systematically pessimistic (it uses the non-V-Ordered pushdown selectivity), and the actual scan is faster than estimated. The positive surprise is harmless but explains why some Fabric queries outperform their plan estimates.
Managed Pool Impact on Cost Assumptions

In standalone Spark, the cost model's broadcast threshold considers executor memory — broadcasting a 500MB table to executors with 2GB memory is risky. In Fabric, the managed pool determines executor memory based on the capacity SKU, and the actual memory available may differ from what Catalyst's cost model assumes. Fabric's default broadcast threshold (10MB) is conservative because it must work across all SKU sizes — from F2 (minimal executors) to F128 (large executors). GridUnion's F128 capacity allocates executors with 16GB+ memory, making 30MB broadcasts safe and efficient. The mismatch between Fabric's conservative default and GridUnion's actual executor capacity explains why many join conversions that could be broadcast are planned as sort-merge — and why overriding the threshold is a common senior-level optimization.
Locked Configurations

Some Spark configurations are locked in Fabric's managed environment — setting them has no effect or raises a warning. The locked configurations include properties related to the external shuffle service (Fabric manages its own shuffle infrastructure), memory management (executor memory is determined by the capacity SKU, not by spark.executor.memory), and certain security-related properties (Fabric enforces its own authentication and authorization). When a senior engineer migrates a Spark optimization from a standalone cluster to Fabric, they must check whether the property they are tuning is actually writable in Fabric. GridUnion maintains a list of 'Fabric-writable' Spark properties that the platform team has verified are effective in their capacity configuration — properties not on the list are assumed locked until tested.
Native Connectors and Pushdown

Fabric's native Delta connector has optimized pushdown paths that differ from open-source Spark's Delta connector. The native connector pushes certain predicates and projections at the storage layer (OneLake) rather than at the Spark operator level, which means they do not appear in the Catalyst physical plan's PushedFilters field. A senior engineer inspecting the plan for pushdown verification may incorrectly conclude that pushdown is not happening when it is actually happening below Catalyst's visibility. GridUnion's diagnostic for this scenario: compare the actual scan input bytes (from the Spark UI's SQL tab) to the table's total size. If the scan reads significantly less than the total size but PushedFilters is empty, the native connector's below-Catalyst pushdown is active. This Fabric-specific behavior does not exist in open-source Spark.

blocks.title: Fabric's optimizer is Catalyst-plus, not Catalyst-different
blocks.content: The differences between Fabric's managed Spark and open-source Spark are additive rather than substitutive. Catalyst's rules, the CBO, and AQE work identically. Fabric adds optimizations on top: V-Order scan improvements, native connector pushdown, and managed pool memory allocation. These additions make Fabric's optimizer generally more effective than standalone Spark — queries are usually faster, not slower. The differences matter for diagnostics: when a plan does not match open-source documentation expectations, the explanation is usually a Fabric-specific addition that the documentation does not cover, not a Fabric-specific bug that needs reporting.

The mid-level track verified pushdown by checking PushedFilters in the plan — present means pushdown is working, absent means it is not. At senior level, you need to understand why pushdown fails in specific cases and how to restructure queries to restore it. Pushdown is not all-or-nothing: a complex WHERE clause may have some conditions pushed and others retained as post-scan filters. Understanding which conditions push and which do not — and why — enables you to rewrite conditions for maximum pushdown effectiveness. At GridUnion's scale, the difference between a fully-pushed WHERE clause and a partially-pushed one can be 3TB of unnecessary scan volume.
The PushDownPredicates Rule

The PushDownPredicates optimization rule operates during the Optimization phase. It examines each Filter operator in the logical plan and attempts to push its conditions closer to the data source. The rule traverses downward through the plan tree: if a filter sits above a join, the rule checks whether the filter condition references columns from only one side of the join — if so, it pushes the filter below the join to that side. If a filter sits above a projection, the rule checks whether the filter condition can be evaluated before the projection — if the condition uses only columns present before the projection, it is pushed below. This recursive pushing continues until the filter is directly above the scan operator, where it becomes a PushedFilter or PartitionFilter.
Conditions That Block Pushdown

rows: UDF in the predicate
Catalyst cannot reason about UDF semantics — it does not know if the UDF is deterministic or what range of inputs it accepts
Replace the UDF with built-in functions where possible; compute the UDF result as a derived column and filter on the derived column
Custom tariff classification UDF prevented pushdown of the price_band filter
Non-deterministic function
Functions like rand(), current_timestamp() produce different results on re-evaluation — pushing them below a join could change join semantics
Compute the non-deterministic value once, store in a variable, and use the variable in the filter
current_date() in a filter prevented pushdown until replaced with a literal date parameter
Predicate on a computed column
A filter on a column created by withColumn does not push through the withColumn because the column does not exist before the computation
Apply the filter on the source columns before the withColumn, then derive the computed column after filtering
Filter on consumption_tier (derived from daily_kw) — restructured to filter on daily_kw directly
Complex disjunction across tables
A filter like WHERE a.x = 1 OR b.y = 2 references both sides of a join — neither condition can be pushed to one side
Rewrite as two separate queries with UNION, each with a pushable single-table condition
Cross-country OR condition in the regulatory query — split into per-country CTEs
Column alias obscuring the source
A filter on an aliased column may not be traced back to the source column by the pushdown rule in certain plan structures
Filter on the original column name before the alias is applied
GridUnion's 8TB shuffle — the alias chain prevented pushdown tracing

visual-recommendation.description: A plan tree diagram showing a query with 4 filter conditions, with arrows indicating which conditions successfully push down to the scan level and which are blocked at the Filter operator above the scan. Each blocked condition has an annotation showing the blocking reason (UDF, alias, complex OR) and the restructured version that enables pushdown.

blocks.content: Verifying Pushdown Completeness

# Systematic pushdown verification for a multi-condition filter
df = spark.read.table("silver_meters")

# Original: 4 filter conditions
df_filtered = df.filter(
    (col("year") == 2025) &          # Partition column — should be PartitionFilter
    (col("month") == 3) &            # Partition column — should be PartitionFilter
    (col("region") == "NORTH-1") &   # Non-partition — should be PushedFilter
    (col("daily_kw") > 100)          # Non-partition — should be PushedFilter
)

df_filtered.explain()
# Inspect the plan:
# PartitionFilters: [year = 2025, month = 3]   ← 2 of 4 pushed as partition filters
# PushedFilters: [region = NORTH-1, daily_kw > 100]  ← 2 of 4 pushed as data filters
# Filter: (none remaining)  ← All 4 conditions pushed — complete pushdown

# If any condition remains in the Filter operator (above the scan),
# that condition was NOT pushed — investigate why

Complete pushdown means every condition in the original WHERE clause appears in either PartitionFilters or PushedFilters, and no conditions remain in the Filter operator above the scan. Partial pushdown means some conditions are pushed and others remain as post-scan filters — the pushed conditions reduce the scan volume, but the remaining conditions must be evaluated on every row that passes the pushed filters. GridUnion's pushdown audit runs monthly: for every production notebook, the platform team's audit script captures the plan, checks for conditions that remain in the post-scan Filter, and reports them as pushdown gaps. The audit has identified 14 pushdown gaps across 500+ pipelines, each representing a potential optimization opportunity.

blocks.title: Push the filter to the source, not the result
blocks.content: The most common pushdown failure at GridUnion is filtering on a derived column (consumption_tier, billing_category, risk_score) rather than on the source columns it was computed from. The derived column does not exist in the Parquet file, so it cannot be pushed to the scan. The source columns do exist. Restructure the query to filter on the source columns (daily_kw > 100 instead of consumption_tier = 'HIGH'), then compute the derived column after the filter. This pattern applies to any filter on a computed or aliased column — always push the business logic into the filter condition using source columns, and derive the human-readable labels after the pushdown has reduced the scan volume.

The mid-level track taught three join strategies: broadcast hash join for small tables, sort-merge join for large tables, and shuffle hash join as a fallback. At senior level, you need to understand the decision tree that Catalyst uses to choose between them — the conditions, the thresholds, and the fallback logic. This understanding reveals why some joins use an unexpected strategy and how to influence the decision without resorting to hints. GridUnion's platform team maintains a join strategy audit that reviews every production join's strategy quarterly, identifies suboptimal choices, and determines whether the fix is a statistics improvement, a configuration change, or (rarely) a hint.
The Decision Tree

Catalyst's join strategy selection follows a priority chain. First check: is one side estimated below spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold? If yes, choose broadcast hash join — the cheapest strategy because it eliminates the shuffle on the large side. Second check: are both sides sortable on the join key? If yes, choose sort-merge join — the most general strategy that works for any data size. Third check: is one side small enough to build an in-memory hash table? If yes, choose shuffle hash join — cheaper than sort-merge because it avoids the sort but requires enough memory for the hash table. Fourth: fall back to sort-merge (the safe default). Each check uses the estimated data size from statistics. If the statistics are wrong, the wrong check passes, and the wrong strategy is selected.
The Threshold Chain

# The three key thresholds that determine join strategy selection
# All three interact — changing one can shift the strategy for a specific join

# Threshold 1: Broadcast threshold (default 10MB)
# If one side's estimated size < this value, broadcast hash join is chosen
print(spark.conf.get("spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold"))
# GridUnion: set to 30MB on F128 capacity

# Threshold 2: Shuffle hash join preference (default false)
# If enabled AND one side is 3x smaller than the other, shuffle hash join is preferred over sort-merge
print(spark.conf.get("spark.sql.join.preferSortMergeJoin"))
# Default: true (sort-merge preferred). GridUnion: true (sort-merge is safer at scale)

# Threshold 3: AQE broadcast conversion threshold
# If AQE detects post-shuffle size < this value, it converts sort-merge to broadcast
print(spark.conf.get("spark.sql.adaptive.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold"))
# GridUnion: set to 30MB (aligned with static threshold)

# Diagnostic: compare estimated size to these thresholds
# If estimated size is 35MB (above 30MB threshold), Catalyst chooses sort-merge
# If actual post-filter size is 8MB, AQE converts to broadcast at runtime
