# The first shuffle was wasted — a static hint would have been more efficient

The threshold chain reveals a gap between Catalyst's static decision and AQE's runtime correction. If Catalyst estimates a table at 35MB (above the 30MB broadcast threshold) and chooses sort-merge, but the actual post-filter size is 8MB, AQE converts to broadcast after the first shuffle is already complete. The CUs spent on the first shuffle are wasted. GridUnion's join audit identifies these cases: joins where AQE consistently converts sort-merge to broadcast, indicating that the static estimate is persistently wrong. For persistent conversions, the fix is either improving statistics (so Catalyst gets the estimate right on the first attempt) or adding a broadcast hint (bypassing the estimate entirely). AQE conversion should be the exception, not the norm — it is a safety net, not a strategy.
Strategy Verification in the Spark UI

The Spark UI's SQL/DataFrame tab shows the actual join strategy used for each join operator. Look for the operator name: BroadcastHashJoin, SortMergeJoin, or ShuffledHashJoin. If AQE converted the strategy at runtime, the SQL tab shows the original operator with an AQE annotation. GridUnion's join audit script parses the Spark UI data (available via the Spark History Server REST API) programmatically, extracts the join strategy for each join in every production notebook, and writes the results to a join_strategy_log table. The quarterly review compares the logged strategies to the expected strategies (broadcast for small tables, sort-merge for large tables) and flags discrepancies for investigation.

blocks.title: The best join strategy is the one that avoids the most shuffle
blocks.content: The cost model assigns numbers to strategies. The practical measure is simpler: how many bytes were shuffled? Broadcast hash join shuffles the small side to all executors (small shuffle, scales with executor count). Sort-merge shuffles both sides by the join key (large shuffle, scales with data size). Shuffle hash join shuffles both sides but avoids the sort. At GridUnion's scale, a sort-merge join on the country-level Silver table shuffles 12TB. A broadcast join on the 200-row substations table shuffles 50KB. The 240,000x difference in shuffle volume dwarfs any cost model nuance. When in doubt, choose the strategy that minimizes shuffle bytes — the cost model and the practical measure usually agree, and when they disagree, the practical measure is right.

GridUnion's German Silver notebook ran stably at 14 minutes for 3 months. On a Tuesday, without any code change, it took 38 minutes. The code was identical. The data volume was normal. The capacity was not throttled. The Spark UI showed a sort-merge join where a broadcast join had been used for 3 months. The cause: a Delta table metadata update (from a weekly OPTIMIZE) changed the table's reported size in the transaction log, pushing the estimated size above the broadcast threshold. The plan changed silently — no alert, no notification, no log entry. This lesson covers how to detect plan regressions before they manifest as SLA breaches.
Plan Fingerprinting

A plan fingerprint is a hash of the physical plan's structure — the sequence of operators, their types, and their key parameters (join strategy, partition count, scan type). When the plan changes, the fingerprint changes. GridUnion's monitoring notebook captures the plan fingerprint after every production notebook run and stores it in the pipeline_metrics table. The weekly anomaly detector compares the current fingerprint to the previous fingerprint — if they differ, the plan changed. A plan change is not inherently bad (AQE improvements are plan changes), but an unexpected plan change on a notebook whose code did not change warrants investigation. The fingerprint turns the invisible plan change into a detectable, alertable event.

import hashlib

def capture_plan_fingerprint(df, plan_name: str) -> str:
    """Capture a fingerprint of the physical plan for regression detection.
    The fingerprint changes when the plan structure changes."""
    plan_string = df._jdf.queryExecution().executedPlan().toString()
    
    # Normalize: remove runtime-specific IDs (plan_id, codegen_id)
    # that change between runs but do not indicate structural changes
    import re
    normalized = re.sub(r'#\d+', '#N', plan_string)  # Normalize column IDs
    normalized = re.sub(r'plan_id=\d+', 'plan_id=N', normalized)
    normalized = re.sub(r'codegen id : \d+', 'codegen id : N', normalized)
    
    fingerprint = hashlib.sha256(normalized.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]
    
    # Log to metrics table
    spark.sql(f"""
        INSERT INTO pipeline_metrics (notebook, metric, value, timestamp)
        VALUES ('{plan_name}', 'plan_fingerprint', '{fingerprint}', current_timestamp())
    """)
    
    return fingerprint

# Usage at the end of the Silver notebook:
fp = capture_plan_fingerprint(df_final, "nb_de_transform_meters")
print(f"Plan fingerprint: {fp}")

The fingerprint function normalizes the plan by replacing runtime-specific IDs (column reference numbers, plan IDs, codegen IDs) with placeholders, then hashes the normalized string. This normalization ensures that identical plans produce identical fingerprints across different runs. Structural changes — a different join strategy, a different scan type, a new exchange operator — produce a different fingerprint. GridUnion's alerting pipeline compares today's fingerprint to yesterday's for every production notebook and sends a Slack notification when a fingerprint changes: 'Plan change detected for nb_de_transform_meters. Previous: a3f8b2c1. Current: 7e2d9f4a. Investigate before the next scheduled run.'
Root Cause Analysis for Plan Changes

When a plan fingerprint changes, the root cause is one of four categories. Configuration change: someone modified a Spark property (unlikely in production if CI/CD is enforced, but possible through workspace settings). Statistics change: an OPTIMIZE, VACUUM, or data load changed the table's metadata, which changed the optimizer's estimates. Data volume change: a seasonal or business-driven volume change pushed the data size across a threshold. Fabric platform update: Microsoft updated the managed Spark version or optimizer behavior. GridUnion's investigation checklist for plan changes: (1) check for code changes in the Git log, (2) check for Spark configuration changes in the workspace settings audit, (3) compare table statistics before and after the change using DESCRIBE DETAIL, (4) check Fabric service health for platform updates. The checklist eliminates categories until the root cause is identified.

blocks.title: Silent plan changes are the most dangerous performance regressions
blocks.content: A code change that degrades performance is caught by the test suite and the CI/CD pipeline. A data volume spike is caught by the row count monitor. A capacity throttling event is caught by the capacity metrics. But a plan change caused by a statistics update is caught by nothing in the standard monitoring stack — the pipeline succeeds, the row count is correct, the capacity is not throttled, and the only symptom is a slower runtime that may not breach the SLA immediately. Plan fingerprinting is the only mechanism that detects these regressions at the point of change rather than weeks later when the cumulative slowdown finally breaches a threshold.

The mid-level track taught column pruning for flat schemas: select only the columns you need, verify ReadSchema in the plan, and measure the I/O reduction. At senior level, column pruning extends to nested schemas — structs, arrays of structs, and maps that are common in semi-structured data like IoT telemetry, API responses, and event logs. GridUnion's SCADA telemetry table has a nested schema with 200+ fields organized in a 4-level struct hierarchy (turbine → sensor_group → sensor → readings). Reading the entire struct to extract one sensor's temperature value reads 200 fields when 1 would suffice — a 200x I/O overhead that is invisible in the flat-schema pruning verification.
Nested Column Pruning in Spark

Spark's NestedColumnPruning rule (introduced in Spark 3.0, enabled by default) prunes individual fields within nested structs. When a query accesses only turbine.sensor_group.temperature.readings.avg_temp, the pruning rule modifies the ReadSchema to read only that nested path, skipping the other 199 fields. The pruning works at the Parquet level: Parquet stores nested fields as separate column chunks, so reading one nested field is a physical column read, not a struct deserialization. The pruning is verified in the plan's ReadSchema field — instead of showing the full 200-field struct, it shows only the accessed nested path.

# GridUnion's SCADA telemetry table with deeply nested schema
# Schema: turbine_id, timestamp, telemetry (struct with 200+ nested fields)

# BAD: reads the entire telemetry struct (all 200 fields)
df = spark.read.table("silver_scada") \
    .select("turbine_id", "timestamp", "telemetry")
# ReadSchema: struct<turbine_id:string, timestamp:timestamp, telemetry:struct<...200 fields...>>

# GOOD: reads only the specific nested field
df = spark.read.table("silver_scada") \
    .select(
        "turbine_id",
        "timestamp",
        col("telemetry.sensor_group.temperature.readings.avg_temp").alias("avg_temp")
    )
# ReadSchema: struct<turbine_id:string, timestamp:timestamp,
#   telemetry:struct<sensor_group:struct<temperature:struct<readings:struct<avg_temp:double>>>>>
# Only the specific nested path is read — 199 fields pruned

df.explain()  # Verify ReadSchema shows the pruned nested path

The I/O reduction from nested pruning is dramatic at GridUnion's SCADA scale. The full telemetry struct is approximately 2KB per row (200 fields × ~10 bytes average). The pruned path (one double value) is 8 bytes per row — a 250x reduction. At 172 million SCADA events per day, the difference between reading the full struct and reading one nested field is 344GB vs 1.4GB per day of scan volume. This translates directly to CU savings: approximately 200 CU-minutes per day saved by nested pruning on this single table. GridUnion's coding standard requires that all SCADA queries access specific nested fields, never the top-level telemetry struct.
Pruning Limitations

rows: select('telemetry.*') — explode all nested fields
Reads the entire struct — no pruning because all fields are requested
Select only the specific nested fields needed: select('telemetry.temperature', 'telemetry.rpm')
UDF that takes the struct as input
Reads the entire struct — Catalyst cannot determine which fields the UDF accesses
Restructure to pass individual fields to the UDF instead of the struct: udf(col('telemetry.temperature'))
Schema evolution with new nested fields
Pruning applies to the requested fields — new fields added by mergeSchema are not read unless explicitly requested
No workaround needed — pruning automatically ignores new fields (a benefit of nested pruning)
Deeply nested array of structs
Pruning works for struct fields but has limited support for individual elements within arrays
Flatten the array to a struct before querying, or accept the full-array read as the cost of array access

blocks.title: Nested pruning is the senior-level I/O optimization for semi-structured data
blocks.content: Flat column pruning (mid-level) reduces I/O by selecting columns. Nested column pruning (senior-level) reduces I/O by selecting fields within columns. For flat schemas with 10 columns, the maximum pruning benefit is 10x (reading 1 of 10). For nested schemas with 200 fields inside a struct column, the maximum pruning benefit is 200x. GridUnion's SCADA table is the extreme case, but any table with JSON-derived schemas (API responses, event payloads, configuration objects) benefits from nested pruning. The diagnostic is the same as flat pruning: check ReadSchema in the plan. If it shows the full struct, nested pruning is not active — restructure the query to access specific nested paths.

The previous nine lessons covered Catalyst's rule batch architecture, statistics propagation and estimation errors, the CBO cost model and its failure modes, AQE's runtime adaptation mechanics with Fabric-specific configuration, Fabric-managed Spark's proprietary optimizer behaviors, predicate pushdown at the rule level with blocking conditions, join strategy selection internals with the threshold chain, plan fingerprinting for regression detection, and nested column pruning for semi-structured data. This lesson synthesizes everything into GridUnion's optimizer diagnostic toolkit — the systematic process the platform team follows when a query's plan does not match expectations.
The Diagnostic Workflow

blocks.title: GridUnion’s optimizer diagnostic decision tree
blocks.content:
QUERY PERFORMANCE PROBLEM
│
├─ Step 1: Is the plan correct?
│  ├─ Check plan fingerprint — has it changed?
│  ├─ Compare explain("cost") estimates to Spark UI actuals
│  └─ If estimates are 10x+ off → STATISTICS PROBLEM (lesson 2)
│     ├─ Run ANALYZE TABLE on the affected columns
│     └─ Verify improvement and update collection schedule
│
├─ Step 2: Is pushdown complete?
│  ├─ Check PartitionFilters and PushedFilters in the plan
│  ├─ Check for conditions remaining in post-scan Filter
│  └─ If pushdown gaps exist → PUSHDOWN FAILURE (lesson 6)
│     ├─ Identify the blocking condition (UDF, alias, complex OR)
│     └─ Restructure the query to enable pushdown
│
├─ Step 3: Is the join strategy optimal?
│  ├─ Check the join operator in the Spark UI SQL tab
│  ├─ Compare actual side sizes to broadcast threshold
│  └─ If sort-merge on a small table → COST MODEL FAILURE (lesson 3)
│     ├─ Improve statistics or add a broadcast hint with documentation
│     └─ Check if AQE is converting consistently (lesson 4)
│
└─ Step 4: Is it a Fabric-specific behavior?
   ├─ Check V-Order interaction (faster scans than estimated)
   ├─ Check native connector pushdown (below-Catalyst pushdown)
   └─ Check locked configurations (lesson 5)
blocks.caption: The diagnostic workflow follows a priority chain: statistics first (cheapest fix), then pushdown verification (structural fix), then join strategy review (configuration or hint fix), then Fabric-specific behaviors (environment investigation). Each step has a clear action if the diagnosis matches.

visual-recommendation.description: A vertical decision tree diagram with four diagnostic steps, each with branching outcomes (problem identified vs proceed to next step), action items for each identified problem, and time estimates for each diagnostic step. Color-coded by fix type: green (statistics — automatic), yellow (query restructure — code change), red (hint — requires documentation and quarterly review).

blocks.content: The Quarterly Optimizer Review

GridUnion's platform team conducts a quarterly optimizer review of the top 20 production notebooks by CU consumption. The review examines each notebook's plan fingerprint history (has the plan changed?), estimated-vs-actual row counts (are statistics accurate?), join strategies (are any sort-merge joins candidates for broadcast?), pushdown completeness (are any filters not pushed?), and AQE conversion frequency (are any joins consistently converting at runtime?). The review produces a prioritized list of optimization opportunities ranked by estimated CU savings. In the last quarter, the review identified 6 optimization opportunities totaling 1,200 CU-minutes per day — equivalent to a 15% reduction in the engineering capacity's total consumption.
What Comes Next

Module 3 covers partition strategy at enterprise scale — the physical data layout decisions that determine I/O volume before the optimizer even begins. The optimizer produces the best plan it can for the given data layout. If the data layout is wrong (partitioned by the wrong column, fragmented into small files, missing Z-ORDER), the best plan is still expensive. Module 2 taught you to optimize the plan. Module 3 teaches you to optimize the data layout that the plan operates on. Together, they cover both sides of the performance equation: how Spark processes your query (the plan) and how the data is organized for that processing (the layout).

blocks.title: Optimizer knowledge is diagnostic power
blocks.content: Most data engineers treat the optimizer as an authority — it produces a plan, and the engineer accepts the plan. Senior engineers treat the optimizer as a collaborator — it produces a plan based on its knowledge (statistics, rules, cost model), and the engineer evaluates whether that knowledge is correct. When the knowledge is correct, the plan is optimal and no intervention is needed. When the knowledge is wrong (stale statistics, blocked pushdown, incorrect cost estimate), the engineer corrects the knowledge (update statistics, restructure the query, add a documented hint) and the optimizer produces a better plan. The nine lessons in this module gave you the diagnostic tools to evaluate the optimizer's knowledge. The quarterly review ensures those tools are used systematically, not just reactively.

chapters.title: Partition Strategy at Scale
chapters.description: Design, implement, and maintain partition strategies for multi-terabyte Delta tables in Fabric — covering column selection for multi-consumer tables, granularity trade-offs, dynamic partition pruning internals, small files at enterprise scale, and the operational challenge of repartitioning a live production table.

blocks.content: The mid-level track designed partition strategies for tables consumed by one pipeline and one analytics team. The partition column choice was straightforward: partition by year and month because every query filters by date range. At GridUnion's scale, the unified Silver meter table has 40+ downstream consumers with conflicting query patterns. The German billing team queries by date range (WHERE year = 2025 AND month = 3). The French regulatory team queries by country first (WHERE country = 'FR'). The Platinum consolidation pipeline queries by country and date. The ML team queries by meter_id for feature extraction — a column with 5 million distinct values that would create 5 million partition directories. There is no single partition scheme that serves all 40 consumers optimally. Partition design at enterprise scale is a multi-stakeholder trade-off, not a technical decision with one correct answer.
The Consumer Survey

GridUnion's partition design process begins with a consumer survey: for every registered consumer of the table (from the data product manifest), the platform team records the primary filter pattern, the secondary filter pattern, the query frequency (daily, hourly, ad-hoc), and the data volume scanned per query. The survey reveals which filter patterns are most common and most expensive. For the unified Silver meter table, the survey showed: 65% of query volume filters by country + date (the billing and regulatory teams across 4 countries), 20% filters by date only (analytics dashboards), 10% filters by country only (cross-date regulatory queries), and 5% filters by meter_id (ML feature extraction). The partition design optimizes for the 65% case — country as the first partition level, date as the second — while providing Z-ORDER and data skipping for the remaining 35%.
The Partition Column Scoring Matrix

rows: country (4 values)
Very low
85% of queries filter by country
4x pruning — reads 1 of 4 countries
4 directories at top level — minimal overhead
HIGH — best first-level partition
year (3 values)
Very low
95% of queries filter by year
3x pruning — reads 1 of 3 years
12 directories (4 countries × 3 years) — manageable
HIGH — best second-level partition
month (12 values)
Low
70% of queries filter by month
12x pruning within a year
144 directories (4 × 3 × 12) — acceptable
MEDIUM — third level adds value for date-range queries
region (30 values)
Medium
30% of queries filter by region
30x pruning when used
4,320 directories (144 × 30) — approaching overhead threshold
LOW — Z-ORDER instead of partitioning
meter_id (5M values)
Very high
5% of queries filter by meter_id
Extreme pruning for point lookups
5M directories — catastrophic file count
REJECT — never partition on high-cardinality columns
customer_type (5 values)
Very low
15% of queries filter by customer_type
5x pruning when used
720 directories (144 × 5) — acceptable but rarely queried
LOW — Z-ORDER instead of partitioning

visual-recommendation.description: A scatter plot showing each candidate partition column positioned by cardinality (x-axis, log scale) vs query coverage (y-axis, percentage), with bubble size representing the pruning benefit and color indicating the score (green=HIGH, yellow=MEDIUM, red=LOW/REJECT). The optimal partition columns cluster in the low-cardinality, high-query-coverage quadrant.

blocks.content: The Final Design

GridUnion's unified Silver meter table uses three partition levels: country / year / month. This scheme produces 144 directories (4 × 3 × 12) at steady state — well within OneLake's metadata overhead threshold. The 65% majority query pattern (country + date) benefits from full partition pruning. The 20% date-only pattern benefits from year/month pruning (3x × 12x = 36x reduction). The 10% country-only pattern benefits from country pruning (4x reduction). The 5% meter_id pattern does not benefit from partitioning at all — but Z-ORDER by (region, meter_id) within each partition provides data-skipping through Parquet min/max statistics, reducing scan volume by approximately 60% for meter_id lookups. The design serves 95% of query volume with partition pruning and the remaining 5% with Z-ORDER — a deliberate trade-off that the consumer survey justified.

blocks.title: Partition design is a political negotiation, not a technical optimization
blocks.content: The German billing team wanted date-first partitioning (year/month/country) because 80% of their queries filter by date range first. The French regulatory team wanted country-first partitioning (country/year/month) because their queries always filter by country first. Both are technically valid. The platform team chose country-first because the regulatory use case has stricter SLAs (compliance deadlines vs internal reporting timelines) and because country-first enables country-level data isolation through workspace-level access controls — a security requirement that date-first does not satisfy. The technical trade-off was marginal (both schemes provide equivalent pruning when both columns are filtered). The organizational trade-off determined the decision. Document these decisions in the partition design record so that the next team to ask 'why country-first?' finds the answer in the record, not in someone's memory.

The mid-level track's target partition size was 128–256 MB for shuffle partitions. At senior level, the concern is storage partitions — the directories on OneLake that hold the Parquet files for each partition value combination. A storage partition that is too small (under 100 MB) wastes I/O on per-file overhead. A storage partition that is too large (over 10 GB) forces queries to scan more data than necessary even with predicate pushdown, because pushdown operates at the row-group level within files, not at the file level. GridUnion's Silver meter table grows by 5 million rows per day (approximately 15 GB). The partition sizing model must predict the storage partition sizes 2 years into the future to avoid a mid-lifecycle repartitioning that would affect all 40 downstream consumers.
The Sizing Model

# GridUnion's partition sizing projection model
# Predicts partition directory sizes 24 months out

countries = 4
years_of_history = 3
months_per_year = 12

# Current data characteristics
rows_per_day = 5_000_000                    # 5M rows/day across all countries
bytes_per_row_compressed = 180              # After V-Order compression
growth_rate_annual = 0.15                   # 15% YoY meter installation growth

# Country distribution (non-uniform — Germany dominates)
country_share = {'DE': 0.40, 'FR': 0.30, 'NL': 0.20, 'LU': 0.10}

# Project 24 months forward
for month_offset in [0, 6, 12, 18, 24]:
    growth_factor = (1 + growth_rate_annual) ** (month_offset / 12)
    daily_rows = rows_per_day * growth_factor
    
    for country, share in country_share.items():
        country_daily_rows = daily_rows * share
        monthly_rows = country_daily_rows * 30
        monthly_bytes = monthly_rows * bytes_per_row_compressed
        monthly_gb = monthly_bytes / (1024**3)
        
        # Only print Germany (largest) and Luxembourg (smallest)
        if country in ('DE', 'LU'):
            print(f"Month +{month_offset:2d}: {country} monthly partition = "
                  f"{monthly_rows/1e6:.1f}M rows, {monthly_gb:.1f} GB")

# Output:
# Month + 0: DE monthly partition = 60.0M rows, 10.1 GB
# Month + 0: LU monthly partition = 15.0M rows,  2.5 GB
# Month +24: DE monthly partition = 79.4M rows, 13.3 GB
# Month +24: LU monthly partition = 19.8M rows,  3.3 GB

The model reveals two sizing concerns. First, the German monthly partition is already 10.1 GB — above the optimal 1–5 GB range for individual partitions. Queries that filter to one month of German data still scan 10.1 GB, which means predicate pushdown within the partition must work effectively to limit the actual data read. Second, Luxembourg's monthly partition is 2.5 GB — well-sized now but small relative to Germany, creating a 4:1 size skew between the largest and smallest country. This skew affects query planning: the optimizer estimates partition sizes using table-level averages, not per-partition actuals, so it overestimates Luxembourg scan costs and underestimates German scan costs.
Mitigating Large Partitions

For the German monthly partitions exceeding 10 GB, GridUnion has three options. Option A: add a sub-partition level (week or day) to split the 10 GB partition into smaller ones. This increases the directory count (144 × 4 weeks = 576 directories) but brings each partition below 3 GB. Option B: keep the current partition scheme but rely on Z-ORDER to improve data skipping within the large partition. Z-ORDER by region clusters the data so that queries with a region filter skip 70–80% of row groups even within the 10 GB partition. Option C: accept the 10 GB partitions and invest in file-level optimization — OPTIMIZE with bin-packing to produce 4–5 optimally-sized files per partition rather than dozens of small files from daily appends. GridUnion chose Option B (Z-ORDER) for now, with a planned migration to Option A (sub-partitioning by week) when German monthly partitions exceed 15 GB — projected at month +18 based on the sizing model.
The Two-Year Projection Rule

GridUnion's partition sizing policy requires a 24-month projection for every new production table. The projection must show: the partition directory count (must not exceed 5,000 total directories), the largest partition size (must not exceed 15 GB without a documented mitigation plan), the smallest partition size (must not be below 50 MB — otherwise combine partition levels), and the size ratio between the largest and smallest partitions (must not exceed 10:1 without documented skew handling). The projection is included in the data product manifest as a mandatory appendix, reviewed during the certification process (Module 1, lesson 4), and updated quarterly during the partition audit (lesson 10). A table that violates the sizing thresholds at any point in the 24-month window requires a mitigation plan before certification.

blocks.title: Partition sizing is a prediction, not a calculation
blocks.content: The sizing model uses growth rate assumptions, country share estimates, and compression ratios — all of which can change. GridUnion's French meter fleet grew 25% in one quarter (a government smart-meter mandate) when the model predicted 15%. The French monthly partition jumped from 4.5 GB to 5.6 GB in a single quarter, two years ahead of the projection. The model is not wrong — it is a prediction based on available information. The quarterly partition audit (lesson 10) catches prediction errors by comparing actual partition sizes to the model's projections and triggering a redesign review when the deviation exceeds 30%. Model accuracy matters less than model revision frequency.

Partition pruning skips entire directories. Z-ORDER enables data skipping within a partition — skipping individual Parquet row groups based on their column statistics (min/max values per column). Without Z-ORDER, data within a file is organized by insertion order, which means each row group contains a random mix of all regions, all meter_ids, and all customer_types. The min/max statistics span the full range of values, so no row group can be skipped. With Z-ORDER by region, data is clustered so that each row group contains a narrow range of region values — NORTH-1 rows are physically close to other NORTH-1 rows. A query that filters WHERE region = 'NORTH-1' can skip row groups whose min/max range does not include NORTH-1, reading only the row groups that contain NORTH-1 data.
How Z-ORDER Works

Z-ORDER uses a space-filling curve (specifically a Z-order curve) to interleave the binary representations of the specified columns' values and sort the data by the interleaved result. This interleaving clusters data points that are close in multiple dimensions (region and meter_id) into the same row groups. The clustering is approximate — a Z-order curve cannot perfectly cluster multi-dimensional data into one-dimensional file order — but it is effective enough to reduce scan volume by 60–80% for filters on the Z-ORDER columns. The OPTIMIZE command with ZORDER BY applies this clustering: it reads the partition's files, sorts the data by the Z-order curve, and writes new files with clustered row groups.
Column Selection for Z-ORDER

rows: region (30 values)
Medium
30% of queries
High — 30 values produce well-separated row groups with tight min/max ranges
PRIMARY Z-ORDER column — highest query coverage among non-partition columns
meter_id (5M values)
Very high
5% of queries
Medium — 5M values spread across many row groups, each containing a range of ~1000 IDs
SECONDARY Z-ORDER column — less effective than region but still provides 50% skip rate for point lookups
customer_type (5 values)
Very low
15% of queries
Low — 5 values are too few to create meaningful clusters within files that already contain thousands of rows
NOT Z-ORDERED — 5 values do not benefit; use a filter instead
daily_kw (continuous)
Very high (continuous)
2% of queries
Very low — continuous values produce poor clustering because adjacent Z-order curve positions span wide value ranges
NOT Z-ORDERED — continuous numeric columns do not cluster well

blocks.content: Z-ORDER vs Partition Pruning

left.label: Partition Pruning (directory-level)
points: Eliminates entire directories before reading any data
100% effective: skipped directories are completely untouched
Best for: low-cardinality columns used in most queries (country, year, month)
Cost: directory creation at write time, metadata overhead proportional to directory count
GridUnion: country, year, month — 144 directories

right.label: Z-ORDER Data Skipping (row-group-level)
points: Eliminates row groups within files after reading the file footer
60–80% effective: some row groups contain boundary-value overlap
Best for: medium-cardinality columns used in some queries (region, meter_id)
Cost: OPTIMIZE with ZORDER BY must run periodically to maintain clustering as new data is appended
GridUnion: region, meter_id — 60–80% skip rate on non-partition queries

blocks.content:
-- GridUnion's Z-ORDER strategy for the unified Silver meter table
-- Run weekly as part of the maintenance notebook

-- Z-ORDER the current month's German partition by region and meter_id
OPTIMIZE silver_meters
WHERE country = 'DE' AND year = 2025 AND month = 3
ZORDER BY (region, meter_id);

-- Verify data skipping effectiveness: compare scan volume with and without filter
-- 1. Full partition scan (baseline)
-- SELECT COUNT(*) FROM silver_meters WHERE country = 'DE' AND year = 2025 AND month = 3
--    → Spark UI: 10.1 GB input size
-- 2. Region-filtered scan (with Z-ORDER skipping)
-- SELECT COUNT(*) FROM silver_meters WHERE country = 'DE' AND year = 2025 AND month = 3
--   AND region = 'DE-NORTH-1'
--    → Spark UI: 2.8 GB input size (72% skipped via row-group statistics)

blocks.title: Z-ORDER is additive — it does not replace partitioning
blocks.content: The optimal data layout uses both partitioning and Z-ORDER: partition by the highest-coverage, lowest-cardinality columns (country, year, month) to eliminate directories at scan time, then Z-ORDER within each partition by the medium-cardinality columns (region, meter_id) to skip row groups within the remaining files. The two mechanisms operate at different levels (directory vs row-group) and complement each other. A table with only partitioning but no Z-ORDER leaves row-group-level optimization on the table. A table with only Z-ORDER but no partitioning forces Spark to read every file's footer to evaluate row-group statistics — which is more expensive than skipping directories entirely. Use both.

Static partition pruning applies filters that are literal constants in the WHERE clause: WHERE country = 'DE' is a constant that Spark resolves at plan time. Dynamic Partition Pruning (DPP) applies filters that are derived from another table at runtime: in a join between the Silver meter table and a small dimension table filtered to German regions, DPP uses the dimension table's region values as a runtime filter on the Silver table's scan. Without DPP, the join shuffles the entire Silver table (15 TB). With DPP, the join scans only the partitions that match the dimension table's filter — potentially reading 3 TB instead of 15 TB. The difference is enormous, but DPP activation is not guaranteed. Understanding the internals reveals when DPP fires and when it silently fails.
How DPP Works

DPP operates in two phases. In the build phase, Spark executes the small side of the join (the dimension table with its filter), collects the distinct values of the join key, and constructs a broadcast filter containing those values. In the probe phase, Spark pushes this broadcast filter to the large side's scan, using it as a PartitionFilter that prunes partitions whose key values are not in the broadcast filter. The broadcast filter acts like a runtime WHERE IN clause that the optimizer generates automatically from the join's build side. The result is that the large table's scan reads only the partitions that will actually join with the small table — eliminating the rest without a shuffle.
Conditions That Prevent DPP Activation

rows: Join key is not a partition column
DPP can only push filters to partition directories; non-partition join keys cannot be pruned at the directory level
Check if the join key appears in PartitionFilters — if not, DPP is inactive for this key
Join on region (non-partition column) — DPP inactive; Z-ORDER provides partial compensation
Build side too large for broadcast
DPP requires the build side to be broadcast — if it exceeds the broadcast threshold, DPP does not activate
Check plan for BroadcastExchange on the small side; if absent, the build side is too large for broadcast
Dimension table with 50K rows (25MB) exceeded the 10MB default threshold — raised to 30MB to enable DPP
spark.sql.optimizer.dynamicPartitionPruning.enabled = false
DPP is disabled explicitly (sometimes done for debugging and not re-enabled)
Check configuration value — should be true in all production workspaces
Staging workspace had DPP disabled from a debugging session 3 months ago — nobody re-enabled it
Subquery or complex join condition
DPP requires a simple equi-join on the partition column; complex conditions (OR, range, expression) prevent DPP filter generation
Check if the join condition is a simple equality on a partition column
Join condition included a date range expression — rewritten to a simple equality on year/month to enable DPP

visual-recommendation.description: A two-phase diagram showing DPP in action: (Phase 1) the small dimension table is scanned, filtered, and its join key values are collected into a broadcast filter. (Phase 2) the broadcast filter is pushed to the large fact table's scan, pruning partition directories whose key values are not in the filter. Annotated with scan volume before and after DPP: 15TB → 3TB.

blocks.content: The GridUnion DPP Incident

GridUnion's German billing pipeline joined the 15TB Silver meter table with a 200-row tariff dimension table filtered to German tariffs. The join should have activated DPP, pruning the Silver scan to the German country partition (6 TB) — saving 9 TB of unnecessary scan. Instead, the Spark UI showed a full 15 TB scan. The diagnosis followed the DPP blocking conditions checklist: the join key was country, which is a partition column (condition 1 passed). The tariff table was 200 rows — well below the broadcast threshold (condition 2 passed). DPP was enabled (condition 3 passed). The join condition was country = country — simple equality (condition 4 passed). All four conditions passed, but DPP was not active. The root cause: a broadcast hint on the tariff table forced Spark to choose a BroadcastHashJoin strategy that bypassed the DPP code path. Removing the unnecessary broadcast hint — which was a leftover from a pre-DPP optimization — restored DPP activation and reduced the scan from 15 TB to 6 TB.

blocks.title: Broadcast hints can disable DPP
blocks.content: This is counterintuitive: a broadcast hint on the small side of a join should be harmless or helpful, because it ensures the small table is broadcast. But Spark's DPP implementation uses a specific code path (DynamicPruningSubquery) that integrates with the BroadcastExchange operator chosen by the cost model. When a broadcast hint forces the join strategy directly, the DPP code path may not activate because the hint bypasses the cost model's strategy selection — and the DPP filter generation is coupled to that selection. GridUnion's policy: do not use broadcast hints on joins where DPP is expected. Let the cost model choose broadcast naturally (by ensuring the small side is below the threshold), and DPP will activate as a consequence. If a broadcast hint is necessary for other reasons, verify DPP activation in the plan and accept the trade-off if DPP is disabled.

At mid-level, small files were a maintenance concern — weekly OPTIMIZE compacted them, and the problem was manageable. At GridUnion's scale (15 TB, 500+ pipelines, 200+ tables), small files are a systemic operational problem that requires automated detection, prioritized compaction, and organizational discipline. The unified Silver meter table accumulates 144 new files per day (one per partition from the daily MERGE). The 4 SCADA streaming tables accumulate 2,000+ files per day from micro-batch appends. The 8 Gold country-level tables accumulate files from daily INSERT operations. After one month without maintenance, the total file count across all production tables exceeds 500,000 — each one requiring a separate I/O operation to open, read the Parquet footer, and begin decoding. The aggregate scan overhead from small files costs GridUnion approximately 800 CU-minutes per day.
The Compaction Priority Matrix

Not all tables need compaction with the same urgency. A table with 50 files per partition that is queried 200 times per day costs 50 × 200 = 10,000 file-open operations daily. A table with 500 files per partition that is queried once per month costs 500 × 1 = 500 file-open operations monthly. The first table needs compaction more urgently despite having fewer files because its query frequency multiplies the overhead. GridUnion's compaction priority score combines file count per partition, query frequency, and the table's freshness SLA: tables with high query frequency, high file count, and strict SLAs are compacted first. The automated maintenance framework processes tables in priority order within the OPTIMIZE window, ensuring that the most impactful compactions complete even if the maintenance window is cut short.

# GridUnion's compaction priority scoring for automated maintenance
# Runs weekly to determine which tables to OPTIMIZE first

def compute_compaction_priority(table_name: str, spark) -> float:
    """Score a table's compaction urgency. Higher = more urgent."""
    # File count per partition (from Delta table metadata)
    detail = spark.sql(f"DESCRIBE DETAIL {table_name}").collect()[0]
    num_files = detail.numFiles
    
    # Average files per partition (approximate)
    partitions = spark.sql(f"SHOW PARTITIONS {table_name}").count()
    avg_files_per_partition = num_files / max(partitions, 1)
    
    # Query frequency (from the pipeline_metrics and query_log tables)
    query_freq = spark.sql(f"""
        SELECT COUNT(*) as freq FROM gu_query_log
        WHERE table_name = '{table_name}' AND timestamp > current_date() - 7
    """).collect()[0].freq / 7  # Daily average
    
    # Freshness SLA urgency (1.0 for critical, 0.5 for medium, 0.2 for low)
    sla_weight = get_sla_weight(table_name)  # From data product manifest
    
    # Priority = files_per_partition × daily_query_count × sla_weight
    priority = avg_files_per_partition * query_freq * sla_weight
    return priority

# Example output for GridUnion's top-5 tables:
# silver_meters (DE):     priority = 117 × 180 × 1.0 = 21,060  ← OPTIMIZE first
# silver_scada (DE):      priority = 2100 × 5 × 0.5 = 5,250
# gold_monthly_billing:   priority = 12 × 200 × 1.0 = 2,400
# ref_substations:        priority = 3 × 50 × 0.2 = 30         ← OPTIMIZE last
The Automated Maintenance Framework

GridUnion's maintenance framework is a scheduled notebook (nb_gu_weekly_maintenance) that runs every Sunday at 03:00 UTC. The notebook computes compaction priorities for all 200+ production tables, sorts them by priority score, and processes them sequentially: OPTIMIZE with ZORDER for tables that have Z-ORDER columns configured, OPTIMIZE without ZORDER for tables that do not. The notebook has a time budget (3 hours — the maintenance window) and a CU budget (500 CU-minutes). It processes tables in priority order until either budget is exhausted, then logs which tables were completed and which were deferred. Deferred tables are prioritized in the next week's run. The framework ensures that the most impactful compaction always happens, even if the maintenance window cannot process all 200+ tables.
Write-Time Compaction

OPTIMIZE is a post-write cleanup operation. The complementary approach is write-time compaction: reducing the number of files created during the write operation itself. GridUnion enables optimizeWrite (Fabric's write-time coalescing) on all streaming and high-frequency append tables. For the SCADA streaming tables, optimizeWrite reduces daily file creation from 2,000+ to 30–50 — eliminating 98% of the small file problem at the source. For batch MERGE tables like silver_meters, optimizeWrite has less impact (MERGE creates fewer files to begin with), but it still reduces the file count by 20–30% compared to the default write behavior. The combination of optimizeWrite (reducing file creation) and scheduled OPTIMIZE (compacting what gets through) keeps the file count across all 200+ tables below 100,000 — down from the projected 500,000 without either mechanism.

blocks.title: At enterprise scale, maintenance is a workload, not a task
blocks.content: NordGrid's weekly OPTIMIZE was a single notebook that ran in 15 minutes against 3 tables. GridUnion's maintenance framework is a workload that consumes 3 hours and 500 CU-minutes weekly across 200+ tables with priority-based scheduling and budget management. The shift from 'run OPTIMIZE on the Silver table' to 'operate an automated compaction system with priority scoring, time budgets, and deferral logic' is the senior-level shift for table maintenance. The maintenance framework itself is a production system that needs monitoring, alerting, and incident response — when the framework fails (the maintenance notebook crashes), 200+ tables go uncompacted, and scan performance degrades across the entire platform within 2 weeks.

Partition pruning reduces scan volume by skipping directories. Z-ORDER reduces scan volume by skipping row groups. Bucketing eliminates shuffle volume by pre-distributing data at write time so that join keys are already aligned across tables. When two tables are bucketed by the same column into the same number of buckets, a join on that column requires no shuffle — bucket N of table A joins with bucket N of table B locally on the same executor. At GridUnion's scale, the Silver meter table joins with the tariff dimension table 12 times daily (once per country × 3 billing cycles). Each join shuffles 6 TB of Silver data. Bucketing the Silver table by meter_id eliminates that shuffle entirely — a savings of 72 TB of daily shuffle volume across the 12 joins.
Bucketing in Fabric's Delta Lake

Bucketing in Fabric Lakehouses uses Delta Lake's support for bucketBy() during write operations. The writer specifies the number of buckets and the bucket column; Delta distributes each row to a bucket based on the hash of the bucket column value. The bucket assignment is stored in the file naming convention — each file belongs to a specific bucket — and the Delta transaction log records the bucketing metadata. When two Delta tables have the same bucket count and bucket column, Spark's optimizer detects the alignment and plans a bucket join: each task reads the corresponding bucket from both tables and joins them locally without shuffling either side.

# Write the Silver meter table with bucketing by meter_id
# 256 buckets — chosen based on the target file size and data volume
df_silver.write \
    .format("delta") \
    .bucketBy(256, "meter_id") \
    .sortBy("meter_id") \
    .mode("overwrite") \
    .partitionBy("country", "year", "month") \
    .saveAsTable("silver_meters")

# Write the tariff dimension table with the SAME bucket count and column
df_tariffs.write \
    .format("delta") \
    .bucketBy(256, "meter_id") \
    .sortBy("meter_id") \
    .mode("overwrite") \
    .saveAsTable("ref_tariffs")

# Join — no shuffle because both tables are bucketed on meter_id with 256 buckets
df_billing = df_silver.join(df_tariffs, on="meter_id")
df_billing.explain()
# Expected: SortMergeJoin with NO Exchange operators — shuffle eliminated
Bucket Count Coordination

Shuffle-free joins require matching bucket counts across tables. If the Silver table has 256 buckets and the tariff table has 128 buckets, the optimizer cannot match buckets 1:1 and falls back to a shuffle join. GridUnion maintains a shared bucket registry in the platform workspace — a Delta table that records the bucket count and bucket column for every bucketed production table. Before a team creates a new bucketed table, they check the registry for the join partner's bucket configuration and match it. The platform team reviews the registry quarterly and has standardized on three bucket counts: 256 for large fact tables (Silver meters, Silver SCADA), 64 for medium dimension tables (tariffs, substations), and 16 for small reference tables. Tables that join frequently use the same count from the standardized set.
The Cost of Bucketing

Bucketing is not free. The write operation is slower because each row must be hashed and routed to the correct bucket file (approximately 15–20% write overhead). The file layout is rigid — 256 buckets produce at least 256 files per partition, regardless of partition size, which can create small files in low-volume partitions (Luxembourg's monthly partition gets 256 files of 10 MB each instead of the optimal 4–5 files of 500 MB). Schema evolution is constrained — changing the bucket column or bucket count requires a full table rewrite. GridUnion bucketed the Silver meter table because the join frequency (12 times daily × 6 TB shuffle × 365 days = 26 PB of annual shuffle elimination) overwhelmingly justified the costs. GridUnion did not bucket the Gold tables because they are rarely joined — the cost exceeded the benefit.

blocks.title: Bucketing is an architectural commitment, not a configuration change
blocks.content: Once a table is bucketed, every consumer that joins with it must account for the bucket scheme. Adding a new join partner requires matching the bucket count. Changing the bucket column requires rewriting the table and updating every join partner. Removing bucketing requires rewriting the table and accepting the shuffle cost that returns. GridUnion treats bucketing as an architectural decision that requires the same governance review as a schema change: impact analysis, consumer notification, and platform team approval. Three of GridUnion's 200+ tables are bucketed. The other 197 are not — because the shuffle elimination did not justify the rigidity for those tables' access patterns.

Partition schemes are designed for the current query patterns and data volume. Both change over time. GridUnion's Silver meter table was initially partitioned by year/month only (inherited from NordGrid's mid-level design). When the 4-country acquisition closed, the table needed country as a partition level for regulatory data isolation. Adding a partition column to a live production Delta table with 15 TB of data and 40 downstream consumers is one of the most operationally complex changes a senior data engineer faces. This lesson covers the three evolution scenarios — adding, splitting, and removing partition levels — and the consumer impact analysis for each.
Three Evolution Scenarios

rows: Add a partition level
New partition column added (e.g., adding country above year/month)
Existing data must be rewritten with the new partition column — 15 TB rewrite
Consumers must update their queries to include the new partition column for optimal pruning
HIGH — data rewrite, consumer query updates, pipeline modifications
Split a partition level
An existing partition becomes finer-grained (e.g., month → week)
Existing data must be rewritten to redistribute across the new granularity
Consumers filtering by month still work (week rolls up to month) but queries may need adjustment for optimal pruning
MEDIUM — data rewrite, minimal consumer impact if the coarser level still works
Remove a partition level
A partition column is removed (e.g., removing a region partition that is no longer useful)
Existing data must be rewritten to merge the removed level into the parent
Consumers filtering by the removed column lose pruning benefit — must be notified and may need Z-ORDER compensation
MEDIUM — data rewrite, consumer notification, potential performance regression for some queries

visual-recommendation.description: A three-panel diagram showing the directory tree before and after each evolution scenario: (left) adding a partition level inserts a new directory depth, (center) splitting a partition level subdivides existing directories, (right) removing a partition level merges directories upward. Each panel shows the file count and directory structure change.

blocks.content: Consumer Impact Analysis

Before executing any partition evolution, the platform team conducts a consumer impact analysis. For every registered consumer (from the data product manifest), the team evaluates: does this consumer's query pattern include the new/changed partition column? If yes, the consumer benefits from better pruning. If no, the consumer is unaffected. Does any consumer's query pattern depend on the old partition scheme in a way that breaks with the new scheme? This is rare but possible: a consumer that uses the partition directory structure directly (listing files by partition path) would break if the path changes. Does any consumer's pipeline include a hardcoded partition filter that assumes the old scheme? A pipeline with .filter(col('year') == 2025).filter(col('month') == 3) continues to work when country is added — but it does not benefit from country pruning unless the filter is updated. The impact analysis produces a consumer-by-consumer report that the governance committee reviews before approving the evolution.
The 90-Day Evolution Timeline

GridUnion's standard timeline for partition evolution is 90 days from announcement to completion. Days 1–14: announce the change to all registered consumers with the impact analysis report. Days 15–45: consumers update their queries and pipelines in their dev/staging workspaces. The platform team provides the new partition column values in a reference table so consumers can test. Days 46–60: execute the data rewrite in a parallel table (the new-scheme table coexists with the old-scheme table). Consumers validate their queries against the new table. Days 61–75: switch production to the new table. The old table remains as a read-only fallback. Days 76–90: decommission the old table after confirming all consumers have migrated. This timeline is aggressive for a 15 TB table with 40 consumers — but the 90-day regulatory deadline for GridUnion's country-partition requirement left no room for extension.

blocks.title: Partition evolution is a platform migration, not a table operation
blocks.content: Rewriting a 15 TB table is a 4-hour Spark job. Coordinating 40 consumers across 12 teams in 4 countries to update their queries, test against the new scheme, and validate their pipelines is a 90-day organizational effort. The technical complexity (data rewrite) is small compared to the organizational complexity (consumer coordination). GridUnion's partition evolution process is managed by the platform team as a project with a project plan, a consumer tracker, weekly status updates, and an escalation path for consumers who do not respond. The lesson from GridUnion's first partition evolution: announce early, provide test data early, and accept that 10% of consumers will not update until the old table is decommissioned and they have no choice.

This is the flagship lesson of the module: the full operational playbook for repartitioning a live production Delta table in Fabric. GridUnion's unified Silver meter table was partitioned by year/month. The post-acquisition regulatory requirement mandated country/year/month partitioning for data isolation. The table contained 15 TB of data, served 40 downstream consumers, and had a 90-day migration deadline. The platform team evaluated three approaches, selected one, executed it over 6 weeks, and completed the migration with zero data loss and one consumer-reported performance regression (resolved within 24 hours). This lesson presents all three approaches with their trade-offs and the execution details of GridUnion's chosen approach.
Approach 1 — Dual-Write

The dual-write approach creates a new table with the target partition scheme and writes every pipeline's output to both the old and new tables simultaneously during the migration period. Consumers migrate at their own pace — they switch their queries from the old table to the new table when ready. After all consumers have migrated, the old table is decommissioned. The advantage is zero downtime: both tables are always current, and consumers can switch at any time. The disadvantage is double write cost: every pipeline writes to two tables for the duration of the migration, doubling CU consumption for writes and doubling storage consumption. At GridUnion's scale, dual-write added 200 CU-minutes per day and 15 TB of temporary storage — significant but acceptable for a 6-week migration window.
Approach 2 — View Abstraction

The view abstraction approach places a SQL view in front of the table. Consumers query the view, not the table directly. The platform team repartitions the underlying table (rewriting all 15 TB with the new partition scheme) while the view remains stable. After the rewrite, the view is updated to point to the new table. Consumers experience a brief read disruption during the rewrite (the table is being rewritten and may be temporarily inconsistent) but do not need to change their queries — the view absorbs the schema change. The advantage is minimal consumer impact. The disadvantage is the read disruption during the rewrite (which can take 4+ hours for 15 TB) and the requirement that all consumers already access the table through the view — if any consumer references the table directly, the view abstraction is incomplete.
Approach 3 — Delta CRAS (Create or Replace As Select)

The CRAS approach uses Delta Lake's CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE AS SELECT to atomically rewrite the table with the new partition scheme. The CRAS operation reads the entire existing table, repartitions the data, and writes it as a new version of the same table. The operation is atomic: the old version remains readable until the new version is committed, then the new version becomes the current version. No consumer needs to change their query or switch tables — the table name stays the same, only the partition layout changes. The advantage is operational simplicity and atomicity. The disadvantage is the rewrite duration (4–6 hours for 15 TB), the temporary storage consumption (the old and new versions coexist until VACUUM removes the old), and the requirement that no pipeline writes to the table during the CRAS operation.
GridUnion's Decision

left.label: Dual-Write (chosen for historical data)
points: Historical data (3 years): rewrite to new-scheme table in parallel over 2 weeks
Advantage: no read disruption during the 2-week historical rewrite
Cost: 15 TB temporary storage + 200 CU-minutes/day for dual writes
Consumer impact: consumers migrate to the new table at their own pace

right.label: CRAS (chosen for ongoing writes)
points: Current month's data: CRAS after the historical migration completes
Advantage: atomic switch for the current month — consumers see the new scheme instantly
Cost: 4-hour maintenance window (Sunday 02:00–06:00 UTC) for the CRAS operation
Consumer impact: zero — table name unchanged, queries unchanged

blocks.content: GridUnion used a hybrid approach: dual-write for the 3-year historical archive (which took 2 weeks to rewrite) combined with CRAS for the final switchover of the current month's data. The hybrid approach minimized both downtime (the CRAS operated on 1 month of data, not 3 years, reducing the maintenance window from 4 hours to 40 minutes) and consumer impact (the historical data was available in the new table for testing throughout the 2-week migration, and the CRAS made the switchover atomic). The migration completed in 6 weeks with zero data loss. One consumer reported a 15% performance regression on a query that filtered by year/month without country — the regression was expected (the new partition scheme is country-first, not date-first) and was resolved by adding a country filter to the consumer's query.

blocks.title: Repartitioning is a platform event, not a maintenance task
blocks.content: GridUnion's repartitioning involved: 6 weeks of execution, 40 consumer notifications, 12 team-level coordination meetings, 4 country-level regulatory reviews, 1 governance committee approval, 15 TB of temporary storage, and approximately 3,000 CU-minutes of rewrite compute. The technical act of rewriting the data was the easiest part. The organizational act of coordinating 40 consumers across 4 countries was the hard part. GridUnion's lesson: repartition as rarely as possible. Design the partition scheme with a 24-month projection (lesson 2), survey all consumers before choosing (lesson 1), and document the rationale so that the next person who asks 'should we repartition?' can evaluate the question against the existing design rationale rather than starting from scratch.

Partition schemes degrade over time. Files accumulate between OPTIMIZE runs. Data volume growth changes the partition size distribution. New consumers query with patterns that the partition design did not anticipate. Without monitoring, degradation is invisible until a consumer reports slow queries or a pipeline breaches its SLA. GridUnion's partition monitoring system tracks file count, partition size, size skew ratio, and Z-ORDER effectiveness across all 200+ production Delta tables, alerting the owning team when any metric crosses a health threshold. This lesson covers the monitoring infrastructure, the alert thresholds, and the incident response for partition health violations.
Partition Health Metrics

rows: Files per partition
numFiles / numPartitions from DESCRIBE DETAIL
4–20 files per partition
> 100 files — OPTIMIZE not running or failing
Check maintenance framework logs; re-run OPTIMIZE manually if needed
Partition size (max)
Largest partition directory size in GB
> 15 GB — partition too large for efficient scan
Evaluate sub-partitioning or Z-ORDER improvement per lesson 2 mitigation
Size skew ratio
Max partition size / min partition size
> 10:1 — extreme size variation between partitions
Evaluate whether the skew reflects genuine data distribution or a partition design flaw
Z-ORDER effectiveness
% of row groups skipped on a filtered query (from Spark UI)
> 50% skip rate
< 30% — Z-ORDER degraded (new data not clustered)
Re-run OPTIMIZE with ZORDER BY on the affected partitions
Directory count growth
Total partition directory count vs 24-month projection
Within 20% of projection
> 30% deviation — data volume growing faster than projected
Update the sizing model; evaluate partition level addition per lesson 7

blocks.content: The Monitoring Notebook

# nb_gu_partition_health — runs daily at 05:00 UTC
# Computes partition health metrics for all production tables

from delta.tables import DeltaTable

def assess_partition_health(table_name: str, spark) -> dict:
    """Compute partition health metrics for a single table."""
    detail = spark.sql(f"DESCRIBE DETAIL {table_name}").collect()[0]
    num_files = detail.numFiles
    size_bytes = detail.sizeInBytes
    
    # Partition count and per-partition file distribution
    partitions = spark.sql(f"SHOW PARTITIONS {table_name}").collect()
    num_partitions = len(partitions)
    avg_files = num_files / max(num_partitions, 1)
    
    # Size skew: compare largest and smallest partition
    # (simplified — full implementation queries per-partition file sizes)
    size_gb = size_bytes / (1024**3)
    avg_partition_gb = size_gb / max(num_partitions, 1)
    
    return {
        "table": table_name,
        "num_files": num_files,
        "num_partitions": num_partitions,
        "avg_files_per_partition": round(avg_files, 1),
        "total_size_gb": round(size_gb, 1),
        "avg_partition_gb": round(avg_partition_gb, 1),
        "alert_file_count": avg_files > 100,
        "alert_partition_size": avg_partition_gb > 15,
    }

# Run for all production tables and write to health metrics table
for table in get_production_tables():
    health = assess_partition_health(table, spark)
    write_to_health_table(health)  # Delta table in ws_gu_platform

The monitoring notebook runs daily and writes health metrics to a partition_health table in the platform workspace. The platform team's morning dashboard includes a partition health section that shows the number of tables with alerts (target: zero). When an alert fires, the owning team is notified via the automated alerting pipeline with the specific metric that violated the threshold and the recommended action. The alert is tagged with the table's data product ID so the owning team can locate the table, its manifest, and its maintenance schedule immediately. GridUnion's partition monitoring has prevented 8 performance incidents in the past year by detecting file count growth and Z-ORDER degradation before they affected query performance.

blocks.title: Monitor the maintenance system, not just the tables
blocks.content: The maintenance framework (lesson 5) is itself a production system that can fail. If the weekly OPTIMIZE notebook crashes, no tables are compacted, and file counts grow unchecked. GridUnion monitors the maintenance framework separately: the pipeline_metrics table records every maintenance run's status, duration, tables processed, and tables deferred. If the maintenance run did not execute (status missing for Sunday) or processed fewer tables than expected (deferred count > 30% of total), the platform team receives a high-priority alert. The meta-monitoring — monitoring the system that monitors the tables — closes the gap where the monitoring system itself is the single point of failure.

The previous nine lessons covered partition design for multi-consumer tables with consumer surveys and scoring matrices, partition sizing with 24-month projections and mitigation strategies, Z-ORDER for data skipping within partitions, dynamic partition pruning internals and the conditions that block DPP activation, automated small file compaction with priority scoring and time budgets, bucketing for shuffle elimination with cross-table coordination, partition evolution strategies with 90-day migration timelines, the repartitioning playbook with the dual-write/CRAS hybrid approach, and partition monitoring with health metrics and alert-driven maintenance. This lesson synthesizes everything into GridUnion's partition governance framework.
The Partition Design Review Process

items: Consumer survey: before choosing partition columns, survey all registered consumers for their query patterns, filter columns, and query frequency. The survey results drive the partition column scoring matrix.

Sizing projection: model the partition directory count and per-partition size for 24 months forward using current growth rates and planned data source additions. Flag any partition that exceeds the 15 GB or 5,000-directory threshold.

Z-ORDER evaluation: for columns that scored below the partition threshold but above the ignore threshold, evaluate Z-ORDER effectiveness by running test queries with and without Z-ORDER on a staging copy of the table.
Bucketing evaluation: for tables with high-frequency joins on a consistent key, evaluate bucketing by computing the annual shuffle savings vs the write overhead and schema rigidity cost.

Architecture review: present the proposed partition scheme, the sizing projection, the Z-ORDER plan, and the bucketing decision to the governance committee for approval. The review includes the consumer impact analysis and the migration plan if the table already exists with a different scheme.

Certification: the approved partition scheme is recorded in the data product manifest as a mandatory field. The 24-month projection is appended as a supporting document. The quarterly partition audit validates compliance.

blocks.content: The Quarterly Partition Audit

Every quarter, the platform team audits partition health across all production tables. The audit checks five items: file count per partition (are any tables above the 100-file alert threshold?), partition size distribution (has any partition exceeded the 15 GB threshold?), Z-ORDER effectiveness (has Z-ORDER degraded below the 50% skip-rate target for any table?), sizing projection accuracy (has actual growth deviated from the model by more than 30%?), and consumer survey currency (have any new consumers been added since the last survey?). The audit produces a report with three categories: compliant (no action needed), attention (one or more metrics are trending toward thresholds — schedule proactive maintenance), and non-compliant (one or more metrics exceed thresholds — escalate to owning team for immediate action). The audit report is presented to the governance committee alongside the capacity and security reports.
What Comes Next

Module 4 covers advanced join architecture — the join strategies that operate on the partitioned and bucketed tables this module designed. Module 3 taught you to optimize the data layout (partitioning, Z-ORDER, bucketing) that determines I/O volume. Module 4 teaches you to optimize the join operations (bucketed joins, range joins, join elimination, semi-join pushdown) that determine shuffle volume. Together, they cover the two dominant cost drivers in GridUnion's pipelines: I/O (how much data Spark reads from OneLake) and shuffle (how much data Spark moves between executors). Optimizing both is the senior-level performance discipline.

blocks.title: Partition governance prevents the most expensive platform migrations
blocks.content: Repartitioning a 15 TB table cost GridUnion 6 weeks of organizational effort, 3,000 CU-minutes of compute, and 15 TB of temporary storage. The partition governance framework — consumer surveys, sizing projections, quarterly audits, and architecture reviews — exists to prevent repartitioning by getting the design right the first time and detecting degradation before it becomes critical. Every hour spent on the upfront design review saves 10 hours of repartitioning effort later. GridUnion has repartitioned one table in 18 months of operation. The 199 other tables have not needed repartitioning because the governance process ensured their initial designs were sound and the monitoring system caught degradation early.

chapters.title: Advanced Join Architecture
chapters.description: Design join strategies for multi-terabyte Fabric pipelines with complex topologies — bucketed joins, range joins, cross-database joins at enterprise scale, join elimination, and the architectural decisions that determine whether a multi-hop enrichment pipeline takes 10 minutes or 10 hours.

blocks.content: The mid-level track taught join mechanics: broadcast for small tables, sort-merge for large tables, salting for skew. At senior level, the concern shifts from individual joins to join topologies — the graph of tables and join relationships that compose a pipeline. GridUnion's consolidated billing pipeline joins 8 tables in a chain: Silver meters → tariff lookup → substation enrichment → region aggregation → country consolidation → regulatory format → billing output → validation cross-check. Each join has its own strategy, its own shuffle cost, and its own skew profile. Optimizing one join in isolation can degrade another — broadcasting the tariff table reduces join 2's shuffle but increases executor memory pressure, which causes join 4 to spill. Join architecture is the practice of optimizing the entire topology, not individual join operators.
Topology Patterns

rows: Star join
One large fact table joined to multiple small dimensions
3–8 joins, all from the same fact table
Scan of the fact table (repeated for each join unless optimized)
Silver meters joined with region, substation, customer_type, and tariff dimension tables
Snowflake join
Star join where dimension tables are themselves joined to sub-dimensions
8–15 joins across 3+ levels of nesting
Intermediate result accumulation — each level adds columns and rows
Silver meters → substations → grid zones → country settings → regulatory codes
Chain join
Tables joined sequentially — output of join N feeds join N+1
4–10 joins in series
Shuffle accumulation — each join shuffles the growing intermediate result
The 8-table billing pipeline where each join adds an enrichment column
Fan-in join
Multiple source tables joined into one result
3–6 joins, often via UNION + deduplicate
Scan volume — all source tables are read, combined, and deduplicated
Platinum consolidation: 4 country-level Gold tables unioned and joined with cross-country dimensions
Hybrid
Combination of star, chain, and fan-in patterns
10+ joins across multiple subgraphs
Varies — the dominant cost depends on which subgraph is largest
The quarterly regulatory pipeline: fan-in of 4 countries, star join with 6 dimensions, chain through 3 formatting stages

blocks.content: Analyzing a Topology

Before optimizing any individual join, map the entire topology. GridUnion's platform team draws the join graph for every pipeline with more than 3 joins: each table is a node, each join is an edge labeled with the join key, the join strategy, and the estimated shuffle volume. The graph reveals the critical path — the sequence of joins that determines the pipeline's total runtime. In the billing pipeline's 8-join chain, joins 3–5 account for 70% of the total runtime because they shuffle the largest intermediate results. Optimizing joins 1–2 (which are already broadcast joins on small dimensions) has negligible impact. The topology analysis directs optimization effort to the joins that matter.

The topology also reveals optimization opportunities that individual join analysis misses. If joins 3 and 5 both shuffle by region, they can potentially be combined into a single shuffle stage — the intermediate result between them is already partitioned by region, so join 5 can operate on the existing partition without re-shuffling. This co-partitioning optimization is invisible when each join is analyzed in isolation but obvious in the topology graph. Module 2's plan fingerprinting technique applies here: capture the topology's plan structure and alert when a code change alters the shuffle pattern across the join chain.

blocks.title: Optimize the topology, not the join
blocks.content: A common senior-level anti-pattern is join-by-join optimization: tune join 1, measure improvement, tune join 2, measure improvement, and declare victory when each join is individually fast. The problem is that local optima do not guarantee a global optimum. Broadcasting table A makes join 1 faster but increases memory pressure that causes join 4 to spill. The senior approach: map the topology, identify the critical path, measure the end-to-end pipeline duration, and optimize the critical path's dominant cost — shuffle volume, scan volume, or memory pressure. A 20% improvement on the critical path matters more than a 50% improvement on a non-critical join.

Module 3 covered bucketing as a write-time optimization that pre-distributes data for shuffle-free joins. This lesson addresses the enterprise adoption challenges: coordinating bucket counts across 12 teams that independently manage their tables, maintaining bucket alignment during schema evolution, handling the interaction between bucketing and partitioning (country/year/month partitions with 256 buckets per partition), and the governance process that ensures two tables intended for a bucketed join actually have compatible bucket configurations. At GridUnion's scale, bucketing's shuffle elimination saves 72 TB of daily shuffle — but only if the bucket registry is maintained and every team follows the bucket alignment conventions.
The Bucket Registry

GridUnion's bucket registry is a Delta table in ws_gu_platform that records every bucketed table's configuration: table name, workspace, bucket column, bucket count, sort column, and the list of join partners that depend on the bucket alignment. The registry serves two purposes. First, it is the lookup that a team checks before creating a new bucketed table — they find the join partner's configuration and match it. Second, it is the validation source for the automated bucket compatibility check that runs as part of the CI/CD pipeline: when a notebook that writes a bucketed table is deployed, the deployment gate verifies that the bucket configuration matches the registry. A deployment that changes the bucket count without updating the registry and notifying join partners is rejected.

# GridUnion's bucket compatibility check — runs in the CI/CD deployment gate

def verify_bucket_compatibility(table_name: str, bucket_col: str,
                                 bucket_count: int, spark) -> bool:
    """Verify that a table's bucket config matches its registered join partners."""
    registry = spark.read.table("gu_bucket_registry")
    
    # Find all registered join partners for this table
    partners = registry.filter(
        col("join_partners").contains(table_name)
    ).collect()
    
    compatible = True
    for partner in partners:
        if partner.bucket_column != bucket_col:
            print(f"INCOMPATIBLE: {partner.table_name} uses bucket column "
                  f"'{partner.bucket_column}', but {table_name} uses '{bucket_col}'")
            compatible = False
        if partner.bucket_count != bucket_count:
            print(f"INCOMPATIBLE: {partner.table_name} has {partner.bucket_count} "
                  f"buckets, but {table_name} has {bucket_count}")
            compatible = False
    
    if compatible:
        print(f"Bucket config for {table_name} is compatible with all partners")
    return compatible
Bucketing and Partitioning Interaction

GridUnion's Silver meter table is both partitioned (by country/year/month) and bucketed (by meter_id, 256 buckets). This means each partition directory contains 256 bucket files — and each bucket file contains only the rows for that bucket within that partition. The total file count is partitions × buckets: 144 partitions × 256 buckets = 36,864 files. This is within OneLake's metadata overhead threshold but creates a tension with the small files concern from Module 3: Luxembourg's monthly partition has 256 files of 10 MB each (2.5 GB / 256 = ~10 MB per file), well below the optimal 256 MB–1 GB file size. The trade-off: bucketing eliminates 6 TB of daily shuffle at the cost of small files in low-volume partitions. GridUnion accepts the trade-off because the shuffle savings exceed the small-file scan overhead by two orders of magnitude.
Bucket Count Selection

The optimal bucket count depends on the largest partition's size and the target file size. GridUnion's German monthly partition is 10.1 GB. At 256 buckets, each file is approximately 40 MB — below the optimal 256 MB but above the critical 10 MB threshold. At 64 buckets, each file would be approximately 160 MB — closer to optimal but with fewer parallelism options for the join. At 1024 buckets, each file would be 10 MB — too small for efficient I/O. GridUnion chose 256 as the standard bucket count because it provides sufficient join parallelism (256 parallel join tasks) while keeping file sizes above 30 MB for all countries. The standardization matters: all tables that join with the Silver meter table must use 256 buckets. A table with 128 buckets cannot join shuffle-free with a 256-bucket table.

blocks.title: Bucket count changes require a coordinated rewrite
blocks.content: If GridUnion's data volume growth pushes the German monthly partition to 20 GB in 2 years, each 256-bucket file will be 80 MB — still acceptable. But if the team decides to increase to 512 buckets for better join parallelism, every join partner table must also be rewritten with 512 buckets simultaneously, or the shuffle-free join breaks. This is the same coordinated migration problem as repartitioning (Module 3, lesson 8), but applied to bucketing. GridUnion's bucket count selection accounts for 24-month growth projections (the same model from Module 3, lesson 2) to minimize the probability of needing a bucket count change during the projection window.

Equi-joins (WHERE a.key = b.key) can be optimized with hash partitioning, broadcast, and bucketing because equal keys land in the same partition. Range joins (WHERE a.event_time BETWEEN b.start_time AND b.end_time) cannot — there is no hash function that maps overlapping ranges to the same partition. Every row in table A must potentially compare against every row in table B whose range encompasses it. Without optimization, a range join between 5 million meter events and 500,000 tariff validity periods produces a 2.5 trillion-row comparison — a nested-loop join that takes hours.
