This lesson covers the three optimization strategies for range joins and GridUnion's production implementation for the tariff point-in-time lookup.
The Tariff Lookup Problem

GridUnion's billing pipeline must determine the applicable tariff rate for each meter reading. Tariffs change over time: a meter had rate A from 2023-01-01 to 2024-06-30, rate B from 2024-07-01 to 2025-03-31, and rate C from 2025-04-01 onward. The join condition is: meter_readings.date BETWEEN tariff_periods.start_date AND tariff_periods.end_date AND meter_readings.meter_id = tariff_periods.meter_id. The equi-join on meter_id narrows the comparison, but the range condition on date prevents hash partitioning on the date column. At 5 million daily readings and 15 million tariff period records (5M meters × 3 periods average), the naive range join is prohibitively expensive.
Strategy 1 — Broadcast Range Join

If the range table (tariff periods) is small enough to broadcast, Spark sends it to every executor and each executor evaluates the range condition locally against its partition of the large table. This eliminates the shuffle entirely — only the small table is broadcast, and the range comparison is a local nested-loop within each executor. GridUnion's tariff period table has 15 million rows (75 MB compressed) — above the default 10 MB broadcast threshold but within the 30 MB threshold that GridUnion configured. However, 75 MB exceeds even the elevated threshold. The solution: pre-filter the tariff table to the current billing period (the most recent tariff per meter) before the join, reducing it to 5 million rows (25 MB). The pre-filtered table fits within the broadcast threshold, and the range join becomes a broadcast range join with no shuffle.
Strategy 2 — Bin-Based Approximation

from pyspark.sql.functions import floor, col, lit

# Bin-based range join optimization for SCD Type 2 lookups
# Convert the range condition to an equi-join on a binned time column

# Step 1: Bin the meter readings by month
df_readings = df_readings.withColumn(
    "date_bin", floor(datediff(col("date"), lit("2020-01-01")) / 30)
)

# Step 2: Explode the tariff periods into bins that cover their date range
# Each tariff period spanning 18 months generates 18 bin rows
from pyspark.sql.functions import explode, sequence, expr
df_tariffs_binned = df_tariffs.withColumn(
    "date_bin",
    explode(sequence(
        floor(datediff(col("start_date"), lit("2020-01-01")) / 30),
        floor(datediff(col("end_date"), lit("2020-01-01")) / 30)
    ))
)

# Step 3: Equi-join on meter_id + date_bin (hash-partitionable)
# Then filter by the exact date range condition
df_joined = df_readings.join(
    df_tariffs_binned,
    on=["meter_id", "date_bin"]
).filter(
    (col("date") >= col("start_date")) & (col("date") <= col("end_date"))
).drop("date_bin")

The bin-based approach converts the range condition into an approximate equi-join on a binned column, then applies the exact range filter as a post-join condition. The equi-join on (meter_id, date_bin) can use hash partitioning — both sides shuffle by the same composite key. The post-join filter removes the false positives (rows where the bin matched but the exact date did not). The trade-off is the explosion factor: each tariff period spanning N bins generates N rows in the exploded table. A tariff period of 18 months generates 18 bin rows. The total tariff row count expands from 15 million to approximately 45 million (3x average). This expansion is acceptable because the equi-join with hash partitioning is far cheaper than a nested-loop range join, even with the 3x explosion.
Strategy 3 — Pre-Materialize the Lookup

The most radical optimization: eliminate the range join entirely by pre-materializing the tariff assignment. A nightly notebook evaluates every meter's applicable tariff as of today's date and writes a simple meter_id → tariff_code mapping table (5 million rows, one per meter). The billing pipeline joins meter readings with this flat mapping table using a standard equi-join on meter_id — no range condition needed. The pre-materialization notebook runs once nightly (3 minutes, handles the range logic), and every subsequent join is a fast equi-join. GridUnion uses this approach for the daily billing pipeline (which always looks up today's tariff) and the bin-based approach for the quarterly regulatory pipeline (which must look up historical tariffs across the full date range).

blocks.title: Match the strategy to the query pattern, not the data shape
blocks.content: All three strategies work for range joins. The choice depends on the query pattern. If the range table is small enough to broadcast, broadcast is simplest and fastest (no code change, no pre-materialization). If the range spans many bins and the query is historical (spanning months or years), bin-based approximation handles arbitrary date ranges efficiently. If the query always looks up the current value (today's tariff, the current SCD Type 2 record), pre-materialization eliminates the range join entirely and converts it to the cheapest possible equi-join. GridUnion uses all three strategies in different pipelines — the strategy follows the access pattern.

Join elimination is a Catalyst optimization that removes joins that provably do not affect the query result. When a join adds no columns to the output (the SELECT references only columns from one side), does not filter rows (the join is an inner join on a foreign key with a referential integrity guarantee), and does not duplicate rows (the join key is unique on the joined side), Catalyst can eliminate the join entirely — reading only from the side that contributes columns. The query runs as if the join does not exist, scanning one table instead of two and eliminating the shuffle entirely. At GridUnion's scale, join elimination reduced the 7-table enterprise Warehouse reporting view to 3 tables — cutting query time from 12 seconds to 2 seconds.
Conditions for Join Elimination

rows: No columns selected from the joined table
If columns from the joined table appear in the output, the join is needed to produce those values
Check the SELECT list — does it reference any column from the joined table?
A SELECT * that includes all columns from both sides prevents elimination
Join key is unique on the joined side
If the join key is not unique, the join may duplicate rows from the other side — eliminating it changes the row count
Check for PRIMARY KEY or UNIQUE constraint on the join key column
Dimension tables without declared uniqueness constraints are not eligible
Join is inner or left outer (from the preserved side)
A right outer or full outer join can add null rows that affect the result
Check the join type in the plan
Using LEFT JOIN instead of INNER when the dimension is guaranteed to match
No filter on the joined table
A filter on the joined table restricts which rows survive — eliminating the join removes the filter's effect
Check for any WHERE condition referencing the joined table's columns
A WHERE d.region_name = 'North' on a dimension table prevents elimination even if no dimension columns are selected

visual-recommendation.description: A before/after plan diagram showing a 7-table join query on the left (with all 7 tables scanned and 6 join operators) and the optimized 3-table query on the right (with 4 tables eliminated and only 2 join operators remaining). Eliminated tables are shown as grayed-out nodes with strike-through annotations explaining which condition enabled elimination.

blocks.content: Enabling Join Elimination in Fabric

Join elimination requires Catalyst to know that the join key is unique on the joined side. In standalone Spark, this knowledge comes from PRIMARY KEY and UNIQUE constraints declared on the table. In Fabric's Delta Lake, these constraints are informational — Delta does not enforce them, but Catalyst uses them for optimization if they are declared. GridUnion declares PRIMARY KEY constraints on all dimension tables (dim_regions, dim_substations, dim_tariff_codes) so that joins involving these tables are eligible for elimination. The constraint declaration is a metadata-only operation that does not affect write performance or storage — it simply informs the optimizer that the column is unique, enabling the elimination rule to fire.

The enterprise Warehouse reporting view originally joined 7 tables: the fact table with 6 dimension tables. The view's SELECT list only needed columns from the fact table and 2 of the 6 dimensions. After PRIMARY KEY constraints were declared on all 6 dimensions, Catalyst eliminated the 4 joins whose dimensions contributed no columns to the output. The plan dropped from 6 join operators to 2, eliminating 4 shuffles. The query time dropped from 12 seconds to 2 seconds. GridUnion's platform team now declares PRIMARY KEY constraints on every dimension table as part of the data product certification process — the constraint declaration is a one-line DDL that enables significant optimization opportunities across all queries that join with the dimension.

blocks.title: Constraints are metadata for the optimizer, not enforcement for the writer
blocks.content: Delta Lake's PRIMARY KEY and UNIQUE constraints are informational — they declare intent but do not prevent duplicate inserts. The responsibility for uniqueness enforcement remains with the pipeline (deduplication in the transformation logic, MERGE with proper match conditions). The constraints exist to inform the optimizer, not to guard the data. This separation confuses engineers from RDBMS backgrounds where constraints both inform and enforce. At GridUnion, the data quality framework (Module 5's contract tests) enforces uniqueness through automated assertions, and the declared constraints enable the optimizer to exploit the guaranteed uniqueness for join elimination, predicate inference, and cardinality estimation.

Dynamic Partition Pruning (Module 3, lesson 4) pushes a runtime filter to the partition level — skipping entire directories. Semi-join pushdown extends this idea to the row-group level within files: a Bloom filter built from the small side of a join is pushed to the large side's scan, enabling Parquet row-group skipping for rows that definitely do not match the join key. The Bloom filter is a probabilistic data structure: it can definitively say 'this row group does NOT contain any matching keys' (skip it) but may sometimes say 'this row group MIGHT contain matching keys' (read it — with possible false positives). At GridUnion's scale, Bloom filter pushdown on the SCADA pipeline reduced the Silver table scan from 6 TB to 1.2 TB — an 80% reduction in I/O that DPP alone could not achieve because the join key (turbine_id) is not a partition column.
How Bloom Filter Pushdown Works

When Spark plans a join where one side is significantly smaller than the other, and the runtime filter feature is enabled (spark.sql.optimizer.runtime.bloomFilter.enabled), Spark builds a Bloom filter from the small side's join key values after the small side is fully scanned. The Bloom filter is then broadcast to the executors responsible for scanning the large side. During the large side's scan, each Parquet row group's min/max statistics and the Bloom filter are evaluated together: if the Bloom filter indicates that no keys in the row group match any value in the small side, the row group is skipped entirely. The combination of Parquet statistics (coarse-grained, always available) and Bloom filter (fine-grained, built at runtime) provides a more precise skip decision than either mechanism alone.
Configuration and Trade-Offs

rows: spark.sql.optimizer.runtime.bloomFilter.enabled
false
true
Enables Bloom filter pushdown — adds the filter build cost (seconds) but saves scan volume (minutes)
spark.sql.optimizer.runtime.bloomFilter.creationSideThreshold
10MB
50MB
Maximum size of the small side for Bloom filter creation — larger threshold enables Bloom filters on bigger dimension tables
spark.sql.optimizer.runtime.bloomFilter.expectedNumItems
Expected number of distinct keys in the filter — affects Bloom filter size and false positive rate
spark.sql.optimizer.runtime.bloomFilter.maxNumBits
Maximum Bloom filter size — larger filters have lower false positive rates but higher broadcast cost

visual-recommendation.description: A data flow diagram showing the Bloom filter pushdown sequence: (1) small table scanned and Bloom filter built from join keys, (2) Bloom filter broadcast to all executors, (3) large table scan evaluates each row group against the Bloom filter, skipping non-matching groups. Annotated with scan volumes at each stage: 6TB total → 1.2TB after Bloom filter skip.

blocks.content: The SCADA Pipeline Optimization

# Enable Bloom filter pushdown for the SCADA enrichment join
# The join: Silver SCADA telemetry (6TB) joined with active turbine list (5000 rows)
# Without Bloom filter: scans all 6TB, joins, filters to active turbines
# With Bloom filter: builds filter from 5000 turbine_ids, pushes to SCADA scan,
#   skips row groups that contain only inactive turbine data

spark.conf.set("spark.sql.optimizer.runtime.bloomFilter.enabled", "true")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.optimizer.runtime.bloomFilter.creationSideThreshold", "50MB")

df_active_turbines = spark.read.table("ref_active_turbines")  # 5000 rows
df_scada = spark.read.table("silver_scada")                   # 6TB

df_enriched = df_scada.join(df_active_turbines, on="turbine_id")
df_enriched.explain()
# Plan shows: BloomFilterMightContain pushed to the SCADA table scan
# Spark UI: Input size = 1.2 TB (vs 6 TB without Bloom filter)

The 80% scan reduction occurred because GridUnion's SCADA table is Z-ORDERed by turbine_id (Module 3, lesson 3). Z-ORDER clusters the data so that each row group contains a narrow range of turbine_ids. The Bloom filter efficiently skips row groups whose turbine_id range does not intersect with the active turbine list. Without Z-ORDER, the data would be randomly distributed, each row group would contain a mix of active and inactive turbines, and the Bloom filter would rarely be able to skip an entire row group. The combination of Z-ORDER (clustering data for tight row-group statistics) and Bloom filter pushdown (exploiting those statistics at runtime) is greater than either optimization alone — Z-ORDER provides the structure, Bloom filter provides the runtime precision.

blocks.title: Bloom filter pushdown compounds with Z-ORDER
blocks.content: Bloom filter pushdown's effectiveness depends on data clustering. On randomly-distributed data, false positives are high — most row groups contain a mix of matching and non-matching keys, so the Bloom filter cannot skip them. On Z-ORDERed data, each row group contains a narrow range of key values, so the Bloom filter can definitively skip groups whose range does not overlap with the filter. GridUnion enables Bloom filter pushdown on all tables that are Z-ORDERed by the join key — and only on those tables. Enabling it on non-Z-ORDERed tables adds the filter build and broadcast overhead without meaningful skip improvement. The combination is the optimization; neither alone achieves the 80% reduction.

The mid-level track taught reactive skew handling: detect skew in the Spark UI, apply salting or enable AQE skew joining. At senior level, the approach shifts from reactive to proactive: design the join architecture so that skew is eliminated at the source rather than mitigated at join time. GridUnion's dominant skew pattern is country-level: Germany contains 40% of all meters, France 30%, Netherlands 20%, and Luxembourg 10%. Every join that partitions by country produces a German partition that is 4x larger than Luxembourg's. AQE handles this at runtime by splitting the German partition, but the splitting adds overhead that a better join design could avoid entirely.
Strategy 1 — Key Redesign

The simplest skew elimination: change the join key to one with more uniform distribution. Instead of joining on country (4 values with 40/30/20/10 distribution), join on region (30 values with approximately 3% each — still not perfectly uniform, but 3x less skewed than country). This requires both tables to have the region column, which they do in GridUnion's schema. The key redesign changes the shuffle distribution from 4 partitions (with a 4:1 skew ratio) to 30 partitions (with a 2:1 skew ratio at most). The 2:1 ratio is within AQE's comfortable handling range and does not require splitting. GridUnion applies this strategy when the pipeline's join logic does not require country-level grouping — if the downstream aggregation is by region, joining by region is both semantically correct and skew-free.
Strategy 2 — Pre-Aggregation

When the skewed key (country) is semantically required — the billing pipeline must group by country for invoice generation — key redesign is not an option. Pre-aggregation reduces the skewed side before the join: instead of joining 2 million German meter readings with the tariff table, aggregate the readings to a per-tariff-code summary (200 rows for Germany) and join the summary. The join now operates on 200 rows instead of 2 million — a 10,000x reduction that eliminates the skew entirely because 200 rows are trivially broadcast. The trade-off: pre-aggregation loses row-level detail. If the downstream logic needs individual meter readings (for example, to identify the top-10 consuming meters), pre-aggregation is not applicable. GridUnion uses pre-aggregation in the billing pipeline (which needs tariff-level totals, not meter-level detail) and individual-row joins in the analytics pipeline (which needs meter-level detail for ranking).
Strategy 3 — Composite Key with Salt Prefix

# Composite key strategy: add a salt prefix to distribute the skewed key
# This is a last resort when key redesign and pre-aggregation are not applicable

from pyspark.sql.functions import concat, lit, floor, rand

SALT_FACTOR = 8  # Split the German partition into 8 sub-partitions

# Add salt to the large (skewed) table
df_meters_salted = df_meters.withColumn(
    "join_key", concat(col("country"), lit("_"), (floor(rand() * SALT_FACTOR)).cast("string"))
)

# Explode the small table to match all salt values
from pyspark.sql.functions import explode, array
df_tariffs_exploded = df_tariffs.withColumn(
    "join_key",
    explode(array([concat(col("country"), lit(f"_{i}")) for i in range(SALT_FACTOR)]))
)

# Join on the salted composite key — uniform distribution
df_joined = df_meters_salted.join(df_tariffs_exploded, on="join_key")

# The German partition (40% of data) is now 8 sub-partitions of 5% each
# Skew ratio: max 5% / min 2.5% (Luxembourg/8) = 2:1 — manageable

The salting strategy is the most general — it works for any skew pattern without changing semantics or losing detail. But it has costs: the small table is exploded by SALT_FACTOR (8x in GridUnion's case), increasing its size and the broadcast/shuffle volume. The salt column must be dropped after the join to avoid downstream schema pollution. And the salt factor must be tuned: too low (2x) and the German partition is still skewed; too high (64x) and the small table explosion creates excessive overhead. GridUnion's salt factor of 8 was determined empirically: it reduces the maximum partition share from 40% to 5%, bringing the skew ratio below 2:1. The team tested factors of 4, 8, and 16 — factor 8 produced the best end-to-end pipeline duration.

blocks.title: Proactive skew design is cheaper than reactive skew mitigation
blocks.content: AQE's skew splitting handles the German partition problem at runtime — it detects the oversized partition, splits it, replicates the other side, and completes the join. This works but has a cost: the splitting and replication consume CUs, and the detection happens after the shuffle has already produced the skewed partition. Proactive design (key redesign, pre-aggregation, or salting built into the pipeline) eliminates the skew before the shuffle, saving both the shuffle overhead and the AQE splitting overhead. GridUnion's billing pipeline migration from reactive AQE skew handling to proactive pre-aggregation reduced the join stage from 8 minutes to 45 seconds — the pre-aggregation itself takes 20 seconds, making the net savings 7 minutes per run.

GridUnion's consolidated billing pipeline chains 8 joins. The naive implementation — a single DataFrame expression that chains all 8 joins — produces a physical plan with 7 shuffle stages where each stage shuffles the cumulative result of all preceding joins. By join 5, the intermediate result has accumulated columns from 5 tables and its size exceeds the input fact table by 3x. By join 8, the intermediate result is 5x the input. Each subsequent shuffle moves more data than the previous one because the intermediate grows with every join. The total shuffle volume across all 8 joins is 42 TB — far more than the 6 TB input fact table would suggest. Multi-hop join optimization reduces this accumulated shuffle cost.
The Materialization Boundary

The key optimization for multi-hop joins is the materialization boundary: writing an intermediate result to a Delta table before continuing the join chain. Instead of chaining all 8 joins in memory, GridUnion splits the chain into two phases. Phase 1 (joins 1–4): join the fact table with the first 4 dimensions, select only the columns needed for phase 2, and write the intermediate result to a temporary Delta table. Phase 2 (joins 5–8): read the intermediate table and join with the remaining 4 dimensions. The materialization boundary provides three benefits. First, the column pruning between phases drops accumulated columns that phase 2 does not need — reducing the intermediate from 35 columns to 12. Second, the write operation coalesces the data into optimally-sized files. Third, the phase 2 plan is simpler (4 joins instead of 8), giving Catalyst a better chance of finding an optimal plan.
Where to Place the Boundary

The materialization boundary should be placed where the intermediate result is largest and the column pruning opportunity is greatest. In GridUnion's 8-join chain, the intermediate result peaks at join 4 (35 columns, 6 TB). Joins 5–8 add only 5 columns but phase 2 only needs 12 of the 35. Materializing after join 4 and selecting only 12 columns reduces the phase 2 input from 6 TB / 35 columns to 2 TB / 12 columns — a 3x reduction that compounds through the remaining 4 shuffles. GridUnion's empirical result: inserting the materialization boundary between joins 4 and 5 reduced the total pipeline duration from 45 minutes to 20 minutes — a 55% improvement. The materialization write itself took 3 minutes, making the net savings 22 minutes.
Co-Partition Exploitation

When consecutive joins use the same join key (or a subset), the data is already partitioned from the first join's shuffle and does not need re-shuffling for the second join. In GridUnion's chain, joins 3 and 5 both use region as the join key. If the intermediate result after join 3 is still partitioned by region (which it is, unless another join between them repartitions by a different key), join 5 can be executed without a shuffle — the data is already in the right partitions. Catalyst sometimes detects this optimization automatically (it sees that the existing partition matches the required partition), but not always — especially after a materialization boundary that produces a new table with default partitioning. GridUnion's optimization: when writing the intermediate table at the materialization boundary, repartition by the join key that joins 5–8 will use. This preserves the co-partition across the boundary and eliminates the join 5 shuffle.

blocks.title: The materialization boundary is an architectural decision, not a Spark tuning knob
blocks.content: No Spark configuration property inserts a materialization boundary. It is a code-level design decision: the engineer decides where to split the join chain, which columns to carry forward, and which join key to repartition by. This decision requires understanding the full join topology (lesson 1), the column usage of each phase, and the shuffle cost at each join. GridUnion's platform team reviews materialization boundary placement as part of the join architecture review for every pipeline with more than 5 joins. The review checklist includes: where is the intermediate result largest? Which columns can be pruned? Is there a co-partition opportunity? The answers determine the boundary placement.

The mid-level track introduced cross-database joins between the Lakehouse and the Warehouse using three-part names. At senior level, cross-database joins become cross-workspace and cross-country: GridUnion's Platinum consolidation pipeline joins German Silver data (in ws_de_silver) with French Gold data (in ws_fr_gold) with the cross-country dimension table (in ws_gu_warehouse). Each source lives in a different workspace, potentially on a different capacity, and the join must traverse OneLake's storage layer to read data from remote workspaces. The performance implications of cross-workspace joins are significant at 15 TB scale, and the security implications are complex when data crosses country boundaries.
Cross-Workspace Join via OneLake Shortcuts

The preferred mechanism for cross-workspace data access is the OneLake shortcut (Module 1, lesson 3). A shortcut in the Platinum workspace points to the German Silver Lakehouse, and a Spark notebook in the Platinum workspace reads through the shortcut as if it were a local table. The join operates on the shortcut data with the same Spark operators as a local join — the cross-workspace nature is transparent to the join logic. However, the read performance depends on whether the source and destination are in the same Azure region. Same-region shortcuts (German Silver in EU North accessed from the Platinum workspace in EU North) add negligible overhead. Cross-region shortcuts (French Silver in EU West accessed from the Platinum workspace in EU North) add the 3x latency penalty identified in Module 1.
Cross-Workspace Join via Warehouse

An alternative to shortcut-based joins is routing cross-workspace queries through the Warehouse's cross-database query capability. A SQL view in the Gold Warehouse references tables in multiple Lakehouses using three-part names, and the Warehouse engine executes the join. This approach has different performance characteristics than the Spark-based shortcut approach: the Warehouse engine may use different join strategies, the query benefits from Warehouse-specific optimizations (materialized views, result caching), and the security is enforced at the Warehouse level (RLS, column masking) rather than at the OneLake level. GridUnion uses Warehouse-based joins for consumer-facing queries (analyst dashboards, Power BI reports) and shortcut-based joins for pipeline-to-pipeline data flows (the Platinum consolidation notebook).
Federated Joins with External Systems

GridUnion's billing pipeline needs data from the SAP HANA ERP system that is not replicated to Fabric. The Warehouse supports JDBC-based federated queries that push predicates to the external system and return the result to the Warehouse for joining with Fabric-native data. The federated query reads the tariff master data from SAP HANA with a WHERE clause that is pushed to HANA's query engine (reducing the data transfer to the matching rows), then joins the result with the Gold billing table in the Warehouse. The performance depends on the external system's response time, the network latency, and the amount of data transferred. GridUnion caches the SAP HANA result in a staging table (refreshed nightly) to avoid the federated query overhead during business-hours analyst queries — the federated query runs once at 02:30 UTC, and all subsequent access reads the local cache.

blocks.title: Cross-workspace joins bypass the writer's capacity budget
blocks.content: When a Spark notebook in the Platinum workspace reads 6 TB of German Silver data through a shortcut, the CU consumption is charged to the Platinum workspace's capacity — not to the German Silver workspace's capacity. The German team does not see this consumption in their capacity metrics because the read happens through OneLake, not through the German workspace's Spark engine. This creates a blind spot in cost allocation: the Platinum team's cross-workspace reads consume CUs that the German team's capacity budget does not account for. GridUnion's cost allocation model (Module 1, lesson 8) tracks cross-workspace read volumes separately and attributes the CU cost to the reading workspace, not the owning workspace — ensuring that the team that initiates the read bears the capacity cost.

GridUnion's quarterly regulatory report joins the 3-year Silver archive (15 TB) with 6 dimension tables and applies country-specific formatting for submission to the EU energy authority. This join is the most expensive operation in GridUnion's pipeline estate: 15 TB of input, 6 join stages, 4 countries, 40+ downstream validation checks. The quarterly report runs once every 90 days and takes 4 hours on the F128 capacity. Reducing it to under 2 hours is a business priority because the regulatory submission deadline is 5 days after quarter-end, and the report requires 2 rounds of human review after the pipeline completes. Four hours of pipeline time leaves inadequate margin for review and correction.
Memory Planning for Sort-Merge at Scale

The 15 TB join uses sort-merge because both sides exceed any practical broadcast threshold. Each partition in the sort-merge join must be sorted in memory. With 256 shuffle partitions, each partition holds approximately 60 GB (15 TB / 256). Spark's executor memory must accommodate the sort buffer, the merge buffer, and the output buffer for a 60 GB partition — far exceeding any single executor's memory allocation. When the data exceeds memory, Spark spills to disk: it sorts chunks that fit in memory, writes them to disk, then merges the sorted chunks. Spill is the dominant cost for large sort-merge joins — the spill-to-disk and merge-from-disk operations are 10–100x slower than in-memory operations. Reducing spill is the primary optimization target for 15 TB+ joins.
Optimization Stack for 15TB Joins

rows: Partition pruning
Filter to the reporting quarter before the join — scan 1 quarter instead of 12
4x scan reduction (15 TB → 3.75 TB)
WHERE year = 2025 AND month IN (1,2,3) on the Silver table
Column pruning
Select only the 8 columns needed for the report before the join
3x scan reduction (3.75 TB → 1.25 TB at 8 of 24 columns)
select(report_columns) before the first join
DPP on country
The dimension table filtered to one country pushes the filter to the Silver scan
4x within the quarter (1.25 TB → 312 GB per country)
Process one country at a time in a ForEach loop
Materialization boundary
Write the per-country enriched result before the formatting joins
2x shuffle reduction (formatting joins operate on 312 GB, not 1.25 TB)
Intermediate Delta table per country, then formatting joins
Increased partition count
512 shuffle partitions instead of 256 — each partition 600 MB instead of 1.2 GB
Reduces per-partition memory pressure, eliminating 80% of spill
spark.sql.shuffle.partitions = 512 for this pipeline only
Combined effect
All optimizations applied together
15 TB → 312 GB per country × 4 countries, sequential processing
4-hour → 90-minute total pipeline duration

visual-recommendation.description: A funnel diagram showing the progressive reduction in data volume through the optimization stack: 15TB at the top, narrowing through partition pruning (3.75TB), column pruning (1.25TB), DPP per country (312GB), and materialization (150GB effective per formatting join). Each stage is annotated with the optimization technique and the reduction factor.

blocks.content: The optimization stack is applied in order from cheapest to most expensive. Partition pruning and column pruning are free — they change only the query plan, not the code. DPP requires processing one country at a time (a ForEach loop in the pipeline), which adds orchestration complexity but reduces per-iteration data volume by 4x. The materialization boundary requires writing an intermediate table, which adds 3 minutes per country but reduces downstream shuffle by 2x. Increased partition count requires a per-pipeline configuration override. Each optimization is independently measurable: GridUnion tested each in isolation to verify the expected reduction, then combined them. The combined effect (4 hours → 90 minutes) is greater than the sum of individual reductions because the optimizations compound — column pruning reduces the data that DPP must prune, and DPP reduces the data that the materialization boundary must write.

blocks.title: 15 TB joins are architectural problems, not Spark tuning problems
blocks.content: No Spark configuration change turns a 4-hour 15 TB join into a 90-minute join. The reduction came from architectural decisions: process one country at a time (pipeline design), prune to report columns before the join (query design), materialize between join phases (pipeline architecture), and increase partition count for the specific pipeline (configuration scoping). Each decision required understanding the join topology (lesson 1), the data layout (Module 3), and the cost model (Module 2). Senior-level join optimization is the application of architectural reasoning to data processing problems — not the application of Spark tuning knobs.

The previous nine lessons covered join topology analysis for multi-table pipelines, bucketed join coordination with the shared bucket registry, range join strategies including bin-based approximation and pre-materialized lookups, join elimination through PRIMARY KEY constraint declarations, Bloom filter pushdown with Z-ORDER compounding, proactive skew-resistant join design, materialization boundaries for multi-hop join chains, cross-workspace and federated joins at enterprise scale, and the optimization stack for 15 TB+ quarterly regulatory joins. This lesson synthesizes everything into GridUnion's join architecture playbook — the decision framework, the review process, and the governance that ensures every multi-table pipeline uses an appropriate join strategy.
The Join Decision Tree

blocks.title: GridUnion’s join strategy decision tree
blocks.content:
NEW JOIN IN A PIPELINE
│
├─ Is one side < 30MB after filters?
│  YES → Broadcast hash join (verify DPP not disabled by hint)
│  NO ↓
├─ Are both sides bucketed on the join key with matching count?
│  YES → Bucket join (no shuffle, verify in bucket registry)
│  NO ↓
├─ Is the join condition a range (BETWEEN, >, <)?
│  YES → Choose: broadcast range / bin-based / pre-materialize
│        (based on access pattern — current vs historical)
│  NO ↓
├─ Is the join key skewed (>3:1 partition ratio)?
│  YES → Choose: key redesign / pre-aggregation / salting
│        (based on semantic requirements)
│  NO ↓
├─ Is this part of a chain with >5 joins?
│  YES → Add materialization boundary, prune columns between phases
│  NO ↓
└─ Sort-merge join (the general-purpose fallback)
    Verify: DPP active, column pruning applied, partition count appropriate

AFTER IMPLEMENTATION:
• Verify strategy in Spark UI SQL tab
• Check AQE conversions (should be rare, not frequent)
• Record strategy in join_strategy_log
• Submit for join architecture review if >3 joins
blocks.caption: The decision tree evaluates join characteristics in priority order: broadcast first (cheapest), then bucketing (pre-optimized), then range handling (specialized), then skew handling (corrective), then multi-hop optimization (architectural), and finally sort-merge (general purpose). Each decision includes the verification step.

visual-recommendation.description: A flowchart-style decision tree with yes/no branching at each step, the recommended strategy in green boxes, the verification action in blue boxes, and the escalation path (join architecture review) for complex topologies. Each branch is annotated with the typical CU savings vs sort-merge baseline.

blocks.content: The Join Architecture Review

Every pipeline with more than 3 joins undergoes a join architecture review before production deployment. The review evaluates: the join topology graph (star, chain, fan-in, hybrid), the strategy at each join node (broadcast, bucketed, sort-merge, range), the materialization boundary placement (if the chain exceeds 5 joins), the skew analysis (per-key partition size distribution), the estimated and actual shuffle volume (from staging test runs), and the DPP and Bloom filter activation status. The reviewer is a senior engineer from the platform team who has completed both the mid-level and senior Fabric tracks. The review produces either an approval (the architecture is sound) or a recommended redesign with specific optimization suggestions. GridUnion's join reviews have reduced the aggregate shuffle volume of new pipelines by 40% compared to the pre-review era.
What Comes Next

Module 5 covers schema registries and data contracts — the governance system that ensures the tables these joins operate on have stable, documented, and contractually-guaranteed schemas. Every join in this module assumed that the join key columns exist, have consistent types, and are populated correctly across all tables. Module 5 formalizes those assumptions into contracts that are automatically tested, centrally registered, and governed across GridUnion's 12 teams and 4 countries. Join architecture (this module) optimizes how data is combined. Schema governance (Module 5) ensures that the data being combined is structurally compatible.

blocks.title: The join architecture review is the highest-ROI governance activity
blocks.content: At GridUnion's scale, join operations account for approximately 60% of total CU consumption across all pipelines. A 40% reduction in join shuffle volume from architecture reviews translates to a 24% reduction in total platform CU consumption — larger than any other single optimization category (partition tuning: 15%, session management: 8%, column pruning: 12%). The review takes 30 minutes per pipeline. The average CU savings from a review recommendation is 50 CU-minutes per daily pipeline run (18,250 CU-minutes per year). At 20 reviews per quarter, the aggregate annual savings exceed 350,000 CU-minutes — a measurable, financially significant return on 10 hours of senior engineer review time.

chapters.title: Schema Registries and Data Contracts
chapters.description: Design and operate a cross-team schema governance system for Fabric — schema registries, data contracts between producer and consumer teams, contract testing, and the organizational processes that prevent schema changes from breaking downstream consumers across a 12-team platform.

blocks.content: The mid-level track's schema evolution workflow was adequate for NordGrid: the data engineer classified the change (additive, type-widening, breaking), notified the 3-team organization, updated the schema contract test, and deployed through CI/CD. The process worked because the engineer knew every downstream consumer personally and could verify the impact in a 10-minute hallway conversation. At GridUnion's scale — 12 teams across 4 countries producing and consuming 200+ tables — the hallway conversation model collapses. The German data engineer who adds grid_zone_id to the Silver meter table has no idea that the French regulatory team's quarterly submission pipeline depends on a column count check that fails when a new column appears. The schema change passes all German tests, breaks a French pipeline, and the failure is not discovered until the quarterly submission deadline is 3 days away.
Why Mid-Level Schema Practices Break at Scale

rows: Schema contract test in the producer's CI/CD
Tested the producer's own expectations — 3 consumers used the same schema definition
12 consumer teams have different expectations — the producer's test does not know what consumers check
Contract tests owned by consumers, verified against the producer's output after every pipeline run
Classify changes as additive/breaking and notify consumers
One Slack channel, 15 people — notification reached everyone
4 countries, 12 teams, 80 engineers — notifications are missed, misrouted, or ignored
Automated impact analysis using the schema registry and Fabric's lineage view
mergeSchema for additive changes, overwriteSchema for breaking
The single schema contract test caught mismatches before they reached production
Consumer tests may not exist, or may not run until the consumer's next scheduled pipeline — days after the change
Schema drift detection that runs hourly and alerts when actual schema diverges from the registered schema
Schema change reviewed in the same team's pull request
The reviewer knew the downstream impact because they built the downstream pipelines
The German reviewer does not know what the French team depends on
Cross-team schema change requests reviewed by the governance committee with automated impact reports

blocks.content: The Cost of Schema Incidents

GridUnion tracked 14 schema-related incidents in the first year of multi-country operations. Each incident averaged 6 hours of cross-team investigation (identifying the change, identifying the affected consumers, coordinating the fix), 2 hours of reprocessing (re-running affected pipelines after the fix), and 1 day of follow-up (updating tests, adding notifications, documenting the post-mortem). The total cost: 14 incidents × 8 person-hours × €120/hour average engineering cost = approximately €13,500 in direct engineering time, plus the unmeasured cost of late regulatory submissions, stale dashboards, and eroded cross-team trust. The schema governance system described in this module costs approximately 200 engineering hours to build and 20 hours per quarter to operate — a payback period of under 6 months.

blocks.title: Schema governance is trust infrastructure
blocks.content: When the French regulatory team trusts the German Silver table enough to build their quarterly submission pipeline against it, that trust is based on an implicit contract: the schema will not change without notice. Every unnotified schema change erodes that trust. After two incidents, the French team added defensive schema validation that rejects any schema change — even additive ones that would be harmless. This over-defensive behavior (rejecting all changes instead of evaluating each one) is a symptom of broken trust. Schema governance rebuilds trust by making the implicit contract explicit: the schema is registered, changes follow a declared process, consumers are notified automatically, and contract tests verify compatibility before changes reach production.

A schema registry is a centralized store that records the expected schema for every production table — the column names, types, nullability, ordering, and version history. The registry is the single source of truth for 'what does this table's schema look like?' When a consumer team builds a pipeline against a producer's table, they reference the registry version, not the table's current schema. When a producer team changes the schema, they register the new version in the registry before deploying the change. The registry provides the audit trail that answers 'what was the schema at version 3.1, and when was it deployed?' — essential for debugging incidents where a schema change caused a downstream failure.
The Registry Design

# GridUnion's schema registry — a Delta table in ws_gu_platform

registry_schema = StructType([
    StructField("table_fqn", StringType()),          # Fully qualified: ws.lakehouse.table
    StructField("version", StringType()),             # Semantic version: 3.2.0
    StructField("schema_json", StringType()),          # Full StructType serialized as JSON
    StructField("owner_team", StringType()),           # DE Data Engineering DE
    StructField("country", StringType()),              # DE, FR, NL, LU, or GU
    StructField("compatibility_mode", StringType()),   # BACKWARD, FORWARD, FULL, NONE
    StructField("registered_by", StringType()),        # Engineer who registered this version
    StructField("registered_at", TimestampType()),     # Registration timestamp
    StructField("change_type", StringType()),          # ADDITIVE, TYPE_WIDENING, BREAKING
    StructField("change_description", StringType()),   # Human-readable change summary
    StructField("status", StringType()),               # ACTIVE, DEPRECATED, SUPERSEDED
    StructField("deprecation_date", DateType()),       # Null if ACTIVE
])

Every production Delta table and Warehouse table has at least one entry in the registry. When the German data engineering team deploys a new version of the Silver meter table's schema (adding grid_zone_id), they create a new registry entry with version 3.3.0, the full schema JSON, the change type (ADDITIVE), and the change description ('Added grid_zone_id for BNetzA grid zone identification'). The previous version (3.2.0) is updated to status SUPERSEDED. Consumers that reference version 3.2.0 in their contract tests can check whether 3.2.0 is still ACTIVE or has been SUPERSEDED — and the superseding version's change_type tells them whether the change is compatible with their expectations.
Versioning Convention

GridUnion uses semantic versioning for schemas: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH. A PATCH increment (3.2.0 → 3.2.1) indicates a metadata-only change — description update, owner change, no schema modification. A MINOR increment (3.2.1 → 3.3.0) indicates an additive or type-widening change — new columns, wider types. A MAJOR increment (3.3.0 → 4.0.0) indicates a breaking change — column removal, rename, or type narrowing. Consumers who depend on version 3.x can safely accept any 3.x.y update (additive changes are backward-compatible). A MAJOR version change (4.0.0) requires consumer action — they must review the breaking change, update their pipelines, and reference the new major version. This convention enables consumers to express their compatibility tolerance: 'I depend on version 3.x' means 'I accept additive changes but not breaking ones.'

blocks.title: The registry is metadata, not enforcement
blocks.content: The schema registry records what the schema should be. Delta Lake's schema enforcement prevents writes that violate the schema. The registry does not replace Delta's enforcement — it complements it by adding cross-team visibility and version history that Delta's transaction log does not provide. Delta's transaction log records what changed at the file level (new files, removed files). The registry records what changed at the business level (grid_zone_id added for BNetzA compliance, version 3.3.0, classified as ADDITIVE). The registry speaks the language of teams and governance. The transaction log speaks the language of files and transactions. Both are necessary.

A schema registry records what the schema is. A data contract defines what a consumer can depend on. The contract goes beyond the schema: it includes the freshness SLA (the table is refreshed by 03:00 UTC daily), the quality guarantees (null rate for meter_id is zero, row count is between 900K and 1.1M), the retention policy (3 years of rolling history), and the schema compatibility mode (BACKWARD — additive changes are permitted without consumer notification; breaking changes require the governance process). The contract is an agreement between the producer team (who commits to meeting the contract's terms) and the consumer teams (who build their pipelines against the contract's guarantees). When the contract is met, consumers do not need to validate the data themselves — the contract is their validation.
The Contract Definition

The contract is a JSON document stored in the schema registry alongside the schema versions. It is the consumer-facing interface of the data product. When the French regulatory team decides to use the German Silver meter table for their quarterly EU submission, they register as a consumer in the contract, reference schema version 3.x (meaning they accept any 3.x version), and build their pipeline against the contract's guarantees. If the producer violates the contract (freshness SLA missed, null rate above zero, breaking schema change without the governance process), the contract violation is detected by the automated contract tests (lesson 4) and the producer team is notified. The contract makes the implicit dependency explicit and automatable.
Contract Negotiation

Not every consumer wants the same guarantees. The French regulatory team requires 100% data completeness (every meter for every day — missing data causes a regulatory penalty). The German analytics team accepts 99% completeness (a few missing meters do not affect dashboard trends). The Platinum consolidation pipeline requires cross-country consistency (all 4 countries' data must be from the same date — stale data from one country invalidates the consolidated view). These different requirements cannot be satisfied by a single contract. GridUnion supports tiered contracts: the base contract defines the minimum guarantees that all consumers receive (schema compatibility, basic quality thresholds), and supplementary addenda define additional guarantees for specific consumers (the French regulatory addendum adds 100% completeness; the Platinum addendum adds cross-country freshness consistency). Each addendum is a separate document that the producer team agrees to individually.

blocks.title: Contracts make dependencies visible and manageable
blocks.content: Before contracts, the German data engineering team did not know that the French regulatory team depended on their Silver table. The dependency was invisible — discovered only when it broke. After contracts, the dependency is registered, the French team's requirements are documented in a supplementary addendum, and the German team's CI/CD pipeline runs the French team's contract tests after every schema change. The contract did not create the dependency — the dependency already existed. The contract made it visible so both teams could manage it. Every cross-team data dependency at GridUnion that is not registered in a contract is a future incident waiting to happen.

A contract is only as reliable as its verification. If the producer commits to null_rate = 0 for meter_id but nobody checks, the commitment is a promise without enforcement. Contract testing automates the verification: after every pipeline run, a test suite checks the producer's output against every contract guarantee — schema compatibility, quality thresholds, freshness SLA, and uniqueness constraints. The test results are published to a shared contract health dashboard that all consumer teams can view. A contract violation triggers an alert to the producer team, the affected consumer teams, and the governance committee. This lesson covers the contract testing architecture, the test suite design, and the integration with GridUnion's CI/CD pipeline.
The Contract Test Suite

def run_contract_tests(table_fqn: str, spark) -> dict:
    """Run all contract tests for a data product after a pipeline run."""
    # Load the contract from the registry
    contract = load_contract(table_fqn, spark)
    df = spark.read.table(table_fqn)
    results = {"table": table_fqn, "timestamp": datetime.now(), "tests": []}

    # Test 1: Schema compatibility — actual schema matches registered version
    registered = load_schema_version(contract["schema"]["current_version"], spark)
    actual_fields = {f.name: str(f.dataType) for f in df.schema.fields}
    registered_fields = {f["name"]: f["type"] for f in json.loads(registered)}
    schema_match = all(registered_fields[k] == actual_fields.get(k)
                       for k in registered_fields)
    results["tests"].append({"test": "schema_compatibility", "passed": schema_match})

    # Test 2: Null rate for required columns
    for col_name, max_null_rate in contract["quality"]["null_rate"].items():
        null_count = df.filter(col(col_name).isNull()).count()
        null_rate = null_count / max(df.count(), 1)
        results["tests"].append({"test": f"null_rate_{col_name}",
            "passed": null_rate <= max_null_rate, "actual": null_rate})

    # Test 3: Row count range
    count = df.count()
    lo, hi = contract["quality"]["row_count_range"]
    results["tests"].append({"test": "row_count_range",
        "passed": lo <= count <= hi, "actual": count})

    # Test 4: Uniqueness on business key
    key_cols = contract["quality"]["uniqueness"]
    dup_count = df.groupBy(key_cols).count().filter(col("count") > 1).count()
    results["tests"].append({"test": "uniqueness",
        "passed": dup_count == 0, "actual": dup_count})

    # Write results to the contract health table
    write_contract_results(results, spark)
    return results

The contract test suite runs after every producer pipeline execution — as the final step in the pipeline, after the Silver MERGE completes and before the success notification fires. If any contract test fails, the pipeline's status changes from SUCCESS to PARTIAL (the data was written but the contract is violated), and the alerting system notifies both the producer team and all registered consumers. The consumer teams can then decide whether the violation affects their use case (a row count slightly below the range might be acceptable for analytics but not for billing) and whether to proceed with their own pipeline or wait for the producer to remediate. The contract test takes 30–60 seconds to run — a negligible addition to the pipeline duration that provides the verification backbone for cross-team trust.
The Contract Health Dashboard
