The violated Platinum product is highlighted with an expanded detail panel showing the specific failures and the affected consumer teams.

blocks.title: Contract tests belong to the producer, not the consumer
blocks.content: A common misunderstanding: the consumer team should run contract tests against the producer's data. This is wrong. The producer team runs the contract tests because they run after the producer's pipeline, in the producer's workspace, with the producer's alerting. If the consumer runs the tests, there is a timing gap — the producer writes incorrect data at 02:15, the consumer's pipeline runs at 06:00 and discovers the violation, and the 4-hour gap is wasted. The producer's contract tests discover the violation at 02:16 — 1 minute after the write — giving the producer time to remediate before any consumer is affected. GridUnion's convention: the producer's pipeline runs contract tests. The consumer's pipeline checks the contract health dashboard before starting.

Additive changes (new columns) are backward-compatible — consumers that reference version 3.x accept any 3.x.y update without modification. Breaking changes (column removal, rename, type narrowing) require a MAJOR version increment and cannot be deployed without consumer coordination. At GridUnion's scale, a breaking change to the German Silver meter table affects 23 downstream consumers across 4 countries. The mid-level track's schema change workflow (classify → notify → deploy) is insufficient because notification does not guarantee acknowledgment, and acknowledgment does not guarantee migration. This lesson covers the formal breaking change process that ensures every affected consumer migrates before the change reaches production.
The Breaking Change Lifecycle

items: Impact analysis: the producer runs the automated impact analysis tool (lesson 8) against the schema registry and Fabric's lineage view. The tool identifies every table, pipeline, view, semantic model, and report that references the changing column. The output is a consumer impact report listing each affected item, its owning team, and the specific column reference.

Change request: the producer submits a breaking change request to the governance committee. The request includes the impact report, the business justification (why the change is necessary), the new schema (version 4.0.0), the proposed deprecation timeline, and the migration plan for affected consumers.

Governance review: the committee reviews the request, verifies the impact analysis is complete, and approves or requests modifications to the timeline. The committee may extend the deprecation period if a consumer team has a conflicting deadline (the French regulatory submission is due in 2 weeks — do not break their pipeline now).

Deprecation announcement: the producer publishes the old version as DEPRECATED in the registry with a deprecation_date 90 days in the future. All registered consumers receive an automated notification with the change details and migration instructions.

Migration support window (90 days): the producer's table runs in dual-schema mode — writing both the old column and the new column simultaneously. Consumers migrate at their own pace: they update their pipelines to reference the new column, test in staging, and register their updated contract reference (version 4.x). The producer tracks migration progress on the change request.

Migration verification: the producer verifies that all registered consumers have updated their contract references to version 4.x. Any consumer still referencing 3.x receives a final reminder 14 days before the deprecation date.

Breaking change deployment: after the 90-day window, the producer removes the old column, registers version 4.0.0 as ACTIVE, and marks 3.x as DEPRECATED. The old column is gone, and any consumer that did not migrate will fail — but they had 90 days of notice and support.

visual-recommendation.description: A horizontal timeline diagram showing the 90-day breaking change lifecycle: Day 0 (impact analysis and change request), Day 7 (governance approval and deprecation announcement), Days 14-75 (dual-schema migration window with consumer progress tracking), Day 76 (migration verification), Day 90 (old column removal). Each phase is color-coded and annotated with the responsible party and the key deliverable.

blocks.content: The 90-day lifecycle is GridUnion's standard. Emergency breaking changes (a security vulnerability in a column's data, a regulatory mandate with a shorter deadline) can use an expedited process with a 30-day window and direct escalation to the governance committee. GridUnion has executed 3 breaking changes in 18 months of operation. The longest migration (renaming meter_id to device_id across 23 consumers) took 75 days — within the 90-day window. The shortest (removing a debug column that only 2 consumers referenced) took 14 days. All three completed with zero consumer-reported incidents because the migration support window gave every consumer time to test and validate.

blocks.title: 90 days is not optional — it is the cost of breaking a contract
blocks.content: Engineers accustomed to agile development find 90-day deprecation windows frustrating. 'I just need to rename one column — why does it take 3 months?' Because 23 consumers built their pipelines against a contract that promised the column's existence. Each consumer has their own development cycle, testing pipeline, and deployment schedule. Asking 23 consumers across 4 countries to coordinate a migration in 2 weeks is operationally unrealistic and organizationally disrespectful. The 90-day window respects each consumer's ability to plan, test, and deploy on their own schedule. The frustration with the 90-day window is a design signal: if breaking changes are frequent enough to be frustrating, the schema was not designed for stability — and the solution is better upfront schema design, not shorter deprecation windows.

GridUnion operates in 4 countries with 4 regulatory regimes. The German regulator (BNetzA) requires a grid_zone_id column for grid topology tracking. The French regulator (CRE) does not require it but mandates a tariff_classification column for consumer protection reporting. The Dutch regulator (ACM) requires neither but mandates a renewable_share_pct column for sustainability reporting. Each country's Silver table must accommodate country-specific columns that other countries do not need. The challenge: should the unified Silver schema include all country-specific columns (creating a wide schema with null values for irrelevant columns), should each country have an independent schema (creating 4 schema versions to maintain), or should the schema use a flexible structure that accommodates variability (like a JSON metadata column)?
Three Approaches

rows: Unified wide schema
All country-specific columns in one schema; non-applicable columns are null
One schema to maintain, one contract, cross-country queries work without schema translation
Schema grows with every country-specific requirement; null columns waste scan volume; consumers must know which columns apply to their country
CHOSEN for Silver — simplicity and cross-country queryability outweigh the null-column overhead
Per-country schemas
Each country has an independent schema with only its required columns
Clean schemas per country, no null columns, each country evolves independently
4 schemas to maintain, cross-country queries require schema mapping, Platinum consolidation becomes a schema union operation
REJECTED — the Platinum consolidation cost exceeds the null-column savings
Flexible metadata column
A JSON or MAP column that holds country-specific key-value pairs
Infinitely flexible, no schema changes needed for new country requirements
Loses type safety, cannot be indexed or Z-ORDERed, queries require JSON parsing that blocks pushdown
REJECTED — the pushdown loss on a 15TB table is unacceptable

blocks.content: Managing the Unified Schema's Growth

The unified wide schema approach means that every country-specific column request expands the schema for all countries. GridUnion's Silver meter table started with 9 columns at NordGrid scale. After 18 months of multi-country operation, it has 15 columns — 9 universal and 6 country-specific. The growth rate is approximately 3–4 columns per year. At this rate, the schema will have 25 columns in 3 years — wide but manageable. GridUnion controls the growth with two policies. First, a column addition requires a governance committee review that evaluates whether the column is genuinely country-specific (warranting addition to the unified schema) or team-specific (warranting a derived table in the requesting team's workspace instead). Second, column pruning is enforced in every consumer's query — the mid-level track's select() discipline scales directly to the unified schema. A consumer that needs 8 of 15 columns reads only 8 columns regardless of the total width.
Country-Specific Contract Addenda

The unified schema has 15 columns, but not all consumers need all 15. The base contract guarantees the 9 universal columns (meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, year, month, daily_mw, processing_timestamp). The German addendum guarantees the grid_zone_id column's population for German data (non-null for country = 'DE', null for other countries). The French addendum guarantees the tariff_classification column's population for French data. Each addendum is a supplementary document attached to the base contract — the producer commits to populating the country-specific columns for the relevant country's data. Consumers in other countries see the column (it exists in the unified schema) but do not depend on it (their contract does not guarantee its population). This tiered contract structure lets the unified schema accommodate country variability without burdening consumers with columns they do not use.

blocks.title: The unified schema is a trade-off, not a best practice
blocks.content: GridUnion chose the unified wide schema because cross-country analytics is a frequent, high-value query pattern. An organization where cross-country analytics is rare or non-existent would be better served by per-country schemas — the maintenance overhead is lower and each country's schema stays clean. The choice depends on how often consumers need to query across country boundaries. If the answer is 'daily' (GridUnion's case), the unified schema's simplicity for cross-country queries justifies the null-column overhead. If the answer is 'quarterly' (a different organization's case), per-country schemas with a quarterly schema-union notebook may be more appropriate. There is no universal best practice — only trade-offs evaluated against usage patterns.

Schema governance in the mid-level track ended at the Delta table. At senior level, schema changes propagate through four layers: the Delta table (the data), the SQL analytics endpoint and Warehouse views (the query interface), the Power BI semantic model (the analytical model), and the Power BI reports and dashboards (the consumer interface). A column added to the Silver Delta table appears in the SQL endpoint within seconds, but the semantic model does not see it until refreshed, and Power BI reports do not include it until a report author adds it. A column renamed in the Delta table breaks the semantic model's column mapping and every report visual that references the old column name. Schema governance must span all four layers to prevent changes from propagating partially — updated in the table but not in the model, creating inconsistency.
The Four-Layer Propagation

rows: Delta table
Direct write with mergeSchema or overwriteSchema
Immediate
None — mergeSchema adds the column automatically
overwriteSchema replaces the schema (coordinated deployment)
SQL endpoint / Warehouse views
Automatic sync from Delta transaction log
5–30 seconds
Column appears automatically; update views to include it if needed
Views referencing renamed/removed columns break — update views before the table change
Semantic model (Direct Lake)
Refresh from the SQL endpoint metadata
Requires explicit refresh or scheduled refresh
Column not visible until refresh; trigger refresh from deployment pipeline
Column mapping breaks on refresh — update model definition before triggering refresh
Power BI reports
Manual by report author or automated via API
Depends on author action or automation
Column available in model; report author adds it to visuals when ready
Visuals referencing the old column show errors; report author must update

visual-recommendation.description: A four-layer vertical diagram showing schema change propagation from Delta table at the bottom through SQL endpoint, semantic model, to Power BI reports at the top. Each layer shows the propagation mechanism (automatic, refresh-required, manual) and the latency. Arrows between layers are color-coded: green for automatic propagation, yellow for refresh-required, red for manual action needed.

blocks.content: The Coordinated Deployment Pattern

For breaking changes, the four layers must be updated in a specific sequence to avoid intermediate inconsistency. GridUnion's sequence for a column rename (daily_kw → daily_kwh): Step 1: add the new column (daily_kwh) to the Delta table as an additive change — both columns coexist. Step 2: update SQL views and Warehouse stored procedures to reference daily_kwh. Step 3: update the semantic model definition to map to daily_kwh. Step 4: trigger a semantic model refresh. Step 5: report authors update their visuals from daily_kw to daily_kwh. Step 6: after all consumers have migrated (verified via the contract health dashboard), remove the old column (daily_kw) from the Delta table. The rename is decomposed into an addition (non-breaking), a migration (consumer-paced), and a removal (breaking but with zero remaining consumers). This decomposition converts one breaking change into two additive changes separated by a consumer migration window.

The deployment pipeline automates steps 1–4. Step 5 (report updates) requires human action by report authors — the automation sends a notification with instructions. Step 6 (column removal) is gated on the contract health dashboard showing zero consumers referencing the old column. GridUnion's schema change deployment notebook executes the automated steps and blocks on step 6 until the migration verification passes. The entire process typically takes 30–60 days for a column rename — most of the time is the consumer migration window (step 5), not the technical deployment.

blocks.title: The semantic model is the most fragile layer
blocks.content: The Delta table handles additive changes gracefully (mergeSchema). The SQL endpoint syncs automatically. But the semantic model's Direct Lake connection caches column metadata, and a schema change that adds or removes columns requires an explicit model refresh to update the cache. If the refresh happens before the report author has updated their visuals, the report may show errors for a column that was renamed or removed. GridUnion's deployment sequence places the semantic model refresh after the SQL view update but before the report author notification — giving the model correct metadata when the report author opens it. The sequence matters: refreshing the model before updating the views propagates the old (wrong) metadata. Refreshing after the views propagates the new (correct) metadata.

When the German team proposes adding grid_zone_id to the Silver meter table, the governance committee needs to know: which tables, views, pipelines, semantic models, and reports reference this table? Which of those reference the specific columns being changed? Which teams own the affected items? Fabric's built-in lineage view answers the first question (which items reference the table). The schema registry answers the second question (which consumers depend on the current schema version). Combining both produces a complete impact analysis: item-level dependencies from lineage, plus column-level dependencies from the registry, plus team-level ownership from the data product manifest. This lesson covers the impact analysis pipeline that automates this combination.
The Impact Analysis Pipeline

def analyze_schema_change_impact(table_fqn: str, changed_columns: list,
                                  change_type: str, spark) -> dict:
    """Compute the full impact of a proposed schema change."""
    impact = {"table": table_fqn, "change_type": change_type,
              "changed_columns": changed_columns, "affected_items": []}

    # Layer 1: Fabric lineage — which items reference this table?
    # (Uses Fabric REST API to query the lineage graph)
    lineage_items = get_fabric_lineage(table_fqn)

    # Layer 2: Schema registry — which consumers depend on the current version?
    contract = load_contract(table_fqn, spark)
    consumers = contract.get("consumers", [])

    # Layer 3: Column-level analysis for breaking changes
    for item in lineage_items:
        affected_cols = get_column_references(item, changed_columns)
        if affected_cols or change_type == "BREAKING":
            impact["affected_items"].append({
                "item_name": item["name"],
                "item_type": item["type"],  # pipeline, view, semantic_model, report
                "workspace": item["workspace"],
                "owner_team": get_owner_team(item, spark),
                "affected_columns": affected_cols,
                "risk_level": "HIGH" if change_type == "BREAKING" else "LOW"
            })

    # Layer 4: Consumer contract analysis
    for consumer in consumers:
        impact["affected_items"].append({
            "item_name": f"Contract: {consumer['team']}",
            "item_type": "contract",
            "owner_team": consumer["team"],
            "contract_version": consumer["contract_version"],
            "risk_level": "HIGH" if change_type == "BREAKING" else "NONE"
        })

    return impact

The impact analysis combines four data sources: Fabric's lineage API (item-level dependencies), the schema registry (contract-level dependencies), the ownership registry (team assignments), and a column reference extractor that parses SQL views and notebook code to identify which specific columns are referenced. The output is a structured impact report that the governance committee reviews: for each affected item, the report shows the item name, type, workspace, owning team, the specific columns at risk, and the risk level. For the grid_zone_id addition (an additive change), the report shows LOW risk for all items — no column references are affected because the column is new. For a column rename (a breaking change), the report shows HIGH risk for every item that references the old column name.
Automated Drift Detection

Impact analysis is proactive — it runs before a change is deployed. Drift detection is reactive — it runs hourly and compares each production table's actual schema to its registered schema in the registry. If the actual schema has a column that the registry does not know about (someone added a column without registering it), the drift detector flags the discrepancy. If the registry has a column that the actual table does not (someone removed a column without following the breaking change process), the drift detector flags a critical violation. GridUnion's drift detection notebook runs every hour, compares 200+ production tables to their registry entries, and sends alerts for any discrepancy. In the first month of operation, drift detection caught 4 unregistered schema changes — all made by engineers who forgot to update the registry. After 6 months, unregistered changes dropped to near zero because engineers learned that drift detection would catch them immediately.

blocks.title: Impact analysis prevents incidents; drift detection catches oversights
blocks.content: Impact analysis is the front door: every deliberate schema change goes through it, gets reviewed, and deploys with full consumer coordination. Drift detection is the back door alarm: it catches schema changes that bypassed the front door — accidental mergeSchema additions, debug columns left in production, manual overwriteSchema runs that skipped the governance process. Both are necessary. Impact analysis alone misses the changes that bypass the process. Drift detection alone catches changes after they are deployed — too late to prevent consumer impact. Together, they provide complete schema governance: deliberate changes are managed proactively, and accidental changes are detected reactively within 60 minutes.

The schema governance processes described in lessons 1–8 — registry management, contract testing, breaking change lifecycle, impact analysis, drift detection — involve significant manual work if operated through spreadsheets and email. At 200+ tables with 12 teams, manual governance cannot keep pace with the rate of schema changes (3–4 per week across the platform). Governance automation replaces manual processes with pipeline-driven automation: CI/CD gates that verify schema compatibility before deployment, automated contract test execution after every pipeline run, automated drift detection every hour, and automated impact reports generated from a single command. This lesson covers the automation architecture that makes schema governance scalable.
CI/CD Schema Gates

GridUnion's deployment pipeline (Module 7 of the mid-level track, extended for senior-level governance) includes a schema compatibility gate that runs before any notebook with mergeSchema or overwriteSchema is promoted to production. The gate reads the current registered schema from the registry, compares it to the notebook's output schema (captured from a staging run), classifies the change (additive, type-widening, or breaking), and blocks the deployment if the change is breaking and the governance process has not been completed. The gate checks for the governance committee's approval ticket number in the PR description — no ticket, no deployment. Additive changes pass the gate automatically (they are backward-compatible and do not require governance review). The gate adds 30 seconds to the deployment pipeline and has blocked 6 uncoordinated breaking changes in 18 months.
The Governance-as-Code Pipeline

GridUnion's governance automation is a scheduled Fabric pipeline (pl_gu_schema_governance) that runs daily at 05:00 UTC. The pipeline executes four notebooks sequentially. First, nb_contract_health compiles the contract test results from all production tables into the contract health dashboard. Second, nb_drift_detection compares all 200+ tables against the registry and alerts on discrepancies. Third, nb_deprecation_tracker checks for schemas approaching their deprecation date and sends reminders to consumers who have not migrated. Fourth, nb_governance_report generates the weekly governance summary — new schema versions registered, breaking changes in progress, drift events, contract violations — and writes it to the platform workspace's governance_reports table. The pipeline takes 8 minutes, costs 15 CU-minutes, and produces the operational data that the governance committee reviews in their weekly meeting.
Self-Service Schema Registration

The initial schema registration process required the platform team to manually enter registry records. At 200+ tables, this bottlenecked on the platform team's availability. GridUnion automated registration with a self-service notebook (nb_register_schema) that any data engineer can run. The notebook reads a table's current schema, validates it against naming conventions (column names in snake_case, types matching the standard type mapping), generates the registry entry, and submits it for automatic approval (for new tables) or governance review (for existing tables with version increments). The engineer fills in the change_description and change_type fields, and the notebook handles the rest. Self-service registration reduced the registration turnaround from 2 days (waiting for the platform team) to 5 minutes (the engineer runs the notebook themselves).

blocks.title: Automate the routine, govern the exceptional
blocks.content: GridUnion automates the routine governance tasks — contract testing, drift detection, deprecation tracking, additive schema registration — and reserves human governance review for exceptional cases — breaking changes, new data products, cross-country schema evolution. This balance ensures that the 3–4 schema changes per week are processed without bottleneck (automated), while the 2–3 breaking changes per year receive thorough review (human). The governance committee meets weekly for 30 minutes. Most meetings are status reviews of the automated reports. Occasionally, a breaking change request requires architectural discussion. The automation handles 95% of the governance workload, freeing the committee to focus on the 5% that requires judgment.

The previous nine lessons covered the schema governance problem at multi-team scale, the schema registry with semantic versioning and change classification, data contracts with tiered guarantees and consumer-specific addenda, automated contract testing integrated into the producer's pipeline, the 90-day breaking change lifecycle with governance committee oversight, schema evolution across countries with the unified wide schema trade-off, semantic layer governance spanning Delta tables through SQL views to Power BI reports, schema lineage and impact analysis with automated drift detection, and governance automation with CI/CD gates and self-service registration. This lesson synthesizes everything into GridUnion's schema governance framework.
The Framework Summary

blocks.title: GridUnion's schema governance framework
blocks.content:
SCHEMA GOVERNANCE FRAMEWORK
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                         │
│  REGISTRY          CONTRACTS          TESTING            │
│  • Schema versions   • Base contract     • Contract tests   │
│  • Semantic version   • Country addenda   • After every run  │
│  • Change classif.    • Consumer list     • Health dashboard │
│  • Compatibility mode • Tiered SLAs       • Violation alerts │
│                                                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                         │
│  CHANGE MANAGEMENT    DETECTION          AUTOMATION       │
│  • 90-day lifecycle    • Hourly drift scan • CI/CD gates     │
│  • Impact analysis     • Lineage + registry• Self-service reg│
│  • Governance review   • Column-level deps • Governance pipe │
│  • Migration tracking  • 4-layer propagate • Weekly report   │
│                                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Outcomes: 0 undetected schema incidents (drift detection), 0 breaking
changes without governance (CI/CD gates), 90-day consumer migration
window (lifecycle), <60 min detection for accidental changes (drift)
blocks.caption: The framework has six components organized in two tiers: the declaration tier (registry, contracts, testing) defines what schemas exist and verifies they are met, and the operations tier (change management, detection, automation) governs how schemas evolve. Together they provide complete coverage: deliberate changes are managed, accidental changes are detected, and all changes are tested.

visual-recommendation.description: A six-panel governance framework diagram with the registry, contracts, and testing in the top tier (declaration) and change management, detection, and automation in the bottom tier (operations). Arrows show the flows between components: registry feeds contract tests, drift detection feeds alerts, impact analysis feeds the governance review, and CI/CD gates enforce the process.

blocks.content: What Comes Next

Module 6 goes inside the Spark execution engine — the DAGScheduler, TaskScheduler, shuffle mechanics, and memory management that determine how Fabric's managed Spark actually executes the queries that operate on the governed schemas. Module 5 ensured that schemas are stable, compatible, and contractually guaranteed. Module 6 ensures that the queries against those schemas execute efficiently at the engine level. The extensibility phase (Modules 4–5) designed the logical architecture: join topologies and schema contracts. The deep internals phase (Modules 6–7) explains the physical execution: how Spark turns the logical plan into actual computation on actual hardware.

blocks.title: Schema governance is the contract layer that makes data products trustworthy
blocks.content: Module 1 introduced data products with manifests, certification, and ownership contracts. Module 5 built the schema-specific infrastructure that makes those contracts enforceable: the registry that records what the schema should be, the contract tests that verify it after every run, the drift detection that catches unregistered changes, the breaking change lifecycle that protects consumers, and the automation that operates the entire system at 200+ table scale. Without schema governance, data product contracts are aspirational documents. With schema governance, they are automatically verified, continuously monitored, and organizationally enforceable. The 14 schema incidents from GridUnion's first year dropped to 1 in the second year — after the governance framework was fully operational. That single incident was a timing edge case in the drift detection pipeline — which was itself caught and fixed within 2 hours.

chapters.title: Spark Internals — The Execution Engine
chapters.description: Understand how Fabric's managed Spark executes your code at the engine level — DAGScheduler, TaskScheduler, shuffle mechanics, memory management, and the diagnostic techniques for cluster-level problems that execution plans alone cannot explain.

blocks.content: Module 2 taught you to read and diagnose execution plans — the physical plan that Catalyst produces. But the physical plan is a specification, not an execution. It says 'sort-merge join on region with 200 partitions' — it does not say how Spark actually schedules the work across executors, manages memory for the sort buffer, handles a task that runs 10x slower than its peers, or recovers when an executor dies mid-task. The gap between the plan (what Spark intends to do) and the execution (what Spark actually does) is where the hardest performance problems live. A plan that looks optimal can execute poorly because of memory pressure, task stragglers, GC pauses, or shuffle bottlenecks that are invisible in the plan output. This module bridges that gap.
The Execution Pipeline

items: Physical plan to RDD lineage: Catalyst's physical plan is converted into an RDD (Resilient Distributed Dataset) lineage graph — a DAG of RDD transformations. Each physical operator (Filter, Project, SortMergeJoin) becomes one or more RDD operations. The RDD lineage graph is the execution engine's internal representation of the computation.

DAGScheduler stage splitting: the DAGScheduler analyzes the RDD lineage graph and splits it into stages at shuffle boundaries. Every shuffle (caused by groupBy, join, repartition) creates a new stage. Operations within a stage are pipelined — they execute as a single pass through the data without writing intermediate results to disk.

TaskScheduler task assignment: each stage is divided into tasks — one task per partition. The TaskScheduler assigns tasks to executor slots based on data locality, executor availability, and the scheduling delay configuration. In Fabric's managed Spark, executor count and slot count are determined by the capacity SKU.

Task execution: each task runs in a single thread on a single executor. It reads its input partition (from OneLake for the first stage, from shuffle files for subsequent stages), applies the pipelined transformations, and writes the output (to shuffle files for intermediate stages, to OneLake for the final stage).

Result collection: the driver collects the results from all tasks — either the final output (for write operations) or a collected value (for count(), show(), collect()). The driver does not participate in the distributed computation; it orchestrates and collects.

blocks.content: What the Spark UI Shows vs What the Plan Shows

The plan shows operators and their estimated costs. The Spark UI shows the actual execution: stage durations, task counts, shuffle read/write volumes, spill metrics, GC time, and per-task duration distributions. When the plan says '200 shuffle partitions' and the Spark UI shows stage 3 taking 12 minutes while stage 2 took 30 seconds, the plan does not explain the discrepancy — but the Spark UI's task duration histogram shows that 199 tasks completed in 5 seconds and 1 task took 12 minutes (a straggler caused by data skew in one partition). The execution engine's behavior, not the plan, determines the actual runtime. This module teaches you to diagnose execution-level problems using the Spark UI's stage, task, and executor views.

blocks.title: The plan is the blueprint; the execution is the construction
blocks.content: An architect's blueprint specifies a building's structure. The construction process — materials procurement, weather delays, worker availability, equipment failures — determines whether the building is finished on time and within budget. The physical plan is Spark's blueprint: it specifies the computation's structure. The execution engine — stage scheduling, task assignment, memory management, shuffle coordination — determines whether the computation finishes within SLA and within CU budget. Module 2 taught you to read the blueprint. Module 6 teaches you to diagnose the construction. Both are necessary for senior-level performance engineering.

The DAGScheduler is the component that converts the RDD lineage graph into a sequence of stages. Its primary job is identifying stage boundaries — the points where the computation must materialize intermediate results because a shuffle is required. Every shuffle-inducing operation (groupBy, join, repartition, sortBy) creates a stage boundary: the upstream stage's output is written to shuffle files, and the downstream stage reads from those files. Operations that do not require a shuffle (filter, map, flatMap, project) are pipelined within the same stage — they execute in a single pass through the data without writing intermediate results. Understanding stage boundaries reveals why some operations are 'free' (pipelined within an existing stage) and others are expensive (they create a new stage with its associated shuffle I/O).
Stage Boundary Analysis

GridUnion's German Silver notebook has 7 stages. Stage 0: scan the Bronze Parquet files (a narrow transformation — no shuffle). Stage 1: filter, project, and apply the cleansing transformations (pipelined with stage 0 because no shuffle is needed). Stage 2: the MERGE operation's join with the existing Silver table — this requires a shuffle on meter_id to align the new rows with the existing rows for the match condition. Stage 3: the MERGE's write with partitioning by country/year/month — this requires a shuffle to distribute rows to the correct partition directories. Stage 4: the statistics collection after the MERGE. Stage 5: the contract test's uniqueness check (a groupBy on meter_id + date). Stage 6: the contract test's row count. The 7-stage structure means 4 shuffles (stages 2, 3, 5 are shuffle boundaries). Each shuffle writes data to local storage and reads it back — the I/O cost is proportional to the data volume at each boundary.
Pipelining Within Stages

The filter, project, and cleansing transformations in stages 0–1 do not produce intermediate files. Spark pipelines them: each row is read from Bronze, filtered, projected, and cleansed in a single pass — the row enters the pipeline and emerges transformed without ever being materialized. This pipelining is Spark's core execution efficiency: a stage with 5 transformations is not 5x slower than a stage with 1 transformation — it is approximately 1x because the transformations execute sequentially on each row within a single task, sharing the I/O cost. The implication for performance: adding a filter or a withColumn to an existing stage is nearly free. Adding a groupBy or a join creates a new stage — which is expensive because it triggers a shuffle.

blocks.title: GridUnion's Silver MERGE notebook — stage DAG
blocks.content:
Stage 0+1 (pipelined)          Stage 2              Stage 3

│ Read Bronze Parquet │    │ MERGE join   │    │ MERGE write  │
│ → filter            │    │ on meter_id  │    │ partitionBy  │
│ → project           │ ──▶│ (shuffle on  │ ──▶│ country/year │
│ → cleanse           │    │  meter_id)   │    │  /month      │
│ → cast types        │    │              │    │ (shuffle on  │
│                     │    │              │    │  partition)   │
└─────────────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘
   No shuffle                 Shuffle #1          Shuffle #2
   (pipelined)               (meter_id)          (partition cols)

Stage 4              Stage 5               Stage 6
┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐   ┌──────────────┐
│ ANALYZE      │    │ Contract test:   │   │ Contract test│
│ TABLE stats  │    │ groupBy(meter_id,│   │ count()      │
│              │    │  date).count()   │   │ (no shuffle) │
│              │    │ (shuffle #3)     │   │              │
└──────────────┘    └──────────────────┘   └──────────────┘
   Shuffle #3           Shuffle #4            Tree aggregate
blocks.caption: The 7-stage DAG shows that stages 0 and 1 are pipelined (no shuffle — filter, project, and cleanse execute in a single pass). Stages 2 and 3 are the MERGE operation's join and write shuffles. Stage 5 is the contract test's uniqueness check shuffle. Each shuffle boundary materializes intermediate results to disk, adding I/O overhead proportional to the data volume at that point in the pipeline.

visual-recommendation.description: A DAG diagram showing the 7 stages as boxes connected by arrows, with shuffle boundaries marked as red dashed lines between stages. Each stage box lists the pipelined operations inside it. Shuffle volumes are annotated on the arrows between stages (e.g., '3.2 GB shuffle write' between stages 1 and 2). The pipelined stages are visually grouped to show that they share I/O cost.

blocks.title: Count stages to estimate shuffle cost
blocks.content: A quick way to estimate a notebook's shuffle overhead: count the stages in the Spark UI's Stages tab. Each stage transition is a shuffle. GridUnion's Silver notebook has 4 shuffles across 7 stages. The total shuffle volume (from the Spark UI's SQL tab) is approximately 5 GB. If the notebook were restructured to eliminate one shuffle (for example, by combining the MERGE join and the partition write into a single shuffle), the total shuffle would drop to approximately 3 GB — saving 2 GB of I/O and approximately 15 CU-minutes per run. Before optimizing individual tasks, count the stages and evaluate whether any shuffle boundaries can be eliminated.

The DAGScheduler splits stages into tasks — one task per partition. The TaskScheduler assigns those tasks to executor slots. In a standalone Spark cluster, you control executor count, executor memory, and executor cores through spark-submit parameters. In Fabric's managed Spark, these are determined by the capacity SKU and the workspace's Spark pool configuration. The F128 capacity allocates executors with approximately 16 GB memory and 4 cores each — GridUnion cannot change the executor memory directly but can influence the effective memory per task by changing the partition count (more partitions = less data per task = less memory needed per task). Understanding the TaskScheduler's assignment logic reveals why some tasks are slow (bad locality, overloaded executor) and how to influence the assignment without controlling the cluster directly.
Data Locality in OneLake

In HDFS-based clusters, data locality is critical: a task that reads from a local disk (PROCESS_LOCAL) is 10–100x faster than one that reads over the network (ANY). The TaskScheduler delays assigning a task for up to 3 seconds (spark.locality.wait) to find a PROCESS_LOCAL slot. In Fabric's OneLake storage, all data is remote — there is no local disk that contains the data. Every read goes through the OneLake API, which introduces a consistent latency regardless of which executor reads the data. This means data locality in Fabric is irrelevant: the TaskScheduler's locality preference adds scheduling delay (up to 3 seconds per task) without providing any performance benefit. GridUnion sets spark.locality.wait to 0 on all production workspaces — eliminating the scheduling delay and allowing tasks to start immediately on any available executor.
Executor Slot Utilization

Each executor has a fixed number of task slots (determined by the core count — typically 4 slots per executor on the F128 capacity). If a stage has 200 tasks and the pool allocates 10 executors with 4 slots each (40 slots total), the 200 tasks are processed in 5 waves: tasks 1–40 execute in wave 1, tasks 41–80 in wave 2, and so on. The stage duration is approximately 5 × the average task duration. If one task takes 10x longer than the average (a straggler), it blocks one slot for 10 waves while the other 39 slots sit idle in the final wave. The stage duration becomes the straggler's duration, not the average task duration. The Spark UI's task duration histogram reveals this pattern: a narrow peak at the average duration with one outlier at 10x — the classic skew signature that Module 4 addressed at the join level but manifests at the task level.

rows: Task duration (P50 vs P95 vs max)
Spark UI → Stage detail → Task duration histogram
Skew at the task level — if max >> P95, one partition is oversized
Max / P50 ratio > 5 triggers skew investigation
Executor GC time
Spark UI → Executors tab → GC Time column
Memory pressure — high GC time means the executor is spending cycles on garbage collection instead of computation
> 10% of task duration triggers memory investigation
Task scheduling delay
Spark UI → Stage detail → Scheduler Delay column
Scheduling overhead — delay between task submission and task start
> 500ms indicates slot contention or locality wait overhead
Shuffle read blocked time
Spark UI → Stage detail → Shuffle Read Blocked Time
Shuffle dependency — the task is waiting for shuffle data from a previous stage
> 30% of task duration indicates shuffle bottleneck

blocks.title: In Fabric, you tune partitions, not executors
blocks.content: In standalone Spark, the standard performance tuning knobs are executor count, executor memory, and executor cores. In Fabric, these are fixed by the capacity SKU. The equivalent tuning knobs in Fabric are: partition count (controls data per task), session configuration (controls Spark behavior within the allocated resources), and capacity SKU selection (controls the total resource pool). When a GridUnion notebook has memory pressure (high GC time), the fix is not 'increase executor memory' — the fix is 'increase partition count so each task processes less data within the existing executor memory.' When a notebook is underutilizing the pool, the fix is not 'add executors' — the fix is 'increase partition count so more tasks are available to fill the available slots.' Partition count is the primary performance lever in Fabric's managed environment.

Shuffle is the most expensive operation in distributed data processing. Every groupBy, every join on a non-bucketed key, every repartition triggers a shuffle: the data is redistributed across executors based on the shuffle key's hash value. The shuffle has two phases: the map side (shuffle write) where each task partitions its output into bucket files for each target partition, and the reduce side (shuffle read) where each downstream task reads its bucket files from all upstream tasks. The total network I/O is the data volume × the fan-out factor — 200 upstream tasks writing to 200 downstream partitions produces 40,000 file transfers. At GridUnion's scale, shuffle is the dominant CU consumer, accounting for approximately 40% of total compute cost across all pipelines.
Shuffle Write Mechanics

During shuffle write, each map-side task serializes its output rows, sorts them by the shuffle key's partition hash, and writes them to local shuffle files — one file per target partition. If there are 200 target partitions, each map-side task writes 200 small files. With 200 map-side tasks, the total is 200 × 200 = 40,000 shuffle files. Fabric's managed Spark uses the sort-based shuffle manager: each task sorts all output rows by partition key, then writes them sequentially to a single index file with partition boundaries. This reduces the file count from N² to N — a critical optimization for large shuffle counts. The Spark UI shows shuffle write metrics per stage: shuffle write size (total bytes written), shuffle write time (wall clock time for the write), and shuffle write records (total rows written).
Shuffle Read Mechanics

During shuffle read, each reduce-side task fetches its partition's data from all map-side tasks. With 200 map-side tasks, each reduce-side task makes 200 fetch requests — one to each map-side task's shuffle file. The fetches are parallelized (controlled by spark.reducer.maxReqsInFlight), but the total data transfer traverses the network between executors. In Fabric's managed environment, executors communicate through an internal network managed by the capacity — the bandwidth is shared with other workloads on the same capacity. Heavy shuffles on one notebook can impact other notebooks running on the same capacity, which is why Module 1's capacity isolation strategy (separating engineering and analytics workloads) also reduces shuffle interference.
Shuffle Reduction Strategies at GridUnion Scale

rows: Column pruning before shuffle
Select only needed columns before the groupBy or join that triggers the shuffle
Proportional to column ratio — 8 of 15 columns = 47% reduction
Always — every shuffle should operate on the minimum column set
Broadcast join
Broadcast the small side to avoid shuffling the large side
100% elimination of the large side's shuffle
When one side is < 30MB after filters (Module 2 threshold)
Bucketed join
Pre-distribute data at write time so join keys are already aligned
100% elimination of both sides' shuffle
When join frequency justifies the bucketing overhead (Module 3 lesson 6)
Pre-aggregation
Aggregate before the shuffle to reduce the data volume
Proportional to aggregation ratio — 2M rows → 200 rows = 99.99% reduction
When the downstream operation needs aggregate values, not row-level detail
Partition count tuning
Reduce shuffle partitions to reduce the N² file transfer factor
Reduces overhead, not volume — fewer partitions means fewer but larger transfers
When shuffle write time is dominated by file creation overhead, not data volume
AQE partition coalescing
Let AQE merge small post-shuffle partitions at runtime
Reduces small-file overhead on the reduce side
Always enabled as a safety net — optimizes cases where the static partition count was too high

visual-recommendation.description: A data flow diagram showing the two-phase shuffle process: map-side tasks writing sorted partition files (shuffle write), the network transfer of partition data between executors, and reduce-side tasks reading their partition data (shuffle read). Annotated with the Spark UI metrics at each phase: shuffle write size, shuffle write time, shuffle read size, shuffle read blocked time.

blocks.title: Shuffle is the tax on distributed computation
blocks.content: Every operation that requires seeing all values for a key — groupBy, join, distinct, sortBy — must shuffle because the values may be distributed across many partitions. Shuffle is not a flaw in Spark's design; it is the fundamental cost of distributed data processing. The senior engineer's job is not to eliminate shuffle (which would mean eliminating groupBy and joins) but to minimize shuffle volume: prune columns before shuffling, broadcast small tables, bucket frequently-joined tables, and pre-aggregate when possible. GridUnion's 40% CU consumption for shuffle was reduced to 28% through systematic application of these strategies across the top-20 pipelines — a 30% reduction in shuffle cost that did not change any pipeline's logic or output.

Spark's memory management determines how much memory is available for computation (shuffles, joins, sorts), storage (cached DataFrames), and overhead (internal bookkeeping, user objects). In standalone Spark, you configure executor memory via spark.executor.memory and the split between execution and storage via spark.memory.fraction. In Fabric's managed Spark, executor memory is determined by the capacity SKU and cannot be changed directly. But the unified memory model — where execution and storage share a configurable pool — still applies, and understanding its mechanics is essential for diagnosing memory-related performance problems: GC pauses, spill to disk, and OOM failures.
The Unified Memory Model

Spark divides executor memory into three regions. Reserved memory (300 MB, fixed) is used for internal Spark objects. User memory (1 - spark.memory.fraction of the remaining, typically 40%) is used for user objects — broadcast variables, UDF state, accumulator values. The unified pool (spark.memory.fraction of the remaining, typically 60%) is shared between execution (shuffle buffers, sort buffers, hash tables for joins) and storage (cached DataFrames, broadcast tables). Within the unified pool, execution and storage can borrow from each other: if execution needs more memory and storage has unused capacity, execution borrows it. If storage needs more and execution has unused capacity, storage borrows it. However, execution can evict storage (cached blocks are dropped) but storage cannot evict execution (active computation cannot be interrupted). This asymmetry means that a large cache can be silently evicted during a heavy shuffle — and the next query that expected the cache to be warm will re-read from OneLake.
Diagnosing Memory Pressure

rows: High GC time (> 10% of task time)
Executors tab → GC Time
Executor heap is full — JVM spends time collecting garbage instead of computing
Increase partition count (less data per task) or reduce cache size
Spill to disk
Stage detail → Shuffle Spill (Memory) vs Shuffle Spill (Disk)
Shuffle buffer exceeded execution memory — data written to disk
Increase partition count (smaller partitions fit in memory) or reduce columns before shuffle
OOM (Out of Memory) failure
Executor logs → java.lang.OutOfMemoryError
A single task's data exceeded the executor's memory — common with skewed partitions or large broadcast variables
Fix skew (Module 4), reduce broadcast table size, or increase partition count
Cache eviction
Storage tab → cache blocks show 'Dropped'
Execution borrowed storage memory and evicted cached blocks
Reduce cache scope (cache only what is needed) or separate caching and computation into different stages
Slow serialization
Task detail → Result Serialization Time
Large result objects being serialized for driver collection
Avoid collect() on large results; use write instead of collect

visual-recommendation.description: An annotated Spark UI Executors tab showing the memory breakdown per executor: storage memory used (blue bar), execution memory used (orange bar), and the unified pool boundary. One executor is highlighted with high GC time (red annotation) and another shows normal GC time (green annotation) for contrast. The annotation explains how to read the GC time column relative to task time to diagnose memory pressure.

blocks.content: The Fabric Memory Constraint

In standalone Spark, the fix for memory pressure is often 'increase executor memory.' In Fabric, executor memory is fixed by the SKU. The equivalent fixes are: reduce data per task (increase partition count — the primary lever), reduce data per executor (reduce the number of concurrent tasks per executor via spark.executor.cores, if configurable), or reduce total memory demand (prune columns before memory-intensive operations, reduce cache scope, optimize broadcast variable size). GridUnion's memory optimization process: identify the memory-intensive operation (the stage with the highest spill or GC time), measure the per-task data volume (stage input size / task count), and increase the partition count until per-task data volume is below 70% of the per-task memory allocation. The 70% target leaves 30% headroom for execution overhead, serialization buffers, and JVM objects.

blocks.title: Spill is the performance cliff you do not see in the plan
blocks.content: The execution plan shows a sort-merge join. The Spark UI shows the join stage took 8 minutes. The task detail shows that 6 of those 8 minutes were spill: the sort buffer exceeded memory, data was written to local storage, and the sort-merge was completed by reading from storage instead of memory. Spill is 10–100x slower than in-memory processing, but the plan does not predict it because spill depends on runtime memory availability, not plan structure. GridUnion's Silver notebook had an 8-minute stage that dropped to 45 seconds after increasing the partition count from 200 to 512 — the per-task data volume dropped below the memory threshold and spill was eliminated. No code change, no plan change — only a configuration change that addressed the execution-level problem.

Lesson 5 introduced spill as a memory pressure symptom. This lesson goes deeper into the mechanics: how Spark decides to spill, what data is written to disk, how the spilled data is merged back into the computation, and the specific CU cost of spill in Fabric's managed environment. Spill is the execution engine's safety valve — without it, memory-intensive operations would fail with OOM errors. But spill converts a fast in-memory operation into a slow disk-based operation, and at GridUnion's scale, the CU cost of spill across all pipelines is approximately 150 CU-minutes per day — 5% of total platform consumption spent on reading and writing data that should have been processed in memory.
The Spill Decision

When a task's execution memory (sort buffer, hash table, aggregation buffer) exceeds its allocated share of the unified pool, Spark triggers a spill. The spill process: Spark serializes the in-memory data structure, writes it to the local storage of the executor (not OneLake — local SSD or temporary disk), and frees the memory for continued processing. The task continues processing new input into a fresh in-memory buffer. When the task needs the spilled data (for the merge phase of a sort, or for probing the hash table), it reads it back from local storage, merging it with the current in-memory data. A task may spill multiple times if the input is large enough — each spill writes a new file, and the final merge reads all spill files plus the in-memory data.
Measuring Spill Cost

# Quantify spill cost for a specific stage using Spark UI metrics
# Access via the Spark History Server REST API

import requests
import json

def analyze_spill_cost(app_id: str, stage_id: int, 
                       history_server_url: str) -> dict:
    """Analyze spill volume and estimate CU cost for a stage."""
    url = f"{history_server_url}/api/v1/applications/{app_id}/stages/{stage_id}"
    stage = requests.get(url).json()[0]
    
    spill_memory = stage.get("memoryBytesSpilled", 0)
    spill_disk = stage.get("diskBytesSpilled", 0)
    stage_duration_ms = stage["executorRunTime"]
    shuffle_write = stage.get("shuffleWriteBytes", 0)
    
    # Estimate spill's share of stage duration
    # Heuristic: spill I/O is ~100MB/s on Fabric's local storage
    spill_io_seconds = (spill_memory + spill_disk) / (100 * 1024 * 1024)
    spill_fraction = spill_io_seconds / (stage_duration_ms / 1000)
    
    return {
        "stage_id": stage_id,
        "spill_memory_gb": round(spill_memory / (1024**3), 2),
        "spill_disk_gb": round(spill_disk / (1024**3), 2),
        "stage_duration_sec": round(stage_duration_ms / 1000, 1),
        "est_spill_fraction": round(min(spill_fraction, 1.0), 2),
        "recommendation": "Increase partitions" if spill_fraction > 0.3 
                          else "Spill is minor"
    }

The Spark UI reports two spill metrics: Memory Bytes Spilled (the volume of data that was in memory before spilling — this is the uncompressed size) and Disk Bytes Spilled (the volume of data written to disk after serialization and compression — typically 30–50% of the memory size). GridUnion's diagnostic threshold: if Disk Bytes Spilled exceeds 20% of the stage's Shuffle Read size, spill is a significant cost contributor and mitigation is warranted. Below 20%, the spill overhead is minor relative to the shuffle I/O and does not justify optimization effort. The threshold is empirical — derived from profiling GridUnion's top-20 pipelines and correlating spill fraction with stage duration improvement after mitigation.
Mitigation Hierarchy

GridUnion's spill mitigation follows a priority hierarchy. First: increase partition count. More partitions means less data per task, which means smaller in-memory buffers that fit within the executor's memory allocation. This is the cheapest fix — a single configuration change. Second: prune columns before the memory-intensive operation. A sort on 15 columns requires 15x the buffer memory of a sort on 1 column. If the downstream operation only needs 5 columns, projecting before the sort reduces the buffer by 67%. Third: reduce the data volume before the memory-intensive operation. A filter that eliminates 80% of rows before a groupBy reduces the aggregation buffer by 80%. Fourth (last resort): rethink the operation. A sort-merge join that spills on every run might be better replaced by a bucketed join (no sort needed) or a broadcast join (if the small side fits in memory). Each mitigation level has a higher implementation cost than the previous one.

blocks.title: Partition count is the primary spill lever in Fabric
blocks.content: In standalone Spark, the spill mitigation hierarchy starts with 'increase executor memory.' In Fabric, that option is not available. But the effect is equivalent: doubling the partition count halves the per-task data volume, which is the same as doubling the per-task memory allocation. GridUnion's rule of thumb: target per-task input size of 200–400 MB for the F128 capacity. At 200 MB per task with 4 GB of execution memory, the sort buffer has approximately 10x headroom — sufficient for the sort to complete without spilling. When a stage shows spill, the first diagnostic check is per-task input size: if it exceeds 400 MB, increase the partition count. If it is below 400 MB and spill still occurs, the problem is not data volume — it is memory-intensive operations (large hash tables, wide sorts) that need the column pruning or operational redesign from levels 2–4.

Spark's Tungsten execution engine optimizes CPU and memory efficiency through two mechanisms: off-heap memory management (storing data in binary format outside the JVM heap to reduce garbage collection overhead) and whole-stage code generation (compiling chains of operators into a single optimized Java function that processes rows without the overhead of virtual method calls between operators). Tungsten is why Spark is orders of magnitude faster than naive MapReduce: instead of interpreting each operator separately and passing Java objects between them, Tungsten generates optimized bytecode that processes rows through the entire operator pipeline in a tight loop. Understanding Tungsten explains why some operations are much faster than they 'should' be — and why certain code patterns break Tungsten's optimizations.
Whole-Stage Code Generation

Whole-stage code generation (codegen) fuses multiple operators within a stage into a single Java function. Instead of calling filter() as a method, then project() as a method, then aggregate() as a method — with object creation and virtual dispatch at each boundary — codegen produces a single function that evaluates the filter condition, extracts the projected columns, and updates the aggregate buffer in one tight loop. The Spark UI shows codegen in the SQL tab: operators that participate in codegen are grouped under a WholeStageCodegen node with a codegen ID. Operators outside codegen (such as external sorts, broadcast exchanges, and Python UDFs) appear as separate nodes without a codegen wrapper.
What Breaks Codegen

rows: Python UDFs
Codegen operates on JVM bytecode; Python UDFs require serialization to Python, execution in a separate process, and deserialization back to JVM
10–100x slower than equivalent built-in functions for the same logic
Replace Python UDFs with built-in Spark SQL functions; use Pandas UDFs (vectorized) for unavoidable custom logic
Very wide schemas (100+ columns)
The generated code for 100+ columns exceeds the JVM's method size limit (64KB bytecode)
Falls back to interpreted execution — typically 2–5x slower
Project to needed columns before codegen-intensive operations; avoid SELECT *
Complex expression trees (deeply nested CASE WHEN)
Deep nesting produces deeply recursive code that exceeds codegen limits
Falls back to interpreted execution for the specific expression
Simplify complex expressions by breaking them into intermediate columns
Certain join types (Cartesian, nested loop)
These join types do not have codegen implementations
Falls back to interpreted execution for the join operator
Avoid Cartesian joins; ensure join conditions produce hash or sort-merge plans

visual-recommendation.description: A comparison diagram showing two execution paths for the same filter→project→aggregate pipeline: (left) interpreted execution with separate operator nodes, virtual method calls between them, and Java object creation at each boundary; (right) codegen execution with a single fused function, no virtual dispatch, and direct memory access. Annotated with approximate CPU time per million rows for each path.

blocks.content: Verifying Codegen in the Spark UI

The Spark UI's SQL/DataFrame tab shows the physical plan with codegen annotations. Look for WholeStageCodegen nodes — each one represents a fused operator chain. If an operator appears outside a WholeStageCodegen node (as a standalone operator between two codegen nodes), it broke the codegen chain. GridUnion's codegen audit checks the top-20 notebooks quarterly: the audit script parses the SQL plan, identifies operators outside codegen chains, and reports them as codegen breaks. Common findings: Python UDFs in transformation notebooks (replaced with built-in functions in 8 of 10 cases), and SELECT * patterns in analytics notebooks (replaced with explicit column lists). Each codegen break that is fixed reduces the affected stage's duration by 2–5x for CPU-intensive operations.

blocks.title: Codegen is Spark’s secret weapon — do not accidentally disable it
blocks.content: Most Spark engineers never think about codegen because it works transparently. But a single Python UDF in a transformation chain breaks the codegen pipeline for the entire stage — the fused function cannot include the Python call, so the chain is split into three interpreted phases (before UDF, UDF, after UDF). The performance impact is invisible in the plan (the plan looks the same with or without codegen) but dramatic in the Spark UI (task duration increases 3–10x). GridUnion's coding standard: no Python UDFs in production notebooks without a documented justification that includes the performance cost. The justification must show that the UDF's logic cannot be expressed with built-in functions — and in GridUnion's experience, 80% of Python UDFs can be replaced.

A stage's duration equals its slowest task's duration. If 199 of 200 tasks complete in 5 seconds but one task takes 5 minutes (because of a slow executor, a GC pause, or a hardware issue), the stage takes 5 minutes — the 199 fast tasks sit idle while the straggler finishes. Speculative execution addresses this: when Spark detects that a task is running significantly slower than its peers, it launches a duplicate of that task on a different executor and takes the result from whichever copy finishes first. The straggler is neutralized because the duplicate (running on a healthy executor) finishes in 5 seconds — the stage duration drops from 5 minutes to 5 seconds. The trade-off is CU waste: the straggler's original copy continues running (consuming CUs) even after the duplicate finishes.
How Speculation Works

Speculation is triggered when a task's running time exceeds spark.speculation.multiplier (default 3.0) times the median task duration for the stage, AND the task has been running for at least spark.speculation.interval (default 100ms). When triggered, Spark launches a speculative copy of the slow task on a different executor. Both copies run simultaneously. When either copy completes, the other is killed. The speculative copy reads the same input data (from OneLake or shuffle files) and produces the same output — the copies are deterministic. The Spark UI shows speculative tasks in the task list with a 'SPECULATIVE' annotation, and the killed copy appears as 'KILLED (by speculation).'
Speculation in Fabric's Managed Environment

In a standalone Spark cluster, speculation is commonly enabled to handle hardware variability — a disk failure, a network partition, or a noisy neighbor on a shared VM can slow one executor. In Fabric's managed environment, hardware variability is lower (Microsoft manages the infrastructure), but speculation is still valuable for two scenarios. First, GC-induced stragglers: an executor that triggers a full garbage collection pauses for 5–30 seconds, stalling all tasks on that executor. Speculation detects the stalled tasks and re-launches them on other executors. Second, data-skew stragglers: even with AQE's skew splitting, moderate skew (2–3x the median) may not trigger splitting but still produces tasks that are 2–3x slower than the median. Speculation handles this moderate skew efficiently without the overhead of AQE's splitting and replication.
When to Enable Speculation

GridUnion's speculation policy: disabled by default (the CU waste from speculative copies is unnecessary for well-partitioned pipelines), enabled selectively for two types of notebooks. First, the quarterly regulatory pipeline — a 90-minute notebook where a single straggler can extend the runtime by 20 minutes. The quarterly deadline makes the CU waste of speculation acceptable because the alternative (a 20-minute delay) has higher business cost than the CU waste. Second, any notebook where the task duration histogram (from the Spark UI) shows consistent straggler behavior: a max/P50 ratio above 5 that persists across multiple runs despite partition tuning. If the straggler is caused by data skew, Module 4's skew-resistant design is the permanent fix. If the straggler is caused by executor-level issues (GC, transient slowness), speculation is the appropriate mitigation.

blocks.title: Speculation treats the symptom, not the cause
blocks.content: A task that takes 5 minutes instead of 5 seconds has a cause: data skew, memory pressure, GC pause, or a large intermediate result. Speculation masks the cause by completing a duplicate task on a healthy executor — the stage finishes quickly, but the root cause remains. The straggler will recur on every future run, and the speculative copy will be needed every time — consuming extra CUs indefinitely. GridUnion's policy: when speculation fires more than 3 times in a month for the same stage, investigate the root cause. Speculation is the stopgap that keeps the pipeline within SLA while the team diagnoses and fixes the underlying problem.

Module 2 provided the diagnostic workflow for plan-level problems: wrong statistics, missing pushdown, suboptimal join strategy. This lesson provides the diagnostic workflow for execution-level problems: the plan is correct, but the runtime is unexpectedly slow. The notebook's explain() output shows an optimal plan — broadcast join on the small side, partition pruning active, column pruning verified. But the Spark UI shows a 12-minute runtime instead of the expected 3 minutes. The plan cannot explain the 9-minute gap. The execution-level diagnostic workflow fills that gap by examining stage durations, task distributions, memory metrics, and shuffle behavior to identify the runtime bottleneck.
The Execution Diagnostic Workflow

items: Identify the slow stage. Open the Spark UI's Stages tab, sort by duration, and find the stage that dominates the total runtime. If one stage takes 10 minutes and all others take 30 seconds, that stage is the investigation target. Note the stage's input size, shuffle read/write, and task count.

Examine the task duration distribution. In the slow stage's detail view, look at the task duration summary (min, P25, P50, P75, max). If max >> P50, the problem is a straggler (one or a few tasks are much slower than the rest). If all tasks are uniformly slow, the problem is systemic (every task is doing too much work).

Check for spill. If Shuffle Spill (Disk) is non-zero, the stage is memory-constrained. Compute the per-task input size (stage input / task count). If it exceeds 400 MB on F128, increase partition count. If it is below 400 MB, check for wide sorts or large hash tables.

Check for GC pressure. In the Executors tab, compare GC Time to Task Time for the executors that ran the slow stage's tasks. If GC Time exceeds 10% of Task Time, the executor's heap is full. Reduce cached data, increase partition count, or check for large broadcast variables.

Check for shuffle bottleneck. If Shuffle Read Blocked Time exceeds 30% of task duration, the task is waiting for shuffle data from a previous stage. The previous stage may be slow, the shuffle files may be fragmented, or the network bandwidth may be saturated. Check the upstream stage's completion time — if it overlaps with the downstream stage's blocked time, the problem is upstream latency.

Check for codegen breaks. If the SQL plan shows operators outside WholeStageCodegen nodes, the broken codegen may be the bottleneck — especially for CPU-intensive stages with large data volumes. Replace the codegen breaker (Python UDF, wide schema, complex expression) and remeasure.

blocks.content: GridUnion's 12-minute Silver notebook slowdown was diagnosed using this workflow. Step 1: stage 2 (the MERGE join) took 10.5 minutes. Step 2: the task duration histogram showed 199 tasks at 3 seconds and 1 task at 10.5 minutes — a straggler. Step 3: spill was zero — memory was not the issue. Step 4: GC time was normal. Step 5: shuffle blocked time was zero. Step 6: codegen was intact. The straggler was not caused by any standard execution problem. Further investigation revealed: the straggler task processed the German country partition, which contained 40% of the data. The per-task data volume was 2.4 GB (German partition) vs 200 MB (average). The fix: increase the MERGE's shuffle partition count from 200 to 800, splitting the German partition into 4 sub-partitions of 600 MB each. The stage duration dropped from 10.5 minutes to 45 seconds.

blocks.title: The plan is necessary but not sufficient for performance diagnosis
blocks.content: Module 2's plan-level diagnosis catches optimizer problems: wrong statistics, missing pushdown, suboptimal join strategy. Module 6's execution-level diagnosis catches runtime problems: spill, GC pressure, straggler tasks, shuffle bottlenecks, codegen breaks. The complete diagnostic covers both levels: if the plan is suboptimal, fix the plan first (Module 2). If the plan is optimal but the runtime is slow, diagnose the execution (this module). GridUnion's performance diagnostic checklist starts with the plan (was the plan correct?) and proceeds to execution (was the execution efficient?). Approximately 60% of performance incidents are plan-level problems (fixable with statistics or configuration changes) and 40% are execution-level problems (fixable with partition tuning, memory optimization, or codegen restoration).

The previous nine lessons covered the execution pipeline from physical plan to task completion, the DAGScheduler's stage boundary analysis with pipelining, the TaskScheduler's executor assignment with Fabric-specific locality considerations, shuffle mechanics with map-side write and reduce-side read, memory management with the unified pool and Fabric's fixed-executor constraint, spill mechanics with the mitigation hierarchy, Tungsten's code generation with codegen break identification, speculative execution for straggler mitigation, and the complete execution-level diagnostic workflow. This lesson synthesizes everything into GridUnion's execution engine playbook — the systematic approach the platform team uses for execution-level performance engineering.
The Performance Engineering Process

rows: 1. Plan verification
explain('cost'), plan fingerprint
Verify the plan is optimal per Module 2's diagnostic workflow
Plan is correct → proceed to execution diagnosis. Plan is wrong → fix plan first
2. Stage identification
Spark UI Stages tab
Identify the dominant stage by duration
Focus investigation on the stage that determines total runtime
3. Task analysis
Spark UI Stage detail → task histogram
Classify: straggler (max >> P50) or systemic (all tasks slow)
Straggler → investigate skew/GC/speculation. Systemic → investigate spill/codegen/partition count
4. Memory check
Spark UI → Spill metrics, GC Time
Quantify spill volume and GC overhead
Spill > 20% of shuffle → increase partitions. GC > 10% → reduce memory pressure
5. Shuffle check
Spark UI SQL tab → shuffle metrics
Measure total shuffle volume and blocked time
Shuffle volume excessive → apply Module 4 strategies. Blocked time high → investigate upstream stage
6. Codegen check
Spark UI SQL tab → WholeStageCodegen nodes
Identify operators outside codegen chains
Codegen break found → replace UDF or simplify expression. No breaks → codegen is healthy

blocks.content: What Comes Next

Module 7 covers the storage and I/O layer — OneLake's architecture, Parquet format internals, Delta Lake transaction log mechanics, and V-Order optimization. Module 6 explained how Spark processes data once it is in memory (stages, tasks, shuffle, memory management, codegen). Module 7 explains how data gets into memory in the first place (OneLake reads, Parquet decoding, Delta file selection) and how the storage format's design determines I/O efficiency. Together, Modules 2 (optimizer), 6 (execution engine), and 7 (storage) cover the complete Spark processing stack from query to result — the three layers that determine whether a notebook takes 1 minute or 1 hour.

blocks.title: Execution engine knowledge separates senior engineers from senior debugging
blocks.content: A mid-level engineer who encounters a slow notebook checks the execution plan, verifies pushdown, and opens a support ticket if the plan looks correct but the runtime is slow. A senior engineer who encounters the same situation opens the Spark UI, identifies the slow stage, examines the task histogram, checks for spill and GC pressure, verifies codegen, and diagnoses the root cause in 15 minutes. The difference is not intelligence — it is the mental model of how the execution engine works. The nine lessons in this module built that mental model: stages are separated by shuffles, tasks are assigned to executor slots, memory is shared between execution and storage, spill is the overflow mechanism when memory is insufficient, and codegen fuses operators into tight loops. With this model, the Spark UI's metrics become diagnostic signals rather than opaque numbers.

chapters.title: Spark Internals — Storage and I/O
chapters.description: Understand how Fabric stores and reads data at the format level — Parquet encoding, Delta Lake transaction log internals, OneLake storage architecture, V-Order optimization mechanics, and the diagnostic techniques for I/O-bound performance problems.

blocks.content: Module 1 introduced OneLake as the unified storage layer and covered shortcuts for cross-workspace access. This lesson goes inside OneLake's architecture: the ADLS Gen2 foundation, the namespace hierarchy that maps Fabric items to storage containers, the caching layer that accelerates repeat reads, and the I/O characteristics that determine how fast Spark can scan data. At GridUnion's scale — 15 TB across 80+ workspaces in 2 Azure regions — OneLake's storage behavior is a performance-critical architectural concern. The difference between a cache-warm scan (200 MB/s per executor) and a cache-cold scan (80 MB/s per executor) determines whether the German Silver notebook takes 3 minutes or 8 minutes. Understanding OneLake's caching behavior explains this variability and enables proactive cache management.
The ADLS Gen2 Foundation

OneLake is built on Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 — the same object storage that powers standalone Azure data lakes. Each Fabric workspace maps to an ADLS container, each Lakehouse maps to a directory within that container, and each Delta table maps to a subdirectory containing the Parquet files and the _delta_log directory. The ADLS Gen2 layer provides the durability (triple replication within the region), the RBAC integration (Azure AD-based authentication), and the hierarchical namespace (directory-level operations) that Fabric inherits. Understanding this mapping matters for diagnostics: when OneLake performance degrades, the root cause may be at the ADLS Gen2 layer (storage throttling, regional outage) rather than the Fabric layer. GridUnion's platform team monitors both Fabric-level metrics (capacity CU utilization) and ADLS-level metrics (storage latency, throttling events) to distinguish between the two.
The Caching Layer

OneLake caches frequently-read data on local SSD storage attached to the Fabric capacity's compute nodes. The first read of a Parquet file fetches from ADLS Gen2 (cache-cold: 60–100 MB/s per executor, depending on file size and network conditions). Subsequent reads of the same file within the cache retention window hit the local SSD cache (cache-warm: 150–250 MB/s per executor). The cache is per-capacity — data cached by one workspace's notebook is available to other notebooks on the same capacity that read the same files. GridUnion's Silver notebook benefits significantly from caching: the first run after a Sunday maintenance window (which may evict cache entries) takes 5 minutes, while subsequent daily runs take 3 minutes because the Silver Parquet files are cache-warm from the previous day's run.
Cross-Region I/O Characteristics

rows: Same-region, cache-warm
~5 ms/MB
Served from local SSD
German Silver notebook reading from ws_de_silver (both in EU North)
Same-region, cache-cold
~12 ms/MB
Fetched from ADLS, cached for subsequent reads
First run after maintenance window or new file from OPTIMIZE
Cross-region, cache-warm
~15 ms/MB
Served from local SSD (cross-region data cached locally after first read)
Platinum notebook reading French data shortcut (EU West → EU North) after first run
Cross-region, cache-cold
~35 ms/MB
Fetched from remote ADLS, cached locally
First read of French data from a German workspace — 3x slower than same-region cold
