visual-recommendation.description: A timeline/calendar diagram showing the security operations cadence: continuous activities (real-time alerts) at the top, daily (reconciliation), weekly (scorecard), quarterly (access review, tabletop), and annual (regulatory evidence) activities layered below. Each activity is color-coded by responsible team (security=red, compliance=blue, platform=green, automation=gray).

blocks.content: What Comes Next

Module 9 covers advanced streaming architecture — the Eventstream topologies, multi-stream correlation, exactly-once delivery, and KQL at enterprise scale that extend GridUnion's batch platform into real-time operations. Security (this module) establishes the governance infrastructure that streaming must operate within: Eventstream data inherits the sensitivity labels and access controls of its source, KQL databases are governed by the same workspace isolation and audit logging, and streaming incidents are managed through the same security incident response process. The streaming architecture (Module 9) builds on the security posture (Module 8) — not alongside it.

blocks.title: Security is the platform’s immune system — it must be always on, not periodically checked
blocks.content: The mid-level track's quarterly security review was a periodic health check: every 3 months, the compliance team reviewed labels, access grants, and audit logs. At senior scale, 3 months between checks is 90 days of undetected drift, 90 days of unreviewed anomalies, and 90 days of potential compliance gaps. GridUnion's security operations are continuous: real-time alerts detect anomalies within minutes, nightly reconciliation detects drift within 24 hours, and the weekly scorecard tracks posture trends. The quarterly activities (access reviews, tabletop exercises, regulatory evidence) are deep assessments, not first-pass detections. The continuous monitoring catches problems early; the quarterly activities validate the completeness of the continuous monitoring. Both are necessary, and neither is sufficient alone.

chapters.title: Advanced Streaming Architecture
chapters.description: Design enterprise-grade streaming architectures in Fabric — complex Eventstream topologies, multi-stream correlation, exactly-once delivery guarantees, KQL at enterprise scale, and the operational patterns for streaming systems that process millions of events per day across multiple countries.

blocks.content: The mid-level track built a single Eventstream processing 43 million SCADA telemetry events per day from 5,000 turbines in one country. The architecture was straightforward: one Event Hub source, one Eventstream with dual routing (KQL database for real-time queries, Bronze Lakehouse for batch consolidation), one KQL database, and one Real-Time Dashboard. At GridUnion's scale — 20,000 turbines across 4 countries, 172 million events per day, 4 independent Event Hub sources with country-specific schemas, a unified KQL Eventhouse for cross-country correlation, and streaming SLAs that vary by country — the single-Eventstream model cannot serve. This module redesigns the streaming architecture for enterprise scale: multiple Eventstreams with routing logic, multi-stream correlation for compound anomaly detection, exactly-once delivery guarantees, and the operational infrastructure for continuous streaming across a multi-country platform.
The Scale Shift

rows: Turbines
5,000 in 1 country
20,000 across 4 countries
4 independent Event Hub sources with country-specific authentication and schema
Event volume
43M events/day (~500/sec)
172M events/day (~2,000/sec)
Single Eventstream throughput is sufficient but multi-stream routing adds complexity
Schemas
1 SCADA schema (unified)
4 country schemas (overlapping but not identical — Germany includes grid_zone_id, France includes tariff_classification)
Schema harmonization layer in the Eventstream or at KQL ingestion
KQL databases
1 database (kqldb_turbine_telemetry)
1 Eventhouse with 4 country databases + 1 cross-country correlation database
Cross-database KQL queries for multi-country anomaly detection
SLAs
60-second ingestion lag
30-second for Germany (BNetzA real-time monitoring), 60-second for others
Country-specific routing priorities in the Eventstream topology
Correlation
Single-stream anomaly detection (temperature spike)
Multi-stream correlation (turbine temp + grid frequency + weather data)
Stream-to-stream joins in KQL with time-windowed correlation
DR
Not addressed
Streaming must survive regional outages — EU North failure must not stop French turbine monitoring
Cross-region Eventstream failover and KQL replication

visual-recommendation.description: A side-by-side comparison diagram: (left) the mid-level single-Eventstream architecture with one source, one Eventstream, one KQL DB, and one dashboard; (right) the senior-level multi-country architecture with 4 Event Hub sources, a routing Eventstream, 4+1 KQL databases in a unified Eventhouse, cross-database correlation queries, and country-specific dashboards. Scale annotations show the 4x increase in event volume and the architectural components that handle it.

blocks.content: The Enterprise Streaming Architecture

GridUnion's streaming architecture has four layers. The ingestion layer consists of 4 country-level Event Hubs that receive SCADA telemetry from country-specific IoT gateways. The routing layer consists of Eventstreams that transform, harmonize schemas, and route events to their destinations. The storage layer consists of a unified Eventhouse with 5 KQL databases (4 country-specific for regulatory compliance, 1 cross-country for correlation analytics) and the Bronze Lakehouse for batch consolidation. The consumption layer consists of KQL queries, Real-Time Dashboards per country, and the cross-country anomaly detection system. Each layer is independently scalable and independently monitorable — a failure in the French ingestion layer does not affect German event processing.

blocks.title: Enterprise streaming is not bigger batch — it is a different architecture
blocks.content: The temptation at enterprise scale is to treat streaming as fast batch: increase the Eventstream throughput, add more KQL databases, and scale the same single-stream pattern. This approach fails because enterprise streaming problems are architectural, not throughput-related. Multi-country schema harmonization, cross-stream correlation with time windows, exactly-once delivery across 4 independent sources, country-level SLA differentiation, and streaming disaster recovery — none of these are solved by scaling a single Eventstream. They require a multi-layer architecture with routing, correlation, and operational infrastructure that the mid-level single-stream model does not provide.

The mid-level Eventstream had a simple topology: one source (Event Hub) connected to two destinations (KQL database, Bronze Lakehouse). At GridUnion's scale, the Eventstream topology includes fan-in patterns (4 country Event Hubs feeding into a unified routing Eventstream), fan-out patterns (one routing Eventstream distributing events to 5 KQL databases, the Bronze Lakehouse, and an external analytics system), and transformation nodes (schema harmonization, enrichment with reference data, and country-level routing rules that direct events to the correct country database). The topology is a graph, not a pipeline — events flow through multiple paths simultaneously, and the routing logic determines which path each event takes.
The Routing Eventstream

GridUnion's central component is the routing Eventstream (es_gu_scada_router) that sits between the country Event Hubs and the KQL databases. The routing Eventstream performs three operations on every event. First, schema harmonization: each country's SCADA payload has a slightly different schema (Germany includes grid_zone_id, France includes tariff_classification). The routing Eventstream maps each country's schema to a unified schema with a superset of all fields, filling missing fields with null. Second, enrichment: the routing Eventstream looks up the turbine's metadata (capacity rating, installation date, grid zone) from a reference table and appends it to the event. Third, routing: the event is routed to the correct country-specific KQL database (based on the country field) AND to the cross-country correlation database (all events, regardless of country).
Fan-In and Fan-Out Patterns

blocks.title: GridUnion's Eventstream topology
blocks.caption: The topology shows the fan-in from 4 country Event Hubs into the routing Eventstream, and the fan-out from the router to 5 KQL databases (4 country-specific + 1 cross-country correlation) and the Bronze Lakehouse. The router performs schema harmonization, enrichment, and routing. Total throughput: 2,000 events/second across all sources.

visual-recommendation.description: A detailed network diagram showing the complete Eventstream topology with throughput annotations on each link, the three operations performed by the routing Eventstream (harmonize, enrich, route), and the fan-out destinations. Each destination is color-coded by country (DE=blue, FR=red, NL=orange, LU=green, cross-country=purple).

blocks.title: The routing Eventstream is a single point of failure
blocks.content: If es_gu_scada_router fails, all 4 countries lose streaming data simultaneously. GridUnion mitigates this with two mechanisms. First, the Event Hubs retain events for 24 hours — if the router is down for 2 hours, the events are not lost; they queue in the Event Hubs and are processed when the router recovers. Second, the router Eventstream has a failover configuration: a standby Eventstream (es_gu_scada_router_standby) with identical configuration is maintained but inactive. If the primary router's health check fails for 5 consecutive minutes, the Fabric pipeline that monitors streaming health switches the Event Hub connections to the standby router. The failover takes approximately 2 minutes and results in a brief duplication window (events processed by both the dying primary and the activating standby) that the deduplication logic in the KQL databases handles.

Single-stream anomaly detection identifies problems within one data source: a turbine temperature spike, an unusual RPM reading, a sensor dropout. Multi-stream correlation identifies compound anomalies that are only visible when multiple streams are analyzed together: a turbine temperature spike (SCADA stream) occurring simultaneously with a grid frequency deviation (grid stability stream) and a sudden wind speed drop (weather stream) indicates a potential cascading failure — a condition that no single stream would flag independently. GridUnion's cross-country correlation database (kqldb_gu_correlate) receives events from all three streams and executes time-windowed correlation queries that detect these compound patterns.
The Three Correlation Streams

rows: SCADA telemetry
Turbine IoT gateways (4 country Event Hubs)
172M events/day, 2,000/sec
turbine_id, timestamp, temperature, rpm, power_output, vibration
Country Event Hub → routing Eventstream → kqldb_gu_correlate
Grid stability
National grid operators (4 API feeds, polled every 10 sec)
345,600 events/day, 4/sec
grid_zone, timestamp, frequency_hz, voltage_kv, load_mw
Polling pipeline → dedicated Eventstream → kqldb_gu_correlate
Weather observations
Meteorological services (hourly observations at 120 weather stations)
2,880 events/day
station_id, timestamp, wind_speed_ms, wind_direction, temperature_c, humidity
API polling pipeline → dedicated Eventstream → kqldb_gu_correlate

blocks.content: Time-Windowed Correlation in KQL

// Compound anomaly detection: turbine temp spike + grid frequency deviation + wind drop
// within a 5-minute correlation window

let scada_anomalies = ScadaTelemetry
    | where timestamp > ago(10m)
    | summarize avg_temp = avg(temperature), max_temp = max(temperature) 
        by turbine_id, grid_zone, bin(timestamp, 5m)
    | where max_temp > avg_temp * 1.5;  // 50% above average = temperature anomaly

let grid_anomalies = GridStability
    | where timestamp > ago(10m)
    | where abs(frequency_hz - 50.0) > 0.1;  // >0.1 Hz deviation from 50 Hz nominal

let weather_changes = WeatherObservations
    | where timestamp > ago(1h)
    | summarize wind_drop = max(wind_speed_ms) - min(wind_speed_ms)
        by station_id, bin(timestamp, 30m)
    | where wind_drop > 8;  // >8 m/s drop in 30 minutes = sudden wind change

// Correlate: turbine anomaly + grid anomaly in same zone within 5 minutes
scada_anomalies
| join kind=inner grid_anomalies on grid_zone
| where abs(datetime_diff('minute', scada_anomalies.timestamp, grid_anomalies.timestamp)) <= 5
| join kind=leftouter (
    weather_changes | extend grid_zone = station_to_zone(station_id)
  ) on grid_zone
| project turbine_id, grid_zone, scada_time = scada_anomalies.timestamp,
    max_temp, frequency_hz, wind_drop
| extend severity = iff(isnotnull(wind_drop), "CRITICAL", "HIGH")

The correlation query operates on three tables in the kqldb_gu_correlate database. The SCADA anomalies identify turbines with temperature spikes in the past 10 minutes. The grid anomalies identify grid zones with frequency deviations. The weather changes identify stations with sudden wind drops. The correlation join matches SCADA anomalies with grid anomalies in the same grid zone within a 5-minute window, then optionally joins weather data for the same zone. A match on all three streams (temperature spike + frequency deviation + wind drop) produces a CRITICAL severity alert; a match on SCADA + grid without weather produces a HIGH severity alert. The query runs every 5 minutes as a KQL scheduled query, and results are published to the Real-Time Dashboard's alert panel.

blocks.title: Correlation finds the signal that no single stream contains
blocks.content: A turbine temperature spike alone occurs 50–100 times per day across 20,000 turbines — most are transient and benign. A grid frequency deviation alone occurs 10–20 times per day — most are brief and self-correcting. But a turbine temperature spike coinciding with a grid frequency deviation in the same zone within 5 minutes occurs 0–2 times per month — and when it does, it indicates a genuine grid stability risk that requires operator intervention. The correlation's value is not detecting events (each stream detects its own events) but filtering the noise: 100 SCADA anomalies + 20 grid anomalies → 1–2 correlated alerts per month that actually require action. Without correlation, the operations team drowns in false positives. With correlation, they receive actionable alerts.

At-least-once delivery is easy: if processing fails, replay the events. The result may contain duplicates, but no data is lost. Exactly-once delivery is hard: every event must be processed exactly one time — no losses and no duplicates. For GridUnion's billing pipeline, duplicates mean double-counting meter readings, which produces incorrect invoices. For the regulatory submission, duplicates mean overstated consumption figures, which trigger regulatory scrutiny. Exactly-once delivery in Fabric's streaming stack requires coordinating three components: the Event Hub source (which provides at-least-once delivery with offset tracking), the Eventstream processing (which may replay events after a failure), and the destination (KQL database or Bronze Lakehouse, which must handle duplicate arrivals idempotently).
The Exactly-Once Chain

Fabric's Eventstream provides at-least-once delivery from Event Hub to destination: if the Eventstream fails and restarts, it replays events from the last committed offset, which may include events that were already delivered to the destination before the failure. The exactly-once guarantee must be implemented at the destination. For the KQL database, GridUnion uses ingestion-time deduplication: every event includes a unique event_id (generated by the IoT gateway), and the KQL ingestion mapping specifies event_id as the deduplication key with a 10-minute deduplication window. Events with the same event_id arriving within 10 minutes are deduplicated — only the first is ingested. For the Bronze Lakehouse, the batch consolidation notebook (which runs hourly) uses a MERGE with a match condition on event_id — events that already exist in the Bronze table are skipped, not duplicated.
The Verification Framework

The verification query compares the Event Hub's ingested message count (from Azure Monitor metrics) to the KQL database's row count per hour. A positive delta (more Event Hub events than KQL rows) indicates data loss — events that were ingested by the Event Hub but not delivered to KQL. A negative delta (more KQL rows than Event Hub events) indicates duplication — events that were delivered multiple times and the deduplication failed. GridUnion's verification runs daily and flags any hour with more than 0.1% discrepancy. In 18 months of operation, the verification has detected 3 data loss events (all caused by Eventstream restart delays during Fabric platform updates) and zero duplication events (the KQL deduplication has been 100% effective).
End-to-End Exactly-Once

The exactly-once chain from Event Hub to the Gold billing table spans four processing stages: Event Hub → Eventstream → KQL/Bronze → Silver MERGE → Gold aggregation. Each stage must preserve the exactly-once guarantee. The Event Hub provides offset-based replay (at-least-once). The KQL deduplication handles the replay duplicates at the first destination. The Bronze Lakehouse receives hourly batch writes from the consolidation notebook, which uses MERGE with event_id matching (idempotent). The Silver MERGE uses meter_id + date as the match key (events for the same meter on the same date update the existing row rather than creating a duplicate). The Gold aggregation reads from Silver, which is already deduplicated. The end-to-end guarantee is the composition of each stage's individual guarantee — and a failure in any stage's deduplication breaks the chain for all downstream consumers.

blocks.title: Exactly-once is a system property, not a component feature
blocks.content: No single Fabric component provides end-to-end exactly-once delivery. The Event Hub provides at-least-once. The Eventstream provides at-least-once. The KQL database provides deduplication within a window. The Delta MERGE provides idempotent writes. Exactly-once emerges from the correct composition of these components — each handling duplicates at its boundary. If any component's deduplication is misconfigured (the KQL deduplication window is too short, the MERGE match key is wrong, the event_id is not unique), duplicates propagate through the remaining stages and appear in the billing table. GridUnion's exactly-once verification (this lesson) is the end-to-end check that catches deduplication failures regardless of which component failed.

The mid-level track queried a single KQL database with hundreds of millions of rows. At GridUnion's scale, the Eventhouse contains 5 KQL databases with a combined 5+ billion rows spanning 30 days of retained data (country databases retain 30 days, the correlation database retains 7 days). The queries range from simple point lookups (a specific turbine's last 10 readings for the operations dashboard) to complex analytical queries (cross-country anomaly correlation across 172 million daily events). KQL performance at this scale depends on three factors: retention and caching configuration (which data is in hot cache vs cold storage), materialized views (pre-computed aggregations that accelerate repeated queries), and update policies (transformations applied at ingestion time that reduce query-time computation).
Hot and Cold Cache Tiering

KQL databases store data in two tiers: hot cache (SSD-backed, fast queries, expensive) and cold storage (Azure blob-backed, slower queries, cheaper). The hot cache retention determines how many days of data are served at full speed. GridUnion's configuration: the 4 country databases have 7-day hot cache (operators need fast access to the current week for real-time monitoring) and 30-day total retention (older data is available but queries are slower). The correlation database has 3-day hot cache (correlation queries operate on recent events only) and 7-day total retention (correlated anomalies older than 7 days are archived to the Bronze Lakehouse). The hot cache cost is proportional to the data volume held in cache — GridUnion's 7-day hot cache across all 4 country databases holds approximately 1.2 billion rows (8.4 TB uncompressed, approximately 2 TB compressed on SSD).
Materialized Views in KQL

The materialized view processes new data incrementally: as events arrive in the ScadaTelemetry table, the materialized view engine updates the hourly aggregation for the affected turbine and hour. The dashboard queries the materialized view instead of the base table — reading 3.4 million pre-computed hourly summary rows instead of scanning 1.2 billion raw events. The 350x reduction in query data volume reduces dashboard query latency from 8 seconds to 50 milliseconds — enabling the 30-second auto-refresh that the operations team requires. GridUnion maintains 4 materialized views in the Eventhouse: hourly turbine summary, daily country summary, rolling 7-day anomaly count, and the correlation alert history.
Update Policies

Update policies are KQL's equivalent of triggers: a function that runs automatically when new data is ingested into a source table, transforming and writing the result to a target table. GridUnion uses update policies for three purposes. First, schema normalization: the raw ingestion table receives events in the country-specific schema, and the update policy transforms them to the unified schema and writes to the country's normalized table. Second, anomaly flagging: the update policy evaluates each event against anomaly thresholds (temperature > 85°C, RPM deviation > 10%) and writes flagged events to an anomalies table with the anomaly type and severity. Third, event deduplication: the update policy checks the event_id against a deduplication window and drops duplicates before they reach the main table. Update policies shift computation from query time to ingestion time — the data is already transformed, flagged, and deduplicated when the first query runs against it.

blocks.title: Move computation from query time to ingestion time
blocks.content: At 172 million events per day, every transformation applied at query time is multiplied by the query frequency. If the operations dashboard queries every 30 seconds and each query applies a temperature conversion, the conversion runs 2,880 times per day on 172 million events — 496 billion conversions per day. If the same conversion is applied once at ingestion time via an update policy, it runs once per event — 172 million conversions per day. The 2,880x reduction in total computation is the economic argument for update policies. GridUnion's rule: any transformation that every consumer needs (unit conversion, schema normalization, anomaly flagging) belongs in an update policy, not in a query.

Schema evolution in batch pipelines is complex but manageable: the pipeline runs nightly, the engineer deploys the schema change before the next run, and the MERGE handles the new schema on the next execution. Schema evolution in streaming is harder because the stream is continuous — there is no 'next run' to deploy between. When a German turbine vendor adds a firmware_version field to the SCADA telemetry payload, events with the new field start arriving immediately on the Event Hub. The Eventstream's ingestion mapping does not know about the new field. The KQL database's table schema does not include it. The update policy's transformation logic does not reference it. The new field is silently dropped unless all three components are updated — and they must be updated without stopping the stream.
The Hot Schema Change Pattern

items: Detection: the schema drift detection system (Module 5, lesson 8) monitors Event Hub message schemas. When a new field appears in the incoming events, the system alerts the platform team with the field name, type, country of origin, and first-seen timestamp. Detection target: within 15 minutes of the first new-schema event.

Assessment: the platform team evaluates the change. Is the new field needed by any downstream consumer? If yes, proceed with full schema evolution. If no (the field is a debug field or a vendor-specific diagnostic), configure the Eventstream to drop the field and log the decision.

KQL table update: add the new column to the KQL database table using .alter table command. This is a non-breaking, online operation — existing queries continue to work (the new column returns null for events ingested before the change). No stream restart required.

Eventstream mapping update: update the Eventstream's ingestion mapping to include the new field. This requires the Eventstream to re-read its configuration, which happens automatically within 5 minutes or can be triggered manually. During the re-read, events continue flowing with the old mapping (the new field is dropped), then switch to the new mapping (the new field is ingested). No data loss, brief inconsistency window.

Update policy adjustment: if the update policy references specific columns, add the new field to the policy's transformation logic. If the policy uses a wildcard schema, no change is needed. The policy update is an online operation.
Schema registry update: register the new schema version in the platform workspace's schema registry (Module 5, lesson 2) and notify affected consumers per the contract process.

blocks.content: The hot schema change pattern completes the evolution without stopping the stream. The total evolution time from detection to full ingestion is approximately 30–45 minutes: 15 minutes for detection, 5 minutes for assessment, 5 minutes for the KQL alter, 5 minutes for the Eventstream mapping update, and 5–10 minutes for the update policy and registry updates. During the evolution, events with the new field arrive but the new field is dropped (before step 4 completes). Events arriving after step 4 include the new field. The brief inconsistency window (30 minutes of events missing the new field) is acceptable for a non-breaking additive change. If the change is breaking (field renamed or removed), the evolution requires the full 90-day contract process from Module 5 — applied to the streaming pipeline with the additional constraint that the stream cannot be stopped.

blocks.title: Vendor schema changes arrive without notice — detect, do not assume
blocks.content: GridUnion's turbine vendors do not always notify when they update the SCADA telemetry schema. The firmware_version field appeared without any advance notice — the vendor's firmware update silently added the field to the IoT payload. Without schema drift detection, the new field would have been silently dropped by the Eventstream for weeks until someone noticed it in the raw Event Hub data. GridUnion's drift detection system (which compares incoming event schemas to the registered schema every 15 minutes) caught the change within 20 minutes of the first new-schema event. The lesson: in enterprise streaming, schema changes are detected by monitoring, not by vendor notification. Build the detection system; do not rely on the source to announce changes.

Stateless streaming operations (filter, project, enrich from a static lookup) process each event independently — they do not need to remember anything from previous events. Stateful streaming operations (running aggregations, deduplication, windowed joins) must maintain state across events — they remember the running total, the set of seen event IDs, or the join buffer for unmatched events. State management determines the memory cost and the failure recovery behavior of stateful operations. At GridUnion's scale — 20,000 turbine_ids × 30-day materialized view retention — the state can grow to gigabytes, and state management becomes a capacity planning concern.
State in KQL Materialized Views

KQL's materialized views maintain incremental state: for each combination of group-by keys (turbine_id, country, grid_zone, hourly bin), the view stores the running aggregation result. As new events arrive, the view updates the relevant group's aggregation without re-processing historical events. The state size is proportional to the cardinality of the group-by keys × the retention period: 20,000 turbines × 24 hours/day × 30 days = 14.4 million state entries for the hourly turbine summary view. Each state entry is approximately 200 bytes (the aggregated fields plus the keys), totaling approximately 2.9 GB of materialized view state. This state is stored in the KQL database's hot cache and must be accounted for in the cache capacity planning.
Deduplication State

The deduplication window (lesson 4) maintains state for every event_id seen within the deduplication period. At 172 million events per day with a 10-minute deduplication window, the state holds approximately 1.2 million event IDs (10 minutes × 2,000 events/second). Each event_id is a 36-character UUID consuming approximately 100 bytes with overhead — totaling 120 MB of deduplication state. This is manageable. But if the deduplication window is increased to 1 hour (to handle longer Eventstream recovery times), the state grows to 7.2 million event IDs (720 MB). And if the deduplication window is set to 24 hours (to handle a full Event Hub retention replay), the state grows to 172 million event IDs (17 GB) — exceeding a single executor's memory. GridUnion's 10-minute window is a deliberate trade-off: short enough to keep state small, long enough to handle routine Eventstream restarts (which complete within 5 minutes).
State Size Monitoring

GridUnion monitors streaming state size using KQL's .show materialized-views command, which reports each view's data size, row count, and processing lag. The monitoring notebook (part of the daily governance pipeline) compares each view's state size to its projected size (cardinality × retention × entry size) and alerts when actual state exceeds the projection by more than 20%. A state size exceeding the projection indicates either higher-than-expected event cardinality (more turbine_ids than planned) or a state leak (failed state cleanup for expired entries). The monitoring also tracks the materialized view's processing lag — the time between the most recent event in the source table and the most recent event processed by the view. A growing lag indicates that the view's incremental computation cannot keep pace with the ingestion rate — a capacity warning that requires either reducing the view's complexity or increasing the Eventhouse capacity.

blocks.title: State size determines streaming capacity requirements
blocks.content: Batch pipeline capacity is determined by data volume and processing time — you can estimate CU requirements from the data size and the notebook's profiled duration. Streaming capacity is determined by data volume and state size — the hot cache must hold both the raw data and the materialized view state simultaneously. GridUnion's streaming capacity planning accounts for both: 2 TB of 7-day raw data cache + 3 GB of materialized view state + 120 MB of deduplication state + 20% headroom = approximately 2.4 TB of hot cache requirement. The F32 streaming capacity provides 4 TB of hot cache — sufficient with 40% headroom. If GridUnion adds a fifth country or increases retention to 14 days, the raw data cache doubles and a capacity upgrade is needed.

Batch pipelines have natural recovery points: if today's pipeline fails, re-run it tomorrow with both days' data. Streaming has no natural recovery point — it is continuous, and any interruption creates a data gap. When Fabric's EU North region experiences a 4-hour outage, the German and Dutch Eventstreams stop processing. The KQL databases in EU North stop ingesting. The Real-Time Dashboards for Germany and Netherlands show stale data. The operations team loses real-time visibility into 12,000 turbines. Streaming disaster recovery ensures that this outage does not become a data loss event and that real-time monitoring recovers within the RPO/RTO targets.
RPO and RTO for Streaming

rows: Event Hub
0 (no data loss — events retained for 24 hours)
0 (Event Hub in unaffected region continues operation)
Event Hub geo-disaster recovery with paired regions (EU North paired with EU West)
Eventstream
0–10 minutes (events queue in Event Hub during outage)
5–15 minutes (standby Eventstream activates)
Standby Eventstream in secondary region; failover triggered by health monitor
KQL database
0–30 minutes (depends on replication lag)
15–30 minutes (follower database promoted to read-write)
KQL database follower configuration across regions; follower lags by replication interval
Real-Time Dashboard
Equal to KQL RPO
Immediate after KQL recovery (dashboard reads from the recovered KQL database)
Dashboard reconfigured to point to the promoted follower database
Bronze Lakehouse (batch consolidation)
0 (hourly consolidation replays from Event Hub on recovery)
1–2 hours (next scheduled consolidation run after recovery)
Consolidation notebook re-processes the outage window from Event Hub offsets

visual-recommendation.description: A disaster recovery architecture diagram showing the primary region (EU North) and secondary region (EU West) with the streaming components in each: Event Hubs with geo-DR pairing, primary and standby Eventstreams, leader and follower KQL databases with replication arrows showing the lag interval, and the failover control plane. The diagram annotates the RPO and RTO at each component with color-coded status indicators.

blocks.content: The KQL Follower Database Pattern

KQL's follower database feature creates a read-only replica of a database in a different Eventhouse — potentially in a different region. The follower continuously replicates data from the leader with a configurable lag (typically 5–30 minutes). During normal operation, the follower serves read-only queries (useful for offloading analytics queries from the real-time ingestion database). During a disaster, the follower can be promoted to an independent read-write database — but the promotion breaks the replication link permanently. After promotion, the follower is an independent database that must be re-configured as a new leader, and the failed original leader must be re-attached as a follower when it recovers. GridUnion maintains follower databases in EU West for the EU North KQL databases and vice versa — each country's real-time data is replicated to the other region.
The Failover Playbook

GridUnion's streaming failover playbook is a 7-step process executed by the on-call platform engineer when a regional outage is confirmed. Step 1: confirm the outage is regional (not a transient network issue) by checking Azure Service Health. Step 2: activate the standby Eventstream in the secondary region by switching the Event Hub consumer group. Step 3: promote the KQL follower databases to independent read-write databases. Step 4: update the Real-Time Dashboard connections to point to the promoted databases. Step 5: notify all streaming consumers that the data source has switched regions (latency characteristics may differ). Step 6: verify end-to-end event flow from Event Hub through the new Eventstream to the promoted KQL databases. Step 7: document the failover time, the data gap (if any), and initiate the recovery process for the failed region. The playbook is practiced quarterly in the tabletop exercises (Module 8, lesson 7).

blocks.title: Failover is not failback — the return to normal is harder than the switch
blocks.content: Promoting a KQL follower to an independent database is irreversible — it breaks the replication link. When the original region recovers, you cannot simply 'switch back.' You must re-establish replication in the opposite direction (the former follower is now the leader, the recovered original becomes the new follower), synchronize any events that arrived during the split-brain period (the original may have received events before it fully stopped), and then perform a controlled switchover back to the original region. GridUnion's failback process takes 4–8 hours of careful orchestration and is practiced separately from the failover exercise. The lesson: include failback in the DR plan, not just failover.

Streaming workloads consume CUs continuously — unlike batch pipelines that consume CUs during their execution window and release them. The Eventstreams, the KQL Eventhouse, and the follower databases all consume CUs 24/7. GridUnion's streaming infrastructure consumes approximately 35% of the F32 streaming capacity continuously, leaving 65% for query processing and materialized view computation. At €X per month for the F32 capacity, the streaming workload's share is €0.35X — a significant ongoing cost that must be optimized and justified against the business value of real-time monitoring. This lesson covers the cost levers and the optimization strategies for streaming workloads in Fabric.
The Streaming Cost Model

rows: Eventstream ingestion
Proportional to event volume and routing complexity
Reduce event volume at source (filter before Event Hub, compress payloads)
Turbine gateways filter diagnostic events locally; only operational metrics are sent to the Event Hub — 40% volume reduction
KQL hot cache
Proportional to retained data volume × cache duration
Reduce hot cache days for databases that do not need fast access to older data
Correlation database: 3-day hot cache (not 7) — 57% cache cost reduction
Materialized views
Proportional to view complexity × ingestion rate
Simplify views (fewer aggregation columns, coarser time bins)
Changed 1-minute bins to 1-hour bins for the daily summary view — 60x fewer state entries
Follower databases
Proportional to replicated data volume × replication interval
Increase replication interval for non-critical followers (30 min instead of 5 min)
Weather observation follower: 30-min replication (weather data is hourly, not real-time)
KQL queries
Proportional to query frequency × data scanned per query
Use materialized views for repeated queries; reduce dashboard refresh frequency
Dashboard refresh: 30-sec for CRITICAL panels, 5-min for informational panels — 10x query reduction for 60% of panels

visual-recommendation.description: A stacked bar chart showing the streaming CU consumption breakdown by component: Eventstream ingestion, KQL hot cache, materialized views, follower replication, and KQL queries. Each bar segment is annotated with the optimization applied and the resulting reduction (e.g., source-side filtering: -40%, cache reduction: -57%), showing the total before and after optimization.

blocks.content: Streaming vs Batch Cost Analysis

Not every data source justifies real-time streaming. The cost analysis: GridUnion's SCADA telemetry requires 30–60 second latency for turbine safety monitoring — streaming is justified because the alternative (hourly batch processing) creates a 60-minute blind spot during which a bearing failure could escalate to a turbine fire. The grid stability feed requires 10-second resolution for frequency monitoring — streaming is justified. But the weather observation feed (hourly observations from 120 stations) is a candidate for batch processing: the data arrives hourly, the monitoring use case does not require sub-minute latency, and the streaming overhead (Eventstream, KQL ingestion, follower replication) consumes CUs 24/7 for data that changes once per hour. GridUnion evaluated moving the weather feed to batch processing (a simple hourly Copy activity) and estimated a 15% reduction in the F32 streaming capacity's continuous consumption. The trade-off: the correlation query (lesson 3) would use 1-hour-old weather data instead of near-real-time data — acceptable because wind changes develop over minutes to hours, not seconds.

blocks.title: The cheapest streaming event is the one you do not stream
blocks.content: GridUnion's turbine gateways originally sent all diagnostic data (200+ telemetry fields per event) to the Event Hub. After the streaming cost analysis, the gateways were reconfigured to send only the 12 operational fields needed for real-time monitoring. The diagnostic fields are logged locally on the gateway and uploaded via a daily batch process to the Bronze Lakehouse for offline analysis. This source-side filtering reduced the streaming event volume by 40% — the single largest cost optimization in the streaming architecture. The lesson generalizes: before optimizing the streaming infrastructure (KQL cache, materialized views, replication), evaluate whether every event that enters the stream actually needs real-time processing. Source-side filtering eliminates unnecessary events before they consume any streaming infrastructure at all.

The previous nine lessons covered enterprise streaming scale with 4-country Event Hub sources, complex Eventstream topologies with fan-in routing and fan-out distribution, multi-stream correlation for compound anomaly detection, exactly-once delivery with the verification framework, KQL at enterprise scale with materialized views and update policies, streaming schema evolution with the hot schema change pattern, state management with size monitoring and capacity planning, streaming disaster recovery with the KQL follower pattern, and streaming cost optimization with source-side filtering and cache tiering. This lesson synthesizes everything into GridUnion's complete streaming architecture.
The Streaming Operations Calendar

Automated KQL query + alerting
Event count comparison report; alerts for >0.1% discrepancy
State size monitoring

Schema drift detection
Every 15 minutes
Automated schema comparison
Alert for new/removed fields in Event Hub payloads
Eventstream health check
Every 5 minutes
Fabric monitoring + alerting
Throughput, lag, and error metrics; failover trigger if health check fails 5 consecutive times
Streaming cost review
Monthly
Platform team
CU consumption breakdown by component; optimization recommendations
DR failover drill
Quarterly (part of tabletop exercise)
Platform + security teams
Failover execution time; data gap measurement; failback procedure verification
