blocks.content: What Comes Next

Module 10 — the final module — brings the entire senior track together. You have designed platform architecture (Module 1), understood optimizer internals (Module 2), engineered partition strategies (Module 3), optimized join architectures (Module 4), governed schemas with contracts (Module 5), diagnosed execution engine behavior (Module 6), optimized storage and I/O (Module 7), secured the multi-tenant environment (Module 8), and built enterprise streaming (this module). Module 10 synthesizes these into the enterprise governance framework that sustains GridUnion Continental over years: data lineage at platform scale, multi-capacity planning, platform SLA governance, disaster recovery, team structures, and the complete governance architecture.

blocks.title: Streaming is the platform’s nervous system — it must never sleep
blocks.content: Batch pipelines run and finish. Streaming never finishes. The SCADA Eventstreams process 2,000 events per second, 172 million per day, 365 days per year. The KQL databases ingest, deduplicate, materialize, and serve queries continuously. The correlation engine evaluates compound anomaly patterns every 5 minutes, around the clock. The follower databases replicate state across regions every 5–30 minutes. The cost of streaming is continuous because the value of streaming is continuous: real-time turbine monitoring prevents equipment damage, grid frequency monitoring prevents blackouts, and the correlation engine detects compound failures before they cascade. A streaming architecture that works 99% of the time and fails 1% — 3.65 days per year — is unacceptable when those 3.65 days may include the day a bearing fails while the monitoring system is down. Enterprise streaming reliability is the senior-level engineering challenge that this module addresses.

chapters.title: Enterprise Governance and Capacity Planning
chapters.description: Establish the organizational and operational frameworks that sustain a multi-team, multi-country Fabric platform over years — data lineage at platform scale, capacity planning for growth, platform SLA governance, disaster recovery, team structures, and the enterprise governance practices that make the platform self-sustaining.

blocks.content: When the German Silver meter table's schema changes, which pipelines, views, semantic models, and reports are affected? When the French Bronze data source delivers late, which Gold tables and Platinum consolidations will be stale? When the compliance team asks 'where did this number on the executive dashboard come from?', can you trace it back through the Platinum aggregation, the Gold stored procedure, the Silver MERGE, and the Bronze ingestion to the original source file? These questions require lineage — a complete graph of data flow from source to consumer, spanning all 200+ tables, 500+ pipelines, and 80+ workspaces in GridUnion's platform. Fabric provides a built-in lineage view at the item level. This lesson extends that view to platform-scale lineage that answers questions across country boundaries, across processing layers, and across team ownership domains.
Fabric's Built-In Lineage

Fabric's lineage view shows the dependency graph for items within a workspace and across workspaces: which Lakehouse feeds which notebook, which notebook feeds which Warehouse, which Warehouse feeds which semantic model, which semantic model feeds which report. The lineage is captured automatically from the data flow — no manual configuration needed. For a single-workspace Silver-to-Gold pipeline, the lineage view is sufficient: you can trace from the Gold table back through the notebook to the Silver Lakehouse. At GridUnion's scale, the lineage view's limitation emerges: it shows item-level dependencies (notebook X reads from Lakehouse Y) but not column-level dependencies (column meter_id in the Gold table came from column meter_id in the Silver table via a MERGE on meter_id). Column-level lineage is essential for schema change impact analysis (Module 5, lesson 8) and for regulatory traceability.
The Lineage Enrichment Pipeline

# GridUnion's lineage enrichment pipeline
# Extends Fabric's item-level lineage with column-level dependencies
# Runs weekly and writes to the lineage_graph table in ws_gu_platform

def build_column_lineage(notebook_path: str, spark) -> list:
    """Parse a notebook's Spark code to extract column-level dependencies."""
    # Read the notebook's code cells
    code = extract_notebook_code(notebook_path)
    
    # Parse column references: which source columns map to which output columns
    # Uses a simplified AST analysis of the DataFrame transformations
    dependencies = []
    for transform in parse_transforms(code):
        for output_col, source_cols in transform.column_mapping.items():
            dependencies.append({
                "source_table": transform.input_table,
                "source_columns": source_cols,
                "target_table": transform.output_table,
                "target_column": output_col,
                "transform_type": transform.operation,  # select, withColumn, agg, join
                "notebook": notebook_path
            })
    return dependencies

# Build the complete lineage graph across all production notebooks
# and write to the lineage_graph Delta table for impact analysis queries

The lineage enrichment pipeline parses every production notebook's code to extract column-level mappings: which source columns are read, how they are transformed (selected, renamed, aggregated, joined), and which output columns they produce. The result is a lineage_graph Delta table with one row per column-level dependency. The table enables precise impact analysis: when the German team proposes renaming meter_id to device_id, a query against the lineage graph identifies every downstream column that derives from meter_id across all notebooks, views, and tables — not just the items that reference the Silver table (item-level) but the specific columns within those items that are affected (column-level). GridUnion's lineage graph contains approximately 4,500 column-level dependencies across 200+ tables and 500+ pipelines.

blocks.title: Lineage is the foundation of governed change
blocks.content: Without lineage, every change is a guess: 'I think these 5 pipelines are affected.' With lineage, every change is a query: 'these 23 pipelines and 8 reports reference this column.' The difference is not precision for its own sake — it is the difference between a schema change that breaks 3 unknown consumers (discovered by incident reports over the following week) and a schema change that notifies all 23 consumers 90 days in advance (per the contract process). GridUnion's investment in the lineage enrichment pipeline (approximately 200 engineering hours to build) has prevented every schema change incident since its deployment — because every change now starts with an automated impact analysis that identifies all affected items before the change request is submitted.

Module 5, lesson 8 introduced the impact analysis pipeline for schema changes. This lesson extends impact analysis to all categories of platform change: schema changes (column additions, renames, type changes), data quality degradations (a source system starts delivering null values for a previously non-null column), pipeline delays (a Silver refresh takes 40 minutes instead of 14, cascading freshness breaches to Gold and Platinum), capacity changes (reducing the F128 to F64 to cut costs — which pipelines will be throttled?), and security changes (revoking a team's access to a workspace — which of their pipelines will fail?). Each change category has a different propagation pattern, and the impact analysis must account for all of them.
Change Propagation Patterns

rows: Schema change
Forward: producer → consumer
Immediate (breaking) or gradual (additive)
Schema registry drift detection (Module 5)
German meter_id rename affects 23 downstream consumers
Data quality degradation
Forward: source → Bronze → Silver → Gold → Platinum
Delayed — propagates with the next pipeline run at each layer
Contract test failures at each layer boundary
French source delivers 15% null regions — detected at Silver contract test, blocks Gold refresh
Pipeline delay
Forward: producer pipeline → all consumer pipelines via dependency graph
Cascading — each downstream pipeline adds its own execution time to the delay
Freshness SLA monitoring (Module 1, lesson 9)
German Silver delay cascades to German Gold, Platinum, and the executive dashboard — total impact: 2-hour stale data
Capacity change
Lateral: all workloads on the affected capacity
Immediate — throttling begins when capacity is reduced
Capacity metrics (Module 1, lesson 8)
Reducing F128 to F64 throttles the quarterly regulatory pipeline below its SLA threshold
Security change
Lateral: all items accessed by the affected principal
Immediate — access revocation takes effect within minutes
Pipeline failure notifications for pipelines using revoked service principals
Revoking sp_de_silver_pipeline's access breaks the German Silver refresh

blocks.content: The Unified Impact Analysis Tool

GridUnion's impact analysis tool combines four data sources to evaluate any proposed change. The lineage graph (lesson 1) provides column-level and item-level dependencies — answering 'what downstream items are affected?' The data product registry (Module 1, lesson 4) provides consumer lists and SLA information — answering 'which teams are affected and what are their SLA deadlines?' The capacity metrics (Module 1, lesson 9) provide resource utilization data — answering 'will the change push any capacity past the 70% headroom threshold?' The security policy repository (Module 8, lesson 5) provides access configurations — answering 'will the change break any pipeline's authentication?' The tool accepts a change description, queries all four sources, and produces a unified impact report that the governance committee reviews.

The impact report follows a standard template: change description, change category, affected items (grouped by country and team), affected SLAs (with the estimated freshness impact), affected capacity (with the projected utilization change), affected security (with the list of principals whose access changes), and the recommended mitigation (notification timeline, consumer actions, rollback plan). The report is generated in 2 minutes by the automated tool and replaces the 2-hour manual investigation that preceded it. GridUnion's governance committee reviews 3–5 impact reports per week — the tool's speed makes governance scalable without becoming a bottleneck.

blocks.title: Run impact analysis before proposing the change, not after
blocks.content: The impact analysis tool is most valuable when run before the change request is submitted — not after. An engineer who discovers that their proposed schema change affects 23 consumers may decide to restructure the change to reduce the impact: adding a new column alongside the old one (additive, zero consumer impact) instead of renaming the existing column (breaking, 23 consumers affected). GridUnion's change request template requires an attached impact report — the engineer cannot submit the request without running the tool. This requirement has changed the change design process: engineers now optimize their changes for minimal impact because the impact is visible before they commit to the approach.

The mid-level track managed 2 capacities (F64 for engineering, F32 for analytics) with weekly metrics reviews and quarterly projections. GridUnion operates 4 capacities: F128 for German and Dutch engineering (the largest data volume), F64 for French and Luxembourg engineering, F64 for cross-country analytics and the Gold Warehouse, and F32 for streaming (Eventstreams, Eventhouse, Real-Time Dashboards). Multi-capacity planning coordinates these 4 pools: balancing workloads across capacities, projecting growth per country, right-sizing SKUs based on actual utilization trends, and justifying the annual capacity budget to finance leadership with per-country cost allocation.
The Capacity Projection Model

The projection model accounts for three factors per capacity: organic growth (data volume and query frequency increasing at a measured monthly rate), planned additions (new data sources, new teams, new pipelines from the business roadmap), and optimization pipeline (committed CU reductions from upcoming engineering work). The model projects peak utilization 12 months forward and identifies the month when each capacity crosses the 70% headroom threshold. GridUnion's current projection: the analytics F64 crosses 70% in month 8 due to 2 new analytics teams being onboarded — the platform team has 8 months to either optimize existing analytics workloads or request an F64 → F128 upgrade in the next budget cycle.
The Annual Capacity Budget

The quarterly projection feeds the annual capacity budget request. The budget request itemizes: current capacity costs per SKU, projected costs with growth (assuming no SKU changes), projected costs with recommended SKU changes (upgrades where the 70% threshold is crossed, downgrades where utilization consistently stays below 30%), and the per-country cost allocation that attributes each capacity's cost to the countries and teams that consume it. GridUnion's budget request is presented to finance leadership with a single-page summary: 4 capacities, total annual cost, cost trend (growing 12% year-over-year due to data volume growth), and the recommended actions (upgrade analytics F64 in month 6, no changes to other capacities). The single-page format ensures that the capacity discussion at the leadership level takes 15 minutes rather than an hour.

blocks.title: Capacity planning is financial planning with technical inputs
blocks.content: The platform team provides the technical inputs: utilization trends, growth projections, optimization estimates, and SKU recommendations. Finance leadership makes the financial decision: approve the budget, defer the upgrade, or request deeper optimization to avoid the cost increase. The capacity planning model bridges these two perspectives by translating CU utilization percentages into euros per month — language that finance understands. GridUnion's platform team learned that presenting 'F64 analytics at 52% peak, crossing 70% in month 8' is less effective than presenting 'analytics capacity will be insufficient in October; the upgrade costs €X/month but avoids €Y/month in throttling-induced productivity loss.' Financial framing drives financial decisions.

The mid-level track defined SLIs and SLOs for individual pipelines: Silver freshness under 4 hours, pipeline success rate above 99%. At GridUnion's scale with 45 data products consumed by 12 teams, SLAs must be formalized, tiered, and governed — not just measured. A data product consumed by the billing system (where stale data causes incorrect invoices) needs a stricter SLA than a product consumed by an experimental ML model (where 24-hour-old data is acceptable). SLA governance defines the tiers, assigns data products to tiers based on business criticality, monitors compliance, and arbitrates conflicts when a producer's SLA breach cascades into a consumer's SLA breach across team boundaries.
SLA Tiers

rows: Platinum
< 2 hours
99.9% (< 8.8 hours downtime/year)
Zero tolerance for null business keys; row count within 5% of expected
Severity-1: on-call engineer responds within 15 minutes
Billing data products, regulatory submission data, real-time turbine monitoring
Gold
< 6 hours
99.5% (< 43.8 hours downtime/year)
< 0.1% null rate for required columns; row count within 10% of expected
Severity-2: investigated within 4 hours during business hours
Operations dashboards, analytics data products, cross-country consolidated views
Silver
< 24 hours
99.0% (< 87.6 hours downtime/year)
< 1% null rate; row count within 20% of expected
Severity-3: investigated within 1 business day
ML feature tables, experimental data products, internal engineering metrics

visual-recommendation.description: A three-tier pyramid diagram showing the SLA tiers: Platinum at the top (8 products, strictest SLAs, Severity-1 response), Gold in the middle (22 products, standard SLAs, Severity-2 response), and Silver at the base (15 products, relaxed SLAs, Severity-3 response). Each tier is annotated with its freshness, availability, and quality thresholds, with example data products listed alongside.

blocks.content: SLA Assignment and Review

Each data product is assigned to an SLA tier based on its consumer impact. The assignment is documented in the data product manifest (Module 1, lesson 4) and reviewed by the governance committee when a data product is certified or when a consumer requests a tier change. Tier assignment is not the producer's unilateral decision — it requires agreement between the producer (who must meet the SLA), the consumers (who depend on the SLA), and the platform team (who must ensure the capacity supports the SLA). GridUnion's current distribution: 8 Platinum-tier products (billing, regulatory, real-time monitoring), 22 Gold-tier products (dashboards, analytics, consolidated views), and 15 Silver-tier products (ML features, experimental outputs, internal metrics). The distribution reflects the platform's maturity: early in the platform's life, most products were Silver-tier; as testing, monitoring, and CI/CD matured, products were promoted to higher tiers.
SLA Arbitration

When a producer's SLA breach cascades to a consumer's SLA breach, the governance committee arbitrates responsibility. The German Silver meter table has a Platinum-tier SLA (< 2 hours freshness). The Platinum consolidated view also has a Platinum-tier SLA. If the German Silver refresh is delayed by a vendor data delivery issue (the Bronze source file arrived 3 hours late), the Silver SLA is breached, which cascades to the Platinum SLA. Who is responsible? GridUnion's arbitration rule: the root cause determines responsibility. The vendor delivery delay is an external dependency — the German engineering team is not responsible for the vendor's failure. The SLA breach is recorded as 'external dependency' in the incident log, the compliance team is notified (for regulatory implications), and the platform team adds the vendor delay to the dependency risk register. If the same vendor delays recur, the governance committee escalates to the vendor relationship manager.

blocks.title: SLAs without consequences are aspirations, not commitments
blocks.content: An SLA that is breached without investigation, without a post-mortem, and without corrective action is not a commitment — it is a target that everyone treats as optional. GridUnion's SLA governance includes consequences: a Platinum-tier SLA breach requires a post-mortem within 48 hours, a corrective action plan within 1 week, and a governance committee review within 2 weeks. A data product that breaches its SLA 3 times in a quarter is reviewed for tier demotion (the product's reliability does not justify Platinum status) or for additional investment (the product is critical but under-resourced). The consequences are not punitive — they are corrective. The goal is to identify systemic issues (capacity constraints, unstable sources, insufficient testing) and address them before they become chronic.

The mid-level track's incident management covered single-team pipeline failures: the Silver notebook fails, the on-call engineer investigates, the pipeline is re-run, the post-mortem is written. At GridUnion's scale, incidents cross team boundaries: the German Silver delay cascades to the Platinum consolidation (owned by the platform team), which delays the executive dashboard (consumed by the analytics team), which is noticed by leadership (who escalates to the CTO). The incident involves 4 teams, 3 countries' data, and leadership visibility. Multi-team incident management requires an incident command structure, coordinated communication, and a cross-team post-mortem process that identifies systemic root causes rather than team-specific blame.
The Incident Command Structure

GridUnion's incident response follows a simplified Incident Command System (ICS). For Severity-1 incidents (Platinum SLA breach, security event, data loss), the platform team lead assumes the Incident Commander (IC) role. The IC coordinates all teams, manages the communication, and makes decisions about response priority. The owning team's on-call engineer is the Technical Lead (TL) — they diagnose and fix the root cause. The platform team's communications lead notifies affected consumers, updates the status page, and manages leadership communication. This three-role structure (IC, TL, Communications) prevents the common enterprise incident anti-pattern where 15 people join a call and nobody knows who is making decisions.
The Blameless Post-Mortem

items: Timeline: a minute-by-minute reconstruction of the incident from detection to resolution. Who noticed what, when, and how? What actions were taken? Where were the delays? The timeline is factual — no opinions, no attributions of intent.

Root cause analysis: the technical root cause (vendor delay, configuration error, capacity shortage) and the systemic root cause (missing monitoring for vendor delivery, no CI/CD gate for configuration changes, capacity projection did not account for seasonal volume). The systemic root cause is more important than the technical one — fixing the technical cause prevents this incident, fixing the systemic cause prevents the category of incidents.

Impact assessment: which data products were affected, which SLAs were breached, which consumers were impacted, and what was the business cost (estimated hours of stale data × impact on decisions made with stale data).

Corrective actions: specific, assignable, time-bounded actions that address the systemic root cause. Each action has an owner, a deadline, and a verification criterion. 'Improve monitoring' is not a corrective action. 'Add a vendor delivery time check to the Bronze pipeline that alerts if the source file has not arrived by 01:30 UTC, assigned to DE Data Engineering, due by March 30, verified by triggering the alert in staging' is a corrective action.

Prevention assessment: could this incident category have been prevented by an existing governance mechanism (contract test, schema drift detection, capacity monitoring) that was not configured correctly? If yes, the corrective action includes fixing the governance mechanism. If no, the corrective action includes adding the governance mechanism.

blocks.content: The post-mortem is presented to the governance committee within 2 weeks of the incident. The committee reviews the corrective actions, assigns priority, and tracks completion. GridUnion's post-mortem archive contains 18 incidents from 2 years of operation. The archive is a learning library: new engineers read the post-mortems during onboarding to understand the platform's failure history, and the platform team reviews the archive quarterly to identify recurring patterns. The most common pattern: external dependency failures (vendor delays, API outages) account for 40% of incidents — leading to GridUnion's investment in dependency monitoring and fallback data strategies.

blocks.title: Blameless does not mean accountable-less
blocks.content: Blameless means the post-mortem does not assign personal blame — 'the engineer made a mistake' is not a root cause because it does not prevent future mistakes. But blameless does not mean no one is accountable. The corrective actions have owners and deadlines. The governance committee tracks completion. A team that consistently produces incidents in the same category is asked to invest in the systemic fix — not punished for the incidents, but accountable for the improvement. The blameless culture enables honest root cause analysis (engineers report the full truth because they are not punished for it), while the accountability structure ensures that root causes are actually fixed.

Module 5's contract tests verify data quality at the individual table level: null rates, row counts, uniqueness, and schema compliance. At platform scale, data quality must be monitored across 200+ tables, correlated across layers (a quality issue in Silver propagates to Gold and Platinum), and reported per-team and per-country for governance visibility. The platform-scale quality system extends individual contract tests into a centralized quality framework: common quality rules applied consistently, a quality dashboard that aggregates results across all data products, anomaly detection that catches gradual quality degradation before it triggers a contract test failure, and quality SLAs embedded in the data product tiers (lesson 4).
The Centralized Quality Framework

GridUnion's quality framework operates at three levels. Level 1 (per-table): the contract tests from Module 5, lesson 4 — schema compatibility, null rates, row counts, uniqueness — run after every pipeline execution. These are owned by the producer team and enforce the data product's quality contract. Level 2 (per-layer): cross-table quality rules that verify consistency within a layer — all Silver tables' dates are within the expected range, all Gold tables' row counts are within 10% of the corresponding Silver table (no excessive data loss during aggregation), and all Platinum tables are fresher than the oldest Gold table they derive from. Level 2 rules are owned by the platform team and run daily as part of the governance pipeline. Level 3 (platform-wide): aggregate quality metrics — the percentage of data products passing all quality tests, the trend of quality test pass rates over time, and the distribution of quality scores across countries and teams.
Quality Anomaly Detection

Contract tests use static thresholds: row count must be between 900K and 1.1M. These thresholds catch sudden failures (row count drops to 0) but miss gradual degradations (row count slowly declining from 1.05M to 950K over 3 months — still within the threshold but trending toward breach). Quality anomaly detection uses statistical baselines: the 30-day rolling average row count is 1.02M with a standard deviation of 15K. A daily count of 960K is 4 standard deviations below the mean — flagged as an anomaly even though it passes the static threshold. GridUnion's anomaly detection runs on 5 key metrics (row count, null rate, distinct count for business keys, average value for numeric measures, and freshness) across all 45 data products. The system catches gradual degradations 2–4 weeks before they would breach static thresholds.
The Quality Dashboard

The platform team's quality dashboard aggregates quality metrics into three views. The executive view shows the platform-wide quality score (percentage of data products with all tests passing — target: 100%), the quality trend over 12 months, and the count of quality anomalies detected in the past week. The team view breaks quality scores down by country and by team — identifying which teams' data products consistently pass quality tests and which have recurring quality issues. The product view shows individual data product quality history: test pass/fail trends, anomaly flags, and SLA compliance over time. The dashboard is reviewed in the weekly governance meeting; quality scores below 95% trigger a focused quality improvement initiative with the affected team.

blocks.title: Quality is a leading indicator of platform health
blocks.content: Pipeline duration, CU consumption, and freshness SLAs are lagging indicators — they tell you the platform's health now. Quality metrics are leading indicators — they predict problems before they manifest as SLA breaches. A gradual increase in null rates for a source column predicts a future contract test failure. A slowly declining row count predicts a future data completeness breach. A drift in average value for a numeric measure predicts a future analytics anomaly. GridUnion's quality anomaly detection has predicted 6 SLA breaches 2–4 weeks in advance, giving the owning teams time to investigate and fix the root cause before the breach occurred. Each prediction prevented a Severity-1 incident — making quality monitoring the highest-value preventive investment on the platform.

Data products, pipelines, and workspaces have lifecycles. A data product created for a regulatory requirement that no longer exists should be decommissioned. A pipeline that served the pre-acquisition NordGrid architecture and was replaced by the GridUnion consolidation should be removed. A workspace created for a proof-of-concept that ended 8 months ago should be archived. Without active deprecation, the platform accumulates dead items — consuming storage (orphaned Delta tables with years of unmaintained data), consuming CUs (scheduled pipelines that run nightly but nobody consumes the output), and confusing engineers (3 versions of 'meter readings' in the catalog, only one of which is current). Deprecation is the lifecycle management practice that keeps the platform lean.
The Deprecation Lifecycle

rows: Active
Indefinite
Data product is maintained, monitored, and certified
Consumers may depend on the product per its contract
Deprecation Notice
Day 0
Owner files a deprecation request with the governance committee; the product is marked DEPRECATED in the registry with a sunset date
Consumers are notified; no immediate action required
Migration Window
90 days
Owner maintains the product (continues refreshing, monitoring, and meeting SLAs) while consumers migrate to the replacement
Consumers must identify their dependency and migrate to the replacement product by the sunset date
Grace Period
30 days after sunset
Product continues running but is no longer monitored or SLA-governed; certification badge removed
Consumers who have not migrated receive weekly reminders and escalation to their team lead
Sunsetting
Day 120
Product is decommissioned: pipelines disabled, data archived to cold storage, workspace items deleted
Any consumer still referencing the product will fail; this is expected and documented
Archive
Indefinite
Archived data retained in cold storage per retention policy; metadata retained in the registry with status SUNSET
Archived data available for ad-hoc queries via cold storage access; no SLA, no freshness guarantee

blocks.content: Orphan Detection

Not all deprecated items go through the formal lifecycle. Some items are abandoned informally — a proof-of-concept workspace never formally deprecated, a pipeline that stopped being scheduled but was never deleted, a Delta table that no pipeline writes to and no consumer reads. GridUnion's orphan detection notebook runs monthly and identifies three categories of orphans: tables with no writes in the past 90 days and no reads in the past 30 days (stale data that nobody uses), pipelines that have not run in 60 days (disabled or abandoned), and workspaces with no active items (all items are orphaned or deprecated). Orphans are reported to the workspace owner with a 30-day cleanup deadline. If the owner confirms the item is no longer needed, it enters the deprecation lifecycle at the Grace Period phase (30 days to sunset). If the owner does not respond, the platform team escalates to the governance committee.

blocks.title: Deprecation is harder than creation — budget accordingly
blocks.content: Creating a data product takes 1 engineer 2 weeks. Deprecating it requires notifying all consumers (who may be in different countries and different time zones), supporting their migration (which requires their engineering capacity, not yours), and waiting for the migration window to elapse (90 days of continued maintenance). GridUnion budgets deprecation effort at 50% of the original creation effort — not because the technical work is 50%, but because the coordination work (consumer notification, migration support, governance review) is substantial. The platform team's quarterly roadmap includes a 'deprecation budget' that reserves 10% of engineering capacity for decommissioning legacy items. Without this budget, deprecation is perpetually deferred in favor of new features — and the platform accumulates dead weight.

GridUnion's platform serves 12 teams organized in a hybrid structure that evolved through the acquisition. Understanding the organizational patterns — and when each is appropriate — is essential for senior engineers who participate in organizational design decisions. The team structure determines how work is distributed, how ownership is assigned, how changes are coordinated, and how the platform evolves. A centralized platform team that builds everything creates a bottleneck. A fully decentralized data mesh where every domain team builds their own infrastructure creates inconsistency. The hybrid model that GridUnion uses balances centralized governance with decentralized execution — but the balance requires deliberate design.
Three Organizational Models

rows: Centralized platform
One team builds and operates the entire platform; domain teams consume data products as self-service
Consistent standards, unified governance, efficient resource utilization
Bottleneck on the platform team; domain teams wait for platform capacity; platform team cannot have deep domain expertise
Small organizations (< 5 teams) or early-stage platforms where consistency matters more than speed
Data mesh
Each domain team owns their data end-to-end: ingestion, transformation, quality, publication as data products
Fast iteration per domain; deep domain expertise in each team; no central bottleneck
Inconsistent standards across domains; duplicated infrastructure effort; governance is a federation problem
Large organizations (> 20 teams) with mature engineering practices and strong cross-team governance
Hybrid (GridUnion)
Central platform team owns infrastructure (capacity, security, governance, shared tooling); domain teams own data products within the platform's framework
Consistent infrastructure with domain-specific execution; platform team governs without bottlenecking
Requires clear boundary between 'platform' and 'domain' responsibilities; boundary disputes are common
Mid-size organizations (5–20 teams) transitioning from centralized to decentralized

visual-recommendation.description: A three-panel comparison showing the three organizational models: (left) centralized platform with a single team and arrows from all domains to the platform team, (center) data mesh with independent domain teams and a thin federation layer, (right) GridUnion's hybrid with a central platform team providing infrastructure and domain teams owning their data products. Each panel shows the communication patterns, the governance flow, and the trade-off annotations (bottleneck vs inconsistency vs boundary disputes).

blocks.content: GridUnion's Hybrid Model

GridUnion's platform team (6 engineers) owns: capacity management, security policy, governance automation, the schema registry, the lineage system, the monitoring infrastructure, shared reference data, and the Platinum consolidation pipeline. The 4 country data engineering teams (4–6 engineers each) own: their country's Bronze-Silver-Gold pipelines, their country's data products, their schema evolution within the contract framework, and their pipeline operations including on-call. The 4 analytics teams own: their reports, dashboards, and exploratory workspaces. The 2 ML teams own: their feature tables and model training pipelines. The compliance team (2 people) owns: regulatory evidence production, audit coordination, and DLP policy management. The boundary is clear: the platform team builds the road; the domain teams drive on it.
Organizational Evolution

GridUnion started as a centralized platform team (NordGrid's original model: one team does everything). The acquisition forced decentralization: 3 new country teams brought their own engineers and their own data expertise. The platform team could not absorb 4 countries' pipeline work — they shifted to the hybrid model where they build the infrastructure and the domain teams build the pipelines. This evolution took 12 months and involved 3 organizational restructurings. The lesson for other organizations: do not start with data mesh. Start centralized, build the governance infrastructure (registry, contracts, monitoring, security), and then decentralize domain ownership while keeping governance centralized. The governance infrastructure is the prerequisite for decentralization — without it, decentralization produces chaos.

blocks.title: Conway’s Law applies: the platform reflects the organization
blocks.content: Conway's Law states that systems reflect the communication structures of the organizations that build them. GridUnion's platform architecture — country-level workspaces with centralized Platinum and governance — reflects its organizational structure: country teams with a central platform team. If GridUnion reorganized from country teams to domain teams (billing, operations, regulatory), the workspace architecture would need to reorganize too — from country-based to domain-based. Senior engineers who participate in organizational design discussions should recognize that the organizational decision is simultaneously a platform architecture decision. Changing one without changing the other creates a mismatch that manifests as governance failures, ownership disputes, and coordination overhead.

Module 9, lesson 8 covered streaming disaster recovery: Eventstream failover, KQL follower promotion, and the 7-step failover playbook. This lesson extends DR to the entire platform: the batch pipelines, the Delta tables, the Gold Warehouse, the semantic models, and the governance infrastructure. When EU North suffers a regional outage, GridUnion loses not just the streaming infrastructure but the German and Dutch data engineering workspaces, the Gold Warehouse, the Platinum consolidation pipeline, and the governance platform workspace. Business continuity planning ensures that the most critical platform functions survive a regional outage with defined RPO and RTO targets.
RPO and RTO by Platform Component

rows: Platinum data products (Platinum tier)
4 hours
4 hours
Cross-region OneLake replication of Platinum Delta tables to EU West; failover Gold Warehouse with cross-region Warehouse replication
Storage: 2x for replicated tables; Compute: standby capacity in EU West
Gold data products (Gold tier)
24 hours
8 hours
Gold tables reconstructable from Silver (which is also replicated); no active replication — reconstructed on recovery
Storage: minimal (Silver replication covers it); Compute: burst capacity for reconstruction
Silver data products
24 hours
12 hours
Silver tables replicated to EU West via scheduled OneLake copy (nightly); Bronze data retained in Event Hub for 24 hours for replay
Storage: 2x for Silver replication; Compute: reconstruction pipeline in EU West
Governance platform (ws_gu_platform)
1 hour
2 hours
Schema registry, ownership registry, lineage graph, and pipeline metrics replicated to EU West via continuous copy
Storage: minimal (governance tables are small); Compute: standby notebook in EU West
Semantic models and reports
Equal to their source tier
Equal to their source tier + 30 minutes (model refresh after data recovery)
No independent DR — models refresh from the recovered Gold/Platinum data
No additional cost — recovery is implicit

visual-recommendation.description: A two-region disaster recovery architecture diagram showing EU North (primary) and EU West (secondary) with the components in each region, replication arrows between them with RPO annotations, and the failover sequence numbered 1-7. Components are color-coded by SLA tier (Platinum=red, Gold=orange, Silver=yellow, governance=blue).

blocks.content: The DR Test Program

GridUnion tests disaster recovery semi-annually with a regional failover drill. The drill simulates an EU North outage by disabling the primary workspaces and executing the failover playbook: activate the standby streaming infrastructure (Module 9, lesson 8), promote the replicated governance workspace, fail over the Platinum replication, and reconstruct the Gold Warehouse from the replicated Silver tables. The drill measures actual RPO (how much data was lost — should match the replication lag) and actual RTO (how long the recovery took — should be within the target). The last drill achieved: Platinum RPO = 2 hours (within the 4-hour target), Platinum RTO = 3.5 hours (within the 4-hour target), governance RPO = 45 minutes (within the 1-hour target). The Gold reconstruction took 6 hours (within the 8-hour target but with limited headroom). Each drill produces a report with the measured RPO/RTO, the gaps identified, and the corrective actions for the next drill.

blocks.title: DR that is not tested is not DR — it is documentation
blocks.content: A disaster recovery plan that exists only as a document (not tested, not practiced, not measured) provides false confidence. GridUnion's first DR drill (6 months after the DR plan was written) revealed 4 critical gaps: the standby streaming Eventstream's configuration was 3 months out of date, the governance workspace replication had silently failed 2 weeks earlier, the Gold reconstruction notebook referenced a workspace name that had been renamed, and the failover playbook assumed a team member who had since left the company. All 4 gaps were fixed after the drill. Without the drill, these gaps would have been discovered during an actual outage — when the pressure is highest and the tolerance for errors is lowest. GridUnion's DR test program exists not to verify that DR works but to discover the ways in which it does not.

Ten modules. One hundred lessons. From platform architecture to enterprise governance. This is the final lesson of the Microsoft Fabric Senior-Level Track. The nine preceding modules designed the platform that 80 engineers across 12 teams in 4 countries operate daily: the workspace namespace (Module 1), the optimizer and plan diagnostics (Module 2), the partition strategies (Module 3), the join architectures (Module 4), the schema governance (Module 5), the execution engine internals (Module 6), the storage and I/O optimization (Module 7), the multi-tenant security (Module 8), the enterprise streaming (Module 9), and the governance and capacity planning (this module's preceding lessons). This lesson synthesizes everything into the enterprise governance framework that sustains GridUnion Continental's Fabric platform over years.
The Governance Architecture

blocks.content:

OPERATED BY: 6-person platform team + governance committee

blocks.caption: The governance framework has three tiers: the upper tier (platform architecture, schema governance, data quality) defines what the platform is and how data is structured. The middle tier (security, operations, streaming) defines how the platform is operated and protected. The foundation (technical depth) provides the engineering knowledge that makes the governance decisions informed. The framework is operated by a 6-person platform team with governance committee oversight, serving 80 engineers across 4 countries.

visual-recommendation.description: A comprehensive three-tier governance framework diagram with all 10 modules mapped to their governance role, the operational cadences annotated (weekly reviews, quarterly audits, semi-annual DR drills, annual roadmap), and the organizational roles (platform team, governance committee, domain teams, compliance team) positioned around the framework showing their responsibilities.

blocks.content: The Senior-Level Promise

At the beginning of this track, the promise was: from production pipelines to platform architecture — designing the systems that other engineers build on. You now have the skills to design a multi-tenant workspace namespace for a multi-country organization, to diagnose optimizer behavior at the rule and cost-model level, to engineer partition strategies for multi-terabyte tables with 40 consumers, to architect join topologies for complex multi-hop pipelines, to govern schemas with registries and contracts across 12 teams, to diagnose execution-level problems using the Spark UI's stage, task, and memory views, to optimize storage and I/O at the Parquet encoding and OneLake caching level, to secure a multi-country platform with defense-in-depth and policy-as-code, to build enterprise streaming with multi-stream correlation and exactly-once delivery, and to establish the governance framework that sustains a platform over years. That is the senior-level promise, fulfilled.

blocks.title: The platform outlives the engineer — governance ensures it thrives
blocks.content: The pipelines you build will run for years after you move to your next role. The governance framework you establish will guide engineers who have not yet been hired. The SLA tiers, the schema contracts, the capacity projections, the security policies, the post-mortem archive, and the DR playbooks are not just operational tools — they are institutional knowledge that persists independent of any individual engineer. A well-governed platform can lose its best engineer and continue operating because the governance encodes their decisions, their reasoning, and their standards into processes and automation. The senior engineer's most lasting contribution is not the fastest pipeline or the cleverest optimization — it is the governance framework that makes the platform self-sustaining. That is the ultimate platform engineering achievement.
